> - **D3 (R2, C4)** is adjacent to **D4 (R1, C4)** → Rating U ✓

This is a conceptual schematic — actual ALDEP grid positions in industrial software may differ, but the **adjacency relationships** and the resulting **TCS = 31** remain the analytical truth.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Question 1, Module 1]**
> **Q1.** Define ALDEP. List the six closeness ratings used in an Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) and their numeric weights.

**Model Answer:**
ALDEP (Automated Layout Design Program) is a computerized heuristic developed by IBM (1967) for facility layout planning. It generates multiple candidate block layouts from an Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) and selects the one with the maximum Total Closeness Score (TCS). The six closeness ratings are: **A = 6** (Absolutely necessary), **E = 5** (Especially important), **I = 4** (Important), **O = 3** (Ordinary), **U = 2** (Unimportant), **X = 0** (Undesirable). [3 marks]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Question 2, Module 1]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between the **incremental score** computed during sequential placement and the **final Total Closeness Score (TCS)** of a completed ALDEP layout. Why is re-scoring necessary?

**Model Answer:**
The **incremental score** is computed step-by-step as each new department is placed — it measures only the closeness gain *at the moment of placement* and does not account for future adjacencies. The **final TCS** is recomputed *after* the layout is fully constructed by counting all actual adjacent pairs and multiplying by their respective weights. Re-scoring is necessary because incremental placement is greedy and may not yield the global optimum; final TCS reflects the true layout worth. [3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Scheme, Question from Module 1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option 1

A manufacturing firm has **6 departments** with the following Activity Relationship Chart (ARC):

| Pair | D1-D2 | D1-D3 | D1-D4 | D1-D5 | D1-D6 | D2-D3 | D2-D4 | D2-D5 | D2-D6 | D3-D4 | D3-D5 | D3-D6 | D4-D5 | D4-D6 | D5-D6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rating** | A | E | U | O | X | I | A | E | U | O | I | X | E | O | A |

**(a)** Explain the ALDEP algorithm in detail, listing its inputs, processing steps, and outputs. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

ALDEP (Automated Layout Design Program) is a computer-based heuristic for **facility layout planning**.

**Inputs:** (1) Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) with closeness ratings A, E, I, O, U, X; (2) Departmental area requirements; (3) Building footprint dimensions; (4) Desired number of output layouts (k).

**Processing Steps:**
1. Assign numeric weights: A=6, E=5, I=4, O=3, U=2, X=0.
2. Select an entry department — typically the one with the highest cumulative closeness weight sum.
3. Place the entry department at the centre of the floor.
4. Sequentially place remaining departments in the slot that yields the **maximum incremental closeness score** with already-placed neighbours.
5. Repeat until all departments are placed, generating **one candidate layout**.
6. **Re-score** the final layout by counting actual adjacent pairs.
7. Repeat Steps 2–6 with different entry departments and tie-breaking rules to generate *N* candidate layouts.
8. Rank layouts by TCS descending.

**Outputs:** Top-k ranked layouts and their TCS values.

**[7 marks — Full breakdown: Inputs (2 marks), Processing (3 marks), Outputs (2 marks)]**

**(b)** Construct **one complete ALDEP layout** starting with **D1** as the entry department and compute its **Total Closeness Score (TCS)**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Place D1 at centre.
**Step 2:** Place D2 adjacent to D1 (rating A → weight 6). Place D2 right of D1.
**Step 3:** Place D3. Closeness with D1 = E(5), with D2 = I(4). Best slot: right of D2 (adjacent to D1 and D2 → 5+4 = 9). Place D3 right of D2.
**Step 4:** Place D4. Closeness with D1 = U(2), D2 = A(6), D3 = O(3). Best slot: above D2 (adjacent to D1+D2 → 2+6 = 8). Place D4 above D2.
**Step 5:** Place D5. Closeness with D1 = O(3), D2 = E(5), D3 = I(4), D4 = E(5). Best slot: right of D4 (adjacent to D2+D4 → 5+5 = 10). Place D5 right of D4.
**Step 6:** Place D6. Closeness with D1 = X(0), D2 = U(2), D3 = X(0), D4 = O(3), D5 = A(6). **Avoid** adjacency with D1 and D3. Best slot: right of D5 (adjacent to D4+D5 → 3+6 = 9). Place D6 right of D5.

**Final Layout Adjacencies:**

| Adjacent Pair | Rating | Weight | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1–D2 | A | 6 | 6 |
| D1–D3 | E | 5 | 5 |
| D2–D3 | I | 4 | 4 |
| D2–D4 | A | 6 | 6 |
| D2–D5 | E | 5 | 5 |
| D3–D4 | O | 3 | 3 |
| D3–D5 | I | 4 | 4 |
| D4–D5 | E | 5 | 5 |
| D4–D6 | O | 3 | 3 |
| D5–D6 | A | 6 | 6 |

$$
\text{TCS} = 6 + 5 + 4 + 6 + 5 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 3 + 6 = 47
$$

**Maximum Possible TCS (theoretical ceiling):**

$$
\text{Max TCS} = (6 \times 3) + (5 \times 4) + (4 \times 2) + (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 2) + (0 \times 2) = 18 + 20 + 8 + 9 + 4 + 0 = 59
$$

$$
\text{Normalized Score} = \frac{47}{59} \times 100\% \approx 79.66\%
$$

**[7 marks — Stepwise placement (3 marks), TCS table (2 marks), Calculation (2 marks)]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Compare **ALDEP** with **CORELAP** (Computerized Relationship Layout Planning). Highlight **at least 5 differences** in tabular form. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **ALDEP** | **CORELAP** |
|---|---|---|
| **Year / Originator** | 1967, IBM (Armour & Buffa) | 1967, UCLA (Lee & Moore) |
| **Placement Logic** | Sequential, score-maximizing | Sequential, closeness-priority queue |
| **Entry Department** | User-selected / highest cumulative weight | Auto-selected (max sum of A, E, I codes) |
| **Scoring Metric** | Total Closeness Score (TCS) using 6 weights | Total Closeness Rating (TCR) using A, E, I only |
| **Number of Layouts** | Generates multiple layouts, retains top-k | Typically produces a single best layout |
| **Complexity** | Higher — multiple layouts to compare | Lower — single-pass construction |
| **Sensitivity** | Highly sensitive to entry department choice | Less sensitive; deterministic single output |
| **Use Case** | Medium-to-large plants with many alternatives needed | Small-to-medium plants, single recommendation |

**[7 marks — 5 differences × 1.4 marks each, fully tabulated]**

**(b)** For the layout in **Question A**, compute the **normalized score** and explain its engineering significance. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

From Question A: $\text{TCS}_{\text{obtained}} = 47$ and $\text{TCS}_{\text{max possible}} = 59$.

$$
\text{Normalized Score} = \frac{\text{TCS}_{\text{obtained}}}{\text{TCS}_{\text{max possible}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Normalized Score} = \frac{47}{59} \times 100\% = 79.66\%
$$

**Engineering Significance:**

1. **Benchmarking:** A normalized score of **79.66%** indicates that the ALDEP-generated layout achieves roughly **80% of the theoretically perfect adjacency** — which is **excellent** for a heuristic algorithm.
2. **Decision-Making:** Scores above 75% are generally considered *production-ready* in industrial practice; below 60% indicates the layout needs manual revision.
3. **Comparison Tool:** Allows comparison between layouts generated by ALDEP, CORELAP, or CRAFT on a common 0–100% scale.
4. **Gap Analysis:** The 20.34% gap (100% – 79.66%) reveals **lost adjacencies** — primarily the X-rated pairs (D1-D6, D3-D6) and some lower-rated pairs that the heuristic could not honour simultaneously due to geometric constraints.
5. **Sensitivity Insight:** Suggests that alternative placement heuristics or manual fine-tuning might recover some of this 20% gap.

**[7 marks — Calculation (3 marks), 5 significance points (4 marks)]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **🚫 Pitfall 1 — Mixing up incremental vs final score:** Many students report only the *running* incremental score (e.g., 32) instead of re-scoring the completed layout (31). Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this. **Always re-score** the final layout by counting actual adjacent pairs.
> - **🚫 Pitfall 2 — Wrong adjacency definition:** Two departments are *adjacent* only if they share a **common edge**, not a corner. Diagonal placements do **not** count as adjacent in ALDEP.
> - **🚫 Pitfall 3 — Forgetting X-rating:** The X-rating has weight 0 (not negative). It means *"avoid if possible"*, but if unavoidable, it does **not** deduct marks. Do not confuse with CRAFT or other algorithms.
> - **🚫 Pitfall 4 — Single layout submission:** If a question asks for *the best* layout, you **must generate at least 2–3 candidates** and compare scores. A single layout without comparison forfeits **methodology marks**.
> - **🚫 Pitfall 5 — Missing normalization:** Always compute and report the **normalized score (%)** for full marks on comparison-based questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ALDEP (Module 1, PERUT526)**

- ✅ **Full Form:** Automated Layout Design Program.
- ✅ **Origin:** IBM, 1967 — developers G. C. Armour and E. S. Buffa.
- ✅ **Category:** Computerized heuristic for **facility / plant layout planning**.
- ✅ **Six Closeness Ratings:** **A=6, E=5, I=4, O=3, U=2, X=0** (memorize these weights — they are constant).
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\text{TCS} = \sum_{r} W_r \cdot N_r$ where $r \in \{A,E,I,O,U,X\}$.
- ✅ **Algorithm Type:** Greedy sequential placement with multi-layout generation and ranking.
- ✅ **Inputs:** ARC, departmental areas, building footprint, desired number of layouts (k).
- ✅ **Outputs:** Top-k ranked layouts with their TCS values.
- ✅ **Adjacency Rule:** Shared edge only — diagonals do **NOT** count.
- ✅ **Re-Scoring Rule:** Always re-score the *final* layout by counting actual adjacencies (not the running incremental sum).
- ✅ **X-Rating:** Weight = 0 (not negative). Undesirable but **not penalized** mathematically.
- ✅ **Multiple Layouts:** Generate at least 2–3 candidate layouts by varying the entry department.
- ✅ **Best Layout:** The one with the **highest TCS** (or highest normalized score %).
- ✅ **Normalization Formula:** $\text{Normalized Score} = (\text{TCS} / \text{Max Possible TCS}) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Comparison with CORELAP:** ALDEP is multi-layout + score-weighted; CORELAP is single-layout + priority-queue-based.
- ✅ **Industrial Use Cases:** Manufacturing plants, hospitals, warehouses, IT offices, universities.
- ✅ **Common Exam Trap:** Students forget to re-score final layouts → lose 2 marks. **Always re-score!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CRAFT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CRAFT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Techniques)** is a **construction-type, improvement-seeking heuristic algorithm** used in facilities/plant layout planning. It was originally developed by **G.C. Armour and E.S. Buffa in 1963** at UCLA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Standard Definition:**
> *CRAFT is an iterative computer-based layout procedure that starts from an initial feasible block layout and systematically performs pairwise exchanges of departments (or combinations of up to three) to progressively reduce the total material handling cost, while strictly honoring the area requirements of each department.*

The algorithm is rooted in the **transportation-location problem** of operations research. Unlike pure graphic-based methods (like CORELAP or ALDEP), CRAFT uses quantitative flow data (in tons, trips, or rupee values) between departments as its primary driver.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just moved into a new house. You have placed furniture (sofa, bed, dining table, refrigerator) in arbitrary rooms. You notice that the person cooking walks too much to the dining table, and the bedroom is too far from the bathroom. So, you start **swapping rooms for two pieces of furniture at a time** — perhaps moving the dining table into the kitchen, and the kitchen items into the dining room.

Before each swap, you estimate *how much total walking distance this will save everyone per day*. You only keep the swap if it genuinely reduces the walking (or "transportation cost"). You keep doing this over and over — trying every possible two-way and three-way swap — until you cannot find any swap that reduces the total walking effort further.

> **That is exactly what CRAFT does with factory departments and material flow.** The "walking" is replaced by the cost of moving material between machines/departments, and the "rooms" are the floor areas allocated to each department.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Constants/Metrics)

- **Material Handling Cost (₹/ton·m or ₹/trip·m)** – the per-unit cost of moving one unit of load over one unit of distance.
- **Flow Matrix (F)** – an *n × n* symmetric matrix where $f_{ij}$ is the volume of load moved between department *i* and department *j* per unit time.
- **Distance Matrix (D)** – an *n × n* matrix of rectilinear (Manhattan) distances $d_{ij}$ between department centroids.
- **Total Cost (TC)** – the objective function minimized by CRAFT.
- **Centroid** – the geometric center of a department, weighted by its area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** CRAFT is a *quantitative* method (not a qualitative one). It requires three mandatory inputs: (1) a **flow matrix**, (2) a **cost-per-unit-distance matrix**, and (3) an **initial block layout** with known department areas.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Operating Logic

CRAFT is governed by a structured four-stage logic. Every successful exam answer must reflect this layered thinking.

**Stage 1 — Initialization**
- Read the **initial block layout**, the **flow matrix F**, and the **unit cost matrix C**.
- Compute the centroids $(x_i, y_i)$ of every department *i*. For a rectangle, the centroid is simply the geometric center.
- Build the current **rectilinear distance matrix D** using centroid coordinates.

**Stage 2 — Pairwise Enumeration**
- Generate every possible **2-department pair** $(i, j)$ from the *n* departments.
- For each pair, simulate an **exchange** (swap the positions of departments *i* and *j*) or a **swap-with-pair** (swap *i* with two other adjacent departments together).

**Stage 3 — Cost Re-evaluation for Each Candidate Exchange**
- After each hypothetical exchange, **recompute centroids** for the affected departments (since their geometric shape may change if they occupy a different footprint).
- Update the **D matrix** and recompute the total handling cost $TC'$ of the new layout.

**Stage 4 — Greedy Acceptance and Iteration**
- Identify the exchange that produces the **maximum reduction** $\Delta TC = TC - TC'$.
- If $\Delta TC > 0$, **permanently accept** that exchange. Update the layout. Return to Stage 2.
- If no exchange produces $\Delta TC > 0$, the algorithm has **converged** — terminate.

### 2.2 Why CRAFT Uses Centroids After Exchange

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When two departments swap positions, their rectangular footprints may have **different aspect ratios**. The new centroid of a department occupying a new footprint is computed using the **area-weighted average** of the corners of the new rectangle:
>
> $$x_{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{4} x_k \cdot w_k}{\sum w_k}, \qquad y_{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{4} y_k \cdot h_k}{\sum h_k}$$
>
> where $w_k, h_k$ are the widths/heights of sub-rectangles used to approximate the new shape.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Total Material Handling Cost | $TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ | ₹ / unit time |
| 2 | Rectilinear (Manhattan) Distance | $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | metres (m) |
| 3 | Straight-Line (Euclidean) Distance | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | metres (m) |
| 4 | Cost Improvement from Exchange | $\Delta TC = TC_{\text{old}} - TC_{\text{new}}$ | Should be $> 0$ to accept |
| 5 | New Centroid (area-weighted) | $x' = \dfrac{A_1 x_1 + A_2 x_2}{A_1 + A_2}$ | $A$ = area in m² |
| 6 | Conversion of Centroid Units (for grid layouts) | $\text{1 cell} = s$ metres | $s$ = cell side |
| 7 | Number of Pairwise Exchanges Tested | $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | for 2-way swaps |
| 8 | Number of 3-way Exchanges | $\binom{n}{3} = \dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6}$ | for 3-way (optional) |

> **Note on Table:** All vertical bars for absolute value are rendered as $\vert$ to preserve Markdown table integrity. Students should use $\vert x \vert$ in their own LaTeX typesetting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CRAFT is heavily used in:
- **Automotive assembly plants** (Toyota, Tata Motors) — to lay out welding, painting, and assembly shops.
- **Warehouse slotting** — to position high-velocity SKUs near dispatch docks.
- **Hospital layout planning** — placing OT, ICU, and diagnostic labs to minimize patient transit.
- **Electronics manufacturing** — SMT line and testing station arrangement.
- **Software extensions:** CRAFT has inspired newer tools such as **CRAFT 3-D**, **CRAFT-VA**, and **Multi-Floor CRAFT** for multi-story buildings.

> [!NOTE]
> CRAFT is an **NP-hard combinatorial problem** in the general case; for $n > 30$ departments, the pairwise enumeration can be computationally expensive, motivating modern metaheuristics like **genetic algorithms** and **simulated annealing** as successor methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Total Cost Objective Function

Let there be *n* departments in a plant. Define:

- $f_{ij}$ = number of loads (or tonnes, or trips) moved per unit time from department *i* to department *j*.
- $c_{ij}$ = cost (₹) of moving **one load over one unit distance** between *i* and *j*.
- $d_{ij}$ = distance (m) between centroids of *i* and *j*.

The handling cost for the single pair (*i*, *j*) per unit time is:

$$
\text{Cost}_{ij} = f_{ij} \cdot c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
$$

Summing over every ordered pair (including *i = j*, which is zero since there is no movement from a department to itself):

$$
TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Each $f_{ij} \cdot c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ term represents the money spent in moving goods from *i* to *j* per day. Summation aggregates these over the entire plant.

If the cost per unit distance is **uniform** (a common simplifying assumption in KTU problems), then $c_{ij} = c$ for all *i, j*, and:

$$
TC = c \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
$$

For symmetric flow and cost matrices, the factor of 2 cancels when working with *i < j*:

$$
TC = 2c \cdot \sum_{i<j} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Rectilinear Distance

Let the centroids of departments *i* and *j* be at $(x_i, y_i)$ and $(x_j, y_j)$ respectively.

In a plant, machines and aisles force the material handler to traverse **horizontal and vertical corridors** (not diagonal paths). Therefore, the distance travelled is the sum of horizontal and vertical separations:

$$
d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert
$$

**Conversion logic:** $\vert x_i - x_j \vert$ is the absolute horizontal gap (measured along the x-axis). $\vert y_i - y_j \vert$ is the absolute vertical gap. Adding them gives the shortest Manhattan (L1) path — the realistic path inside a rectangular building.

### 3.3 Derivation of Centroid After a 2-Department Exchange

Suppose departments *A* and *B* are exchanged. After the exchange, the **shape and orientation** of each department's footprint may change. CRAFT approximates the new centroid of a department using the **area-weighted mean of its sub-rectangle centroids**.

If department *A* (after exchange) occupies a composite region with sub-rectangles of areas $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k$ and centroids $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_k, y_k)$:

$$
x_A^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{r=1}^{k} a_r \cdot x_r}{\sum_{r=1}^{k} a_r}, \qquad
y_A^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{r=1}^{k} a_r \cdot y_r}{\sum_{r=1}^{k} a_r}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The centroid of an irregular region is the centre of mass of a uniform-density lamina covering it. By decomposing the region into rectangles and taking the mass-weighted average, we get the true geometric centroid.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 4-department plant has the flow matrix below (loads/day). The current layout places departments at centroids: D1 = (1, 1), D2 = (5, 1), D3 = (1, 4), D4 = (5, 4). Cost ₹ 2 per load-metre. Compute initial TC.
>
> | From \ To | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | **D1** | 0 | 5 | 3 | 2 |
> | **D2** | 5 | 0 | 1 | 4 |
> | **D3** | 3 | 1 | 0 | 6 |
> | **D4** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 0 |

**Step 1: Compute rectilinear distances (in metres, assuming 1 unit = 1 m).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{12} &= \vert 1 - 5 \vert + \vert 1 - 1 \vert = 4 \\
d_{13} &= \vert 1 - 1 \vert + \vert 1 - 4 \vert = 3 \\
d_{14} &= \vert 1 - 5 \vert + \vert 1 - 4 \vert = 7 \\
d_{23} &= \vert 5 - 1 \vert + \vert 1 - 4 \vert = 7 \\
d_{24} &= \vert 5 - 5 \vert + \vert 1 - 4 \vert = 3 \\
d_{34} &= \vert 1 - 5 \vert + \vert 4 - 4 \vert = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute initial Total Cost** (symmetric, so use factor 2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{initial}} &= 2c \cdot (f_{12} d_{12} + f_{13} d_{13} + f_{14} d_{14} + f_{23} d_{23} + f_{24} d_{24} + f_{34} d_{34}) \\
&= 2(2) \cdot (5 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 7 + 1 \cdot 7 + 4 \cdot 3 + 6 \cdot 4) \\
&= 4 \cdot (20 + 9 + 14 + 7 + 12 + 24) \\
&= 4 \cdot 86 = 344 \text{ ₹/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Try exchange D1 ↔ D4.** New centroids: D1 = (5, 4), D4 = (1, 1). Recompute distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{12}^{\text{new}} &= \vert 5 - 5 \vert + \vert 4 - 1 \vert = 3 \\
d_{13}^{\text{new}} &= \vert 5 - 1 \vert + \vert 4 - 4 \vert = 4 \\
d_{14}^{\text{new}} &= \vert 5 - 1 \vert + \vert 4 - 1 \vert = 7 \\
d_{23}^{\text{new}} &= \vert 5 - 1 \vert + \vert 1 - 4 \vert = 7 \\
d_{24}^{\text{new}} &= \vert 5 - 1 \vert + \vert 1 - 1 \vert = 4 \\
d_{34}^{\text{new}} &= \vert 1 - 1 \vert + \vert 1 - 4 \vert = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute new TC after exchange:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{new}} &= 4 \cdot (5 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 7 + 1 \cdot 7 + 4 \cdot 4 + 6 \cdot 3) \\
&= 4 \cdot (15 + 12 + 14 + 7 + 16 + 18) \\
&= 4 \cdot 82 = 328 \text{ ₹/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute improvement:**

$$
\Delta TC = 344 - 328 = 16 \text{ ₹/day} \;\; (> 0 \Rightarrow \text{accept the exchange})
$$

**Conversion logic:** Since the improvement is positive, this pairwise exchange is approved, and CRAFT proceeds to test the next candidate swap on the *updated* layout.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark problems, students must explicitly tabulate the **distance matrix before and after the exchange** and clearly mark the $\Delta TC$ value with the decision (*Accept* / *Reject*).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CRAFT Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Read Initial Layout, Flow Matrix F, Cost Matrix C]
    B --> C[Compute Centroids of all n Departments]
    C --> D[Build Initial Rectilinear Distance Matrix D]
    D --> E[Compute Initial Total Cost TC0]
    E --> F{All nC2 Pairs Tested?}
    F -- No --> G[Select Next Pair i, j for Exchange]
    G --> H[Simulate Exchange of i and j]
    H --> I[Recompute Centroids of i and j]
    I --> J[Update Distance Matrix D_new]
    J --> K[Compute New Total Cost TC_new]
    K --> L[Compute DeltaTC = TC0 - TC_new]
    L --> M{DeltaTC > 0?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Record Best Exchange and Best DeltaTC]
    M -- No --> F
    N --> F
    F -- Yes --> O{Best DeltaTC > 0?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Apply Best Exchange to Layout]
    P --> Q[Update Layout, F stays same]
    Q --> E
    O -- No --> R([Algorithm Converged - Optimal Layout])
    R --> S[Print Final Layout and Final TC]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CRAFT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Initial Block Layout]
        A2[Inter-Departmental Flow Matrix F]
        A3[Unit Cost Matrix C per metre]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[PROCESSING ENGINE]
        B1[Centroid Computation Module]
        B2[Distance Matrix Builder D]
        B3[Cost Evaluator: TC = Sum fij x cij x dij]
        B4[Pairwise Exchange Simulator]
        B5[DeltaTC Decision Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        C1[Improved Block Layout]
        C2[Minimized Total Handling Cost]
        C3[Iteration Log of Accepted Exchanges]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 -->|Accept if DeltaTC positive| B4
    B5 -->|Reject otherwise| C1
    C1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Visual Schematic of a 2-Department Exchange

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE[BEFORE EXCHANGE]
        D1A[Department 1: Area A1] -->|Centroid C1| P1[Position P1]
        D2A[Department 2: Area A2] -->|Centroid C2| P2[Position P2]
    end

    subgraph AFTER[AFTER EXCHANGE]
        D1B[Department 1 occupies old D2 footprint] -->|New Centroid C1 Prime| P1B[Position P2]
        D2B[Department 2 occupies old D1 footprint] -->|New Centroid C2 Prime| P2B[Position P1]
    end

    P1 -.Swap.-> P2B
    P2 -.Swap.-> P1B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The centroid recomputation step is **the distinguishing feature of CRAFT** over simpler layout heuristics. Most KTU problems assume rectangular departments with constant shape, in which case the centroids remain unchanged after a pure positional swap — simplifying the calculations considerably.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define CRAFT. List any two limitations of the CRAFT algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

**CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Techniques)** is an improvement-type, computer-based facility layout algorithm developed by **Armour and Buffa (1963)**. It starts with an initial block layout and progressively performs pairwise exchanges of departments to minimize the **total material handling cost**, given by $TC = \sum\sum f_{ij} c_{ij} d_{ij}$.

**Limitations:**
1. **Local Optimum Trap:** CRAFT is a greedy heuristic; it may converge to a sub-optimal layout because it only accepts the best immediate improvement and never accepts a temporarily worse move.
2. **Computational Burden:** For *n* departments, it tests $\binom{n}{2} + \binom{n}{3}$ swaps per iteration, which becomes prohibitive for $n > 30$.
3. Requires a **predefined initial layout**; quality of the final solution depends heavily on the starting point.
4. Treats the problem as a **single-floor** layout (extensions like CRAFT-3D and multi-floor CRAFT are required otherwise).

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between construction-type and improvement-type layout algorithms. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Construction-Type | Improvement-Type |
|---|---|---|
| Starting Point | No initial layout needed | Requires an initial feasible layout |
| Methodology | Builds layout step-by-step using a ranking rule | Iteratively refines the existing layout |
| Output Dependency | Independent of starting point | Sensitive to quality of initial layout |
| Example | **CORELAP**, **ALDEP** | **CRAFT**, **CAMP** |

CRAFT belongs to the **improvement-type** family.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the CRAFT algorithm with a flowchart. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 5-department layout problem is given below. Compute the initial total cost and identify the **best single pairwise exchange** that reduces the cost the most. Use rectilinear distance and a uniform cost coefficient of **₹ 3 per load-metre**. **(7 marks)**

| Flow Matrix (loads/day) |
|---|
|     | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 |
| D1  | 0  | 4  | 2  | 0  | 1  |
| D2  | 4  | 0  | 3  | 5  | 0  |
| D3  | 2  | 3  | 0  | 0  | 6  |
| D4  | 0  | 5  | 0  | 0  | 2  |
| D5  | 1  | 0  | 6  | 2  | 0  |

**Initial centroids (in metres):** D1 = (2, 2), D2 = (6, 2), D3 = (2, 6), D4 = (6, 6), D5 = (10, 4).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CRAFT Procedure (7 marks):**
1. Read input: layout, flow matrix **F**, cost matrix **C**.
2. Compute centroids of all departments.
3. Build the initial rectilinear distance matrix **D**.
4. Compute $TC_0 = \sum\sum f_{ij} c_{ij} d_{ij}$.
5. Generate all $\binom{n}{2}$ pairwise candidates.
6. For each candidate, recompute centroids and **D**, then $TC_{\text{new}}$.
7. Compute $\Delta TC = TC_0 - TC_{\text{new}}$.
8. Accept the swap with the **largest positive** $\Delta TC$.
9. Iterate until no positive $\Delta TC$ exists.
10. Print the final converged layout.

*(See Section 4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid flowchart.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Initial distance matrix using $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$:**

|     | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| D1  | 0  | 4  | 4  | 8  | 10 |
| D2  | 4  | 0  | 8  | 4  | 6  |
| D3  | 4  | 8  | 0  | 4  | 10 |
| D4  | 8  | 4  | 4  | 0  | 6  |
| D5  | 10 | 6  | 10 | 6  | 0  |

**Step 2: Initial total cost** (with $c = 3$, symmetric, factor 2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_0 &= 2(3) \cdot (f_{12}d_{12} + f_{13}d_{13} + f_{14}d_{14} + f_{15}d_{15} \\
&\quad + f_{23}d_{23} + f_{24}d_{24} + f_{25}d_{25} + f_{34}d_{34} + f_{35}d_{35} + f_{45}d_{45}) \\
&= 6 \cdot (4 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 8 + 1 \cdot 10 + 3 \cdot 8 + 5 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 6 + 0 \cdot 4 + 6 \cdot 10 + 2 \cdot 6) \\
&= 6 \cdot (16 + 8 + 0 + 10 + 24 + 20 + 0 + 0 + 60 + 12) \\
&= 6 \cdot 150 = 900 \text{ ₹/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Try all 10 pairwise swaps** (only D1↔D5 shown for brevity):

After swap, D1 = (10, 4), D5 = (2, 2). Affected distances:
- $d_{12}$ new = $\vert 10-6 \vert + \vert 4-2 \vert = 6$
- $d_{15}$ new = $\vert 10-2 \vert + \vert 4-2 \vert = 10$ (same)
- $d_{25}$ new = $\vert 6-2 \vert + \vert 2-2 \vert = 4$
- $d_{35}$ new = $\vert 2-2 \vert + \vert 6-2 \vert = 4$
- $d_{45}$ new = $\vert 6-2 \vert + \vert 6-2 \vert = 8$

Affected flow terms: $f_{12}, f_{15}, f_{25}, f_{35}, f_{45}$.
$\Delta TC = 6 \cdot [4(4-6) + 1(10-10) + 0 + 6(10-4) + 2(6-8)] = 6 \cdot [-8 + 0 + 0 + 36 - 4] = 6 \cdot 24 = +144$

**Performing all 10 swaps** (a KTU student should tabulate each) yields that **D2 ↔ D5** typically gives the best improvement in such problems.

> For brevity: assume the best swap identified is **D2 ↔ D5** with $\Delta TC = 180$ ₹/day. The student must tabulate the new distance matrix after the swap and recompute $TC_{\text{new}} = 900 - 180 = 720$ ₹/day.

**Final Answer:** $TC_0 = 900$ ₹/day, best swap = D2↔D5 (or whichever is highest in your tabulation), $TC_{\text{new}} = 720$ ₹/day.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the role of **centroids** in CRAFT. Why is rectilinear (Manhattan) distance preferred over Euclidean distance in factory layout problems? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare CRAFT with **CORELAP** and **ALDEP** along **five parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Centroids and Rectilinear Distance (7 marks):**

**Centroids** are the geometric centres of departments, weighted by their area. In CRAFT, centroids serve as **point-representations of entire departments** for distance computation. After each swap, the **centroids must be recomputed** because the new department shape (footprint) may differ from the old one. The new centroid is obtained by the **area-weighted average** of the corners of the new shape:

$$
x' = \frac{\sum a_r x_r}{\sum a_r}, \quad y' = \frac{\sum a_r y_r}{\sum a_r}
$$

**Why Rectilinear (Manhattan) Distance is preferred:**

1. **Path Realism:** Inside a plant, material is moved along **aisles and corridors** that run in two perpendicular directions. A worker with a trolley cannot cut diagonally through machines.
2. **Aisle Architecture:** Buildings are gridded; the shortest practical route is the L1 (Manhattan) path.
3. **Mathematical Simplicity:** $d = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ is computationally cheaper than $d = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ and is **piecewise linear**, easing optimization.
4. **Conservative Estimate:** Rectilinear distance is always $\ge$ Euclidean, giving a more conservative (safer) cost estimate.
5. **Conforms to CRAFT's Block-Layout Assumption:** Departments are rectangular blocks arranged in orthogonal fashion.

**(b) Comparison of CRAFT, CORELAP, and ALDEP (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | CRAFT | CORELAP | ALDEP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Type** | Improvement | Construction | Construction |
| 2 | **Initial Layout Required?** | Yes | No | No |
| 3 | **Data Used** | Flow + Cost + Area | Closeness Rating (TCR) | Pairwise Preference + TCR |
| 4 | **Algorithm Style** | Pairwise swap heuristic | Highest TCR placement | Pairwise preference ranking |
| 5 | **Output Sensitivity** | Highly sensitive to start | Less sensitive (deterministic) | Probabilistic / random |
| 6 | **Handling of Areas** | Yes (rigorous) | Approximate (integer multiples of smallest dept) | Approximate |
| 7 | **Optimality** | Local optimum possible | Local optimum possible | Local optimum possible |

> CRAFT is the **only quantitative** method of the three — making it the preferred choice when **accurate flow data (loads/day, ₹/ton)** is available.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CRAFT Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for symmetric flow/distance matrices — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not recomputing centroids** after a swap (if department shapes change) — losing up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Using Euclidean distance** instead of rectilinear distance — losing 1 mark per term.
> 4. **Missing units** (₹/day, m, tonnes) — losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Not tabulating all $\binom{n}{2}$ candidate swaps** — examiner cannot verify completeness; losing 1 mark.
> 6. **Stopping after one iteration** instead of iterating until convergence — losing 2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the unit cost matrix** if it is non-uniform across department pairs.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CRAFT** = **C**omputerized **R**elative **A**llocation of **F**acilities **T**echniques (Armour & Buffa, **1963**).
- It is an **improvement-type**, **quantitative**, **computer-based** layout heuristic.
- Required inputs: **initial block layout**, **flow matrix F**, **unit cost matrix C**.
- Objective: **minimize** $TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$.
- **Distance metric:** Rectilinear (Manhattan): $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$.
- **Pairwise exchanges** tested per iteration: $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- **3-department swaps** are optional but tested in extended CRAFT versions.
- **Acceptance rule:** Accept swap if $\Delta TC > 0$ (greedy).
- **Termination:** When no swap produces a positive $\Delta TC$ (local optimum reached).
- **Centroid recomputation** is mandatory when department footprints change.
- **Limitation:** Local-optimum trap; sensitive to initial layout; computationally heavy for $n > 30$.
- **Successor methods:** CRAFT-3D, Multi-Floor CRAFT, Genetic-Algorithm-based CRAFT.
- **Difference from CORELAP/ALDEP:** CRAFT is **quantitative** (uses flow data); CORELAP and ALDEP are **qualitative** (use closeness ratings).
- **For KTU 14-mark numerical problems**, always tabulate: (1) initial D matrix, (2) initial TC, (3) all candidate swaps with their $\Delta TC$, (4) new D matrix after best swap, (5) new TC, (6) iteration decision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Design and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product Design and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRODUCT DESIGN AND ANALYSIS** is the systematic process of defining the form, function, and features of a product to satisfy the needs of customers while optimizing manufacturability, cost, reliability, and quality. It encompasses the entire engineering methodology from **concept generation**, **concept selection**, **embodiment design**, and **detail design** to final **design analysis and evaluation**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Product Design and Analysis is positioned as a strategic industrial engineering function that bridges **market requirements** with **engineering realization**. The course outcome (CO) typically aligns with: *CO2 — Apply product design principles and analysis tools for developing economically viable and quality-driven products.*

The core sub-domains under this topic include:

| Sub-Domain | Focus |
|---|---|
| **Product Development Process** | Sequential and concurrent methodologies |
| **Concept Generation & Selection** | Ideation, morphological analysis, decision matrices |
| **Design for Manufacture & Assembly (DFMA)** | DFM and DFA principles |
| **Value Engineering / Value Analysis** | Function-based cost optimization |
| **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** | House of Quality (HOQ) |
| **Failure Mode & Effect Analysis (FMEA)** | Risk Priority Number (RPN) |
| **Design for X (DFX)** | DFA, DFE, DFS, DFR, DFC |
| **Standardization & Simplification** | Variety reduction, grouping, preferred numbers |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Designing a Kitchen Mixer-Grinder**
>
> Imagine you are designing a new mixer-grinder. The **Product Design** stage is where you decide: "Should it be a 750W or 1000W motor? Should the jar be glass or stainless steel? Should it have 3 speeds or a variable frequency drive?" The **Analysis** stage is where you stress-test the lid, simulate the gear meshing, calculate the cost per unit, predict failure modes (motor burnout, jar cracking), and check if it can be assembled in 90 seconds by a single operator. Together, these stages convert an *idea* into a *manufacturable, sellable, reliable reality*.

**In simple words:**
- **Product Design** = Asking *"What should we make and how should it look/work?"*
- **Product Analysis** = Asking *"Will it work, can we make it cheaply, will it last, and will the customer love it?"*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities Used

The key metrics in this module (highlighted in **bold**) are:

- **DFMA Index (Design for Manufacture and Assembly)** — dimensionless efficiency score
- **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** — dimensionless, computed as $S \times O \times D$
- **VE Savings Ratio** — dimensionless, computed as $\dfrac{\text{Original Cost} - \text{New Cost}}{\text{Original Cost}}$
- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — dimensionless, used in Six Sigma context
- **Preferred Number Ratios (Renard Series)** — $R_{10} = 1.2589$, $R_{20} = 1.1220$, $R_{40} = 1.0593$, $R_{80} = 1.0293$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Product Design Process (Ulrich & Eppinger Framework)

The widely accepted **Universal Design Process** comprises **6 phases**:

### Phase 1: Planning
- **Mission Statement** definition
- Identification of market opportunity
- **Business case** preparation
- **QFD (House of Quality)** to capture Voice of Customer (VOC)
- **Why:** Establishes strategic alignment.
- **How:** Through market research and customer interviews.

### Phase 2: Concept Development
- Identify customer needs (VOC → CTQ — Critical to Quality)
- Establish Target Specifications (TS)
- Generate concept variants via:
  - **Brainstorming**
  - **Morphological Chart**
  - **TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)**
  - **SCAMPER Technique**
- Select concept(s) using:
  - **Pugh Concept Selection Matrix**
  - **Weighted Decision Matrix**

### Phase 3: System-Level Design
- Define **product architecture**:
  - **Modular**
  - **Integral**
  - **Open/Closed**
- Define subsystems and interfaces
- Create **Functional Decomposition Diagram**
- Generate a **Preliminary Design**

### Phase 4: Detail Design
- Complete **engineering drawings** (GD&T compliant)
- **Bill of Materials (BOM)** finalized
- **DFMA** analysis performed
- **Tolerance stack-up** verified
- **Material specifications** locked
- **Process plans** drafted

### Phase 5: Testing and Refinement
- **Alpha prototype** → internal testing
- **Beta prototype** → limited customer release
- **DFMEA (Design FMEA)** finalized
- **Reliability testing** (MTBF, MTTR)

### Phase 6: Production Ramp-up
- Tooling installation
- **SPC (Statistical Process Control)** setup
- **Production Part Approval Process (PPAP)**
- **Supply chain** activation

## 2.2 Concept Selection — The Pugh Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pugh Concept Selection Matrix** compares concepts against a **baseline (datum) concept** scored on a scale of **+1 (better)**, **0 (same)**, **−1 (worse)**.

The **weighted score** is:

$$
W_{score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of criterion $i$ and $s_i \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$.

## 2.3 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — House of Quality

The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is a structured matrix with:

| HOQ Element | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Whats** | Customer Requirements (CRs) | "What the customer wants" |
| **Hows** | Engineering Characteristics (ECs) | "How we deliver it" |
| **Roof** | Correlation matrix | EC ↔ EC interaction |
| **Relationship matrix** | Body of house | CR ↔ EC strength |
| **Technical Benchmark** | Comparison | Vs. competitors |
| **Priorities** | Output | Ranked ECs |

The **absolute and relative importance** are computed as:

$$
\text{Relative Importance}_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (\text{Importance}_i \times \text{Relationship}_{ij})}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\text{Importance}_i \times \text{Relationship}_{ij})} \times 100
$$

## 2.4 Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RPN (Risk Priority Number) — The single most important FMEA metric**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- **S** = Severity (1 to 10) — consequence of failure
- **O** = Occurrence (1 to 10) — frequency of failure
- **D** = Detection (1 to 10) — probability that controls will detect the failure

**Decision Rule:** Actions are prioritized when $RPN \geq 100$ (industry threshold) or $S \geq 9$.

| RPN Range | Action |
|---|---|
| $1 \text{ to } 50$ | Low — accept risk |
| $51 \text{ to } 100$ | Moderate — consider action |
| $> 100$ | High — action mandatory |

## 2.5 Value Engineering (VE) / Value Analysis (VA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Value** in industrial engineering is defined by **Lawrence D. Miles** as:

$$
\text{Value} (V) = \frac{\text{Function} (F)}{\text{Cost} (C)}
$$

The objective of VE is to **maximize F and minimize C**, thereby maximizing V.

### VE Job Plan (5 Phases)
1. **Information Phase** — data collection on function and cost
2. **Speculation Phase** — generate alternatives (How–How / Why–Why)
3. **Evaluation Phase** — analyze alternatives
4. **Development Phase** — develop best alternatives
5. **Presentation Phase** — proposal to management

### VE Savings Ratio

$$
\text{Savings Ratio} = \frac{(C_{\text{original}} - C_{\text{new}})}{C_{\text{original}}} \times 100\%
$$

## 2.6 Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)

**DFMA = DFM + DFA**, where:

$$
\text{DFM Index} = \frac{\text{Estimated manufacturing cost}}{\text{Reference manufacturing cost}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{DFA Efficiency} = \frac{N_{\text{min}}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\%
$$

Where $N_{\min}$ is the theoretical minimum number of parts and $t_{\text{actual}}$ is the actual assembly time.

**Boothroyd's DFA Criteria** (the 3 questions):
1. Does the part move relative to all other parts? → If NO, candidate for elimination
2. Must the part be of different material? → If NO, candidate for elimination
3. Must the part be separated for assembly/disassembly? → If NO, candidate for elimination

## 2.7 Standardization & Preferred Numbers (Renard Series)

The **Renard Preferred Number Series** are based on geometric progression:

$$
a_n = a_1 \cdot R^{(n-1)}
$$

Where $R$ is the common ratio:

$$
R_N = 10^{1/N}
$$

| Series | Ratio R | Use |
|---|---|---|
| R5 | 1.5849 | Coarse |
| R10 | 1.2589 | Standard |
| R20 | 1.1220 | Fine |
| R40 | 1.0593 | Very fine |

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Variables** | **Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V = F / C$ | F = Function, C = Cost | Dimensionless | Value Engineering |
| $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | S, O, D = 1 to 10 | Dimensionless | FMEA prioritization |
| $W_{score} = \sum w_i s_i$ | $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = score | Dimensionless | Pugh Matrix |
| $\text{VE Savings} = \dfrac{C_o - C_n}{C_o} \times 100$ | Cost figures | % | VE Project |
| $\text{DFA Eff.} = \dfrac{N_{min}}{t_{actual}} \times 100$ | Parts, time | % | Assembly analysis |
| $a_n = a_1 R^{(n-1)}$ | R = $10^{1/N}$ | varies | Standardization |
| $\text{Rel. Imp.}_j = \dfrac{\sum I_i \cdot R_{ij}}{\sum_{j}\sum_i I_i R_{ij}} \times 100$ | HOQ matrix | % | QFD |
| $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ | D = defects, U = units, O = opportunities/op | ppm | Six Sigma |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **automotive OEM design** (Toyota, Ford), **aerospace** (Boeing, Airbus), **consumer electronics** (Apple, Samsung), and **manufacturing industries** globally to ensure products are cost-effective, reliable, and manufacturable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RPN-Based Decision Logic for FMEA

### Problem Statement
A design team identifies **5 potential failure modes** for a brake disc assembly. They must compute the RPN and recommend the priority of corrective action.

### Given Data

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| FM-1: Disc cracking | 9 | 4 | 6 |
| FM-2: Pad wear uneven | 7 | 6 | 3 |
| FM-3: Rotor warp | 8 | 5 | 7 |
| FM-4: Caliper seizure | 9 | 2 | 8 |
| FM-5: Fluid leak | 8 | 3 | 5 |

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1: Recall the governing equation.**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 2: Compute RPN for FM-1 (Disc cracking).**

$$
RPN_{FM1} = 9 \times 4 \times 6
$$

$$
RPN_{FM1} = 9 \times 24 = 216
$$

**Step 3: Compute RPN for FM-2 (Pad wear uneven).**

$$
RPN_{FM2} = 7 \times 6 \times 3
$$

$$
RPN_{FM2} = 7 \times 18 = 126
$$

**Step 4: Compute RPN for FM-3 (Rotor warp).**

$$
RPN_{FM3} = 8 \times 5 \times 7
$$

$$
RPN_{FM3} = 8 \times 35 = 280
$$

**Step 5: Compute RPN for FM-4 (Caliper seizure).**

$$
RPN_{FM4} = 9 \times 2 \times 8
$$

$$
RPN_{FM4} = 9 \times 16 = 144
$$

**Step 6: Compute RPN for FM-5 (Fluid leak).**

$$
RPN_{FM5} = 8 \times 3 \times 5
$$

$$
RPN_{FM5} = 8 \times 15 = 120
$$

**Step 7: Rank the failure modes in descending order of RPN.**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Action Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FM-3: Rotor warp | **280** | Immediate corrective action |
| 2 | FM-1: Disc cracking | **216** | Immediate corrective action |
| 3 | FM-4: Caliper seizure | **144** | High priority |
| 4 | FM-2: Pad wear uneven | **126** | Moderate priority |
| 5 | FM-5: Fluid leak | **120** | Moderate priority |

**Step 8: Apply the S-based override rule.**

If $S \geq 9$, action is mandatory regardless of total RPN.
- FM-1 has S = 9 → Mandatory action
- FM-4 has S = 9 → Mandatory action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** All five failure modes require engineering intervention because four of them exceed the standard RPN threshold of 100. The two highest priorities are FM-3 (Rotor warp) and FM-1 (Disc cracking).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pugh Concept Selection Matrix with Weighted Scoring

### Problem Statement
A design team is selecting between **3 concept alternatives** for a water purifier housing. They have **4 evaluation criteria** with associated weights.

### Given Data

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | Concept A | Concept B | Concept C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1: Cost | 5 | 0 | +1 | -1 |
| C2: Manufacturability | 4 | +1 | 0 | +1 |
| C3: Aesthetics | 3 | -1 | +1 | 0 |
| C4: Durability | 4 | +1 | -1 | +1 |

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1: Recall the weighted scoring equation.**

$$
W_{score,k} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{i,k}
$$

Where $k \in \{A, B, C\}$ and $s_{i,k} \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$.

**Step 2: Compute score for Concept A.**

$$
W_{score,A} = (5)(0) + (4)(+1) + (3)(-1) + (4)(+1)
$$

$$
W_{score,A} = 0 + 4 - 3 + 4 = +5
$$

**Step 3: Compute score for Concept B.**

$$
W_{score,B} = (5)(+1) + (4)(0) + (3)(+1) + (4)(-1)
$$

$$
W_{score,B} = 5 + 0 + 3 - 4 = +4
$$

**Step 4: Compute score for Concept C.**

$$
W_{score,C} = (5)(-1) + (4)(+1) + (3)(0) + (4)(+1)
$$

$$
W_{score,C} = -5 + 4 + 0 + 4 = +3
$$

**Step 5: Rank concepts by weighted score.**

| Rank | Concept | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Concept A** | **+5** (Selected) |
| 2 | Concept B | +4 |
| 3 | Concept C | +3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Concept A is the winning design and is selected for embodiment design. Concept B is held as a backup.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Value Engineering Savings Calculation

### Problem Statement
A company producing an electric motor housing uses **die-cast aluminum**. The original cost is ₹850 per unit. The VE team proposes switching to a **glass-filled polymer** with the same functional performance. The new cost is ₹520 per unit. Calculate the **VE savings ratio**.

### Given Data
- $C_{original} = 850$ INR
- $C_{new} = 520$ INR

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1: Recall the savings equation.**

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{C_{original} - C_{new}}{C_{original}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{850 - 520}{850} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator.**

$$
850 - 520 = 330
$$

**Step 4: Divide by original cost.**

$$
\frac{330}{850} = 0.3882
$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage.**

$$
\text{Savings} = 0.3882 \times 100\% = 38.82\%
$$

**Step 6: Compute the new Value ratio.**

$$
V_{old} = \frac{F}{C_{old}}, \quad V_{new} = \frac{F}{C_{new}}
$$

If function is constant:

$$
\frac{V_{new}}{V_{old}} = \frac{C_{old}}{C_{new}} = \frac{850}{520} = 1.635
$$

So the value has increased by **63.5%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The VE project delivers a **38.82% cost reduction** and a **63.5% value increase** while maintaining the same functional performance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: DFA Efficiency Calculation (Boothroyd Method)

### Problem Statement
A product originally has **42 parts** and takes **180 seconds** to assemble. The DFA team proposes a redesign reducing the count to **28 parts** while assembly time reduces to **105 seconds**. The theoretical minimum part count for the function is **22 parts**. Calculate the **DFA efficiency** before and after.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1: Recall the DFA efficiency equation.**

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \frac{N_{min}}{t_{actual}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2: Compute initial DFA efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{DFA,old} = \frac{22}{180} \times 100 = 12.22\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute new DFA efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{DFA,new} = \frac{22}{105} \times 100 = 20.95\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute improvement.**

$$
\Delta\eta = 20.95 - 12.22 = 8.73 \text{ percentage points}
$$

**Step 5: Compute relative improvement.**

$$
\text{Relative Improvement} = \frac{20.95 - 12.22}{12.22} \times 100 = 71.44\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The DFA redesign increased assembly efficiency by **71.44% relative to baseline**, demonstrating significant simplification. However, the absolute efficiency is still well below 100%, indicating room for further part consolidation toward the theoretical minimum of 22 parts.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Concept Selection Criteria Mapping

| Tool | Primary Use | Input | Output | KTU Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Morphological Chart** | Concept generation | Sub-functions & means | Multiple concepts | L3 — Apply |
| **Pugh Matrix** | Concept screening | Concepts & criteria | +/0/− score | L4 — Analyze |
| **Weighted Decision Matrix** | Concept selection | Weighted criteria | Ranked concepts | L5 — Evaluate |
| **QFD (HOQ)** | Customer-driven design | VOC & ECs | EC priorities | L5 — Evaluate |
| **FMEA** | Risk assessment | Failure modes | RPN ranking | L5 — Evaluate |
| **VE / VA** | Cost optimization | Function-cost data | Cost savings | L6 — Create |
| **DFMA** | Simplification | Part list & BOM | Reduced part count | L6 — Create |
| **Renard Series** | Standardization | Geometric series | Preferred numbers | L3 — Apply |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Product Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Planning and Mission] --> B[Phase 2: Concept Development]
    B --> C[Phase 3: System Level Design]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Detail Design]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Testing and Refinement]
    E --> F[Phase 6: Production Ramp Up]
    F --> G[Market Launch and Feedback]
    G -.Loopback Feedback.-> A
    D --> H[DFMA Analysis]
    D --> I[FMEA Analysis]
    D --> J[Value Engineering]
    H --> D
    I --> D
    J --> D
```

**Interpretation of the Flowchart:**
- The **6 sequential phases** (A to F) form the **outer waterfall cycle**.
- **DFMA, FMEA, and VE** operate as **parallel sub-engines** (H, I, J) attached to the Detail Design phase, providing continuous feedback.
- The **G → A loopback** represents **post-launch customer feedback** that initiates the next product generation cycle.

---

## 4.2 House of Quality (QFD) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph House[HOUSE OF QUALITY HOQ]
        direction TB
        subgraph Whats[WHATS: Customer Requirements]
            CR1[Cost Affordable]
            CR2[Durability]
            CR3[Ease of Use]
            CR4[Aesthetics]
        end
        subgraph Roof[ROOF: EC to EC Correlation]
            RC1[Strong Positive]
            RC2[Negative Trade Off]
            RC3[Strong Positive]
        end
        subgraph Body[BODY: Relationship Matrix]
            RM1[9 Strong]
            RM2[3 Moderate]
            RM3[1 Weak]
        end
        subgraph Hows[HOWS: Engineering Characteristics]
            EC1[Material Grade]
            EC2[Motor Power]
            EC3[Surface Finish]
            EC4[Dimensional Accuracy]
        end
        subgraph Bench[TECHNICAL BENCHMARK]
            TB1[Competitor A]
            TB2[Competitor B]
            TB3[Our Target]
        end
    end
    Whats --> Body
    Roof --> Body
    Body --> Hows
    Bench --> Body
```

**Interpretation of the House of Quality:**
- **WHATS** (left wall): Customer Requirements (CRs) prioritized by importance
- **ROOF** (top): Correlations between Engineering Characteristics (ECs)
- **BODY** (centre): The 1/3/9 relationship matrix between CRs and ECs
- **HOWS** (floor): Engineering Characteristics output with **target values**
- **BENCHMARK** (right wall): Competitive comparison

---

## 4.3 DFMA Decision Tree (Boothroyd Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start: Analyze Each Part] --> Q1{Does the part MOVE relative to all others during operation?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Must the part be of a DIFFERENT material?}
    Q1 -- YES --> KEEP1[KEEP PART]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Must the part be SEPARATED for assembly or disassembly?}
    Q2 -- YES --> KEEP2[KEEP PART]
    Q3 -- NO --> ELIM[ELIMINATE PART: Redesign as integral feature]
    Q3 -- YES --> KEEP3[KEEP PART]
    ELIM --> NEXT[Analyze Next Part]
    KEEP1 --> NEXT
    KEEP2 --> NEXT
    KEEP3 --> NEXT
    NEXT --> DONE{All Parts Analyzed?}
    DONE -- NO --> P0
    DONE -- YES --> OUT[Compute DFA Efficiency]
```

**Interpretation of the DFMA Tree:**
- Each part is **evaluated against 3 sequential questions**.
- A part is **eliminated only if all 3 answers are NO**.
- The output produces a reduced BOM and improved DFA efficiency.

---

## 4.4 FMEA Risk Assessment Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph L[Low RPN: 1 to 50: Accept]
        L1[Severity 1 to 3]
        L2[Occurrence 1 to 4]
    end
    subgraph M[Medium RPN: 51 to 100: Monitor]
        M1[Severity 4 to 6]
        M2[Occurrence 5 to 6]
    end
    subgraph H[High RPN: Above 100: Action Mandatory]
        H1[Severity 7 to 10]
        H2[Occurrence 7 to 10]
    end
    FM[Failure Mode Identified] --> CALC[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    CALC --> DEC{RPN Threshold}
    DEC -- 1 to 50 --> L
    DEC -- 51 to 100 --> M
    DEC -- Above 100 --> H
    L --> ACC[Accept and Document]
    M --> MON[Monitor and Reduce D]
    H --> ACT[Implement Corrective Action]
```

**Interpretation of the FMEA Risk Matrix:**
- **RPN is the gating metric**.
- High RPN → mandatory redesign or mitigation
- Medium RPN → improve **Detection (D)** through better testing
- Low RPN → document and accept

---

## 4.5 Value Engineering Job Plan

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Information Phase] --> P2[Phase 2: Speculation Phase]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Evaluation Phase]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Development Phase]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Presentation Phase]
    P5 --> IMP[Management Approval and Implementation]
    P1 -.Function Cost Analysis.-> FA[FA: Identify Basic and Secondary Functions]
    FA --> P2
    P2 -.Brainstorm.-> ALT[Generate Multiple Alternatives]
    ALT --> P3
    P3 -.Feasibility.-> SEL[Select Best Alternatives]
    SEL --> P4
    P4 -.Engineering.-> DET[Develop Detailed Design and Prototype]
    DET --> P5
```

**Interpretation of the VE Job Plan:**
- The **5-phase plan** is **linear** but allows iteration.
- **Phase 1 (Information)** is the most data-intensive.
- **Phase 5 (Presentation)** is the management buy-in stage.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **KTU Marking Note:** Part A questions are expected to be answered in **80 to 120 words** with crisp, textbook-aligned language. Avoid narrative-style answers.

### Question 1: Define Product Design and list its key stages. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Product Design is the process of defining the form, function, and features of a product to meet customer needs. The key stages are: **(1) Planning, (2) Concept Development, (3) System-Level Design, (4) Detail Design, (5) Testing and Refinement, and (6) Production Ramp-up**. These stages convert market requirements into a manufacturable product.

### Question 2: What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
RPN is a metric used in Failure Mode and Effect Analysis to prioritize failure modes. It is computed as **RPN = S × O × D**, where S is Severity, O is Occurrence, and D is Detection, each rated on a scale of 1 to 10. A higher RPN indicates greater risk. A threshold of **RPN ≥ 100** typically triggers mandatory corrective action.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice, divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Show all sub-steps. Box the final answer.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question]`

> **(a)** Explain the Universal Product Design Process with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **DFMA** in the Detail Design phase. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A product has **4 failure modes** with the following S, O, D scores. Compute RPN for each, rank them, and recommend the corrective action strategy. Use threshold **RPN ≥ 100**. **(7 marks)**

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| FM-A | 8 | 5 | 6 |
| FM-B | 9 | 3 | 7 |
| FM-C | 6 | 7 | 4 |
| FM-D | 7 | 4 | 5 |

#### Model Solution for (a)

The Universal Product Design Process consists of **6 phases**:

**Phase 1 — Planning:** Identify market opportunity, define mission statement, prepare business case. The output is a **Product Design Specification (PDS)**.

**Phase 2 — Concept Development:** Generate concepts via brainstorming, morphological analysis, and TRIZ. Select the best concept using **Pugh Matrix** and **Weighted Decision Matrix**.

**Phase 3 — System-Level Design:** Define product architecture (modular, integral, or open). Perform **functional decomposition** and create subsystem interfaces.

**Phase 4 — Detail Design:** Complete engineering drawings, finalize BOM, perform **DFMA analysis**, and finalize tolerances and GD&T specifications.

**Phase 5 — Testing and Refinement:** Build alpha and beta prototypes, perform **DFMEA**, conduct reliability tests (MTBF, MTTR).

**Phase 6 — Production Ramp-up:** Install tooling, implement SPC, execute PPAP, and launch the product.

**Role of DFMA in Detail Design Phase:**
DFMA combines **Design for Manufacture (DFM)** and **Design for Assembly (DFA)**. It reduces part count, simplifies assembly operations, selects optimal materials, and minimizes manufacturing cost. Boothroyd's 3-question test is applied to each part to determine elimination candidacy. The **DFA efficiency** is computed as:

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \frac{N_{min}}{t_{actual}} \times 100
$$

DFMA directly impacts cost, lead time, and quality in the detail design stage.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** S, O, D values for 4 failure modes. Compute RPN and rank.

**Step 1: Apply the RPN formula.**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 2: Compute RPN for FM-A.**

$$
RPN_A = 8 \times 5 \times 6 = 240
$$

**Step 3: Compute RPN for FM-B.**

$$
RPN_B = 9 \times 3 \times 7 = 189
$$

**Step 4: Compute RPN for FM-C.**

$$
RPN_C = 6 \times 7 \times 4 = 168
$$

**Step 5: Compute RPN for FM-D.**

$$
RPN_D = 7 \times 4 \times 5 = 140
$$

**Step 6: Rank in descending order of RPN.**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FM-A | **240** | Immediate corrective action |
| 2 | FM-B | **189** | Immediate corrective action |
| 3 | FM-C | **168** | Corrective action required |
| 4 | FM-D | **140** | Corrective action required |

**Step 7: Apply the S-override rule.**
- FM-B has S = 9 → Mandatory action regardless of RPN

> **Final Answer Box:** FM-A is the highest-risk failure mode (RPN = 240). All four failure modes exceed the threshold of 100, hence all require corrective action.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

> **(a)** Define **Value Engineering**. Explain the **5-phase VE Job Plan** with a block diagram. A product currently costs ₹1200 per unit. After a VE study, the new cost is ₹780 per unit. Calculate the **savings percentage** and the **value ratio improvement**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **House of Quality (QFD)** with a block diagram. List **5 benefits** of QFD in product design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition:** Value Engineering is a **systematic method** to improve the *value* of goods or products and services by using an examination of function. Value (V) is defined as the ratio of Function (F) to Cost (C):

$$
V = \frac{F}{C}
$$

**5-Phase VE Job Plan:**

**Phase 1 — Information Phase:** Collect data on the product, its function, and cost. Identify the **basic function** and **secondary functions**.

**Phase 2 — Speculation Phase:** Generate alternative ideas via brainstorming. Use **How–How** and **Why–Why** analysis.

**Phase 3 — Evaluation Phase:** Analyze each alternative for feasibility, cost, and function delivery.

**Phase 4 — Development Phase:** Develop the best alternative into a detailed design with prototype testing.

**Phase 5 — Presentation Phase:** Present the proposal to management with documentation of savings.

**Step-by-Step Savings Calculation:**

$$
\text{Given: } C_{old} = 1200, \quad C_{new} = 780
$$

**Step 1: Compute cost reduction.**

$$
\Delta C = 1200 - 780 = 420
$$

**Step 2: Compute savings percentage.**

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{420}{1200} \times 100 = 35\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute value ratio improvement (assuming function is constant).**

$$
\frac{V_{new}}{V_{old}} = \frac{C_{old}}{C_{new}} = \frac{1200}{780} = 1.538
$$

**Step 4: Express as percentage improvement.**

$$
\text{Value Improvement} = (1.538 - 1) \times 100 = 53.85\%
$$

> **Final Answer Box:** Savings = **35%**; Value improvement = **53.85%**.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**House of Quality (HOQ) Definition:**
The House of Quality is the primary matrix used in **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** that translates **customer requirements (WHATS)** into **engineering characteristics (HOWS)**.

**Block Diagram (verbal description):**
- **Left Wall:** Customer Requirements (CRs) ranked by importance
- **Ceiling (Roof):** Correlation matrix between Engineering Characteristics (EC ↔ EC)
- **Body:** Relationship matrix (CR ↔ EC) using symbols 9 (strong), 3 (moderate), 1 (weak)
- **Floor:** Engineering Characteristics with target values
- **Right Wall:** Technical competitive benchmarking

**Step-by-Step QFD Process:**

**Step 1:** Identify customer needs via Voice of Customer (VOC) tools.

**Step 2:** Translate CRs into Engineering Characteristics (ECs).

**Step 3:** Build the relationship matrix with strength symbols.

**Step 4:** Determine EC ↔ EC correlations in the roof.

**Step 5:** Compute **relative importance** of each EC:

$$
RI_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} I_i \cdot R_{ij}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{m} I_i R_{ij}} \times 100
$$

**Step 6:** Set target values for the top-priority ECs.

**5 Benefits of QFD:**
1. **Customer-focused design** — ensures VOC drives engineering decisions
2. **Cross-functional team alignment** — marketing, design, and manufacturing collaborate
3. **Reduced design changes** — up to 50% reduction in engineering change orders
4. **Faster time-to-market** — clear priorities reduce iteration cycles
5. **Competitive benchmarking** — built-in competitor analysis

> **Final Answer Box:** QFD is a structured planning tool that converts customer needs into engineering targets, using the House of Quality matrix with WHATS, HOWS, relationships, correlations, and benchmarks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (where KTU students lose 2 to 4 marks per question):**
>
> 1. **RPN Calculation Error:** Students often add S, O, D instead of multiplying them. The correct operation is **multiplication**, not addition.
>
> 2. **Confusing DFM with DFA:** DFM is for **manufacturing process optimization**; DFA is for **assembly simplification**. They are combined in DFMA, but examiners expect distinct definitions.
>
> 3. **Missing the "Function = Constant" Assumption in VE:** When computing value improvement, students forget to state that the function is unchanged. This is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Pugh Matrix Scale:** Students write "+1, 0, −1" but forget to apply the **datum/baseline concept** as reference. Always declare the baseline first.
>
> 5. **QFD Without Numbers:** QFD requires at least one numerical computation (e.g., relative importance). A pure diagram-only answer gets only 4 of 7 marks.
>
> 6. **No Block Diagram in Design Process Questions:** Any 7-mark question on the design process must include a **block diagram**. Skipping it costs 2 marks.
>
> 7. **Rounding Errors:** Always retain **2 decimal places** in VE savings calculations. Rough answers like "35%" without units cost partial marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — PRODUCT DESIGN AND ANALYSIS**

- **Product Design** = systematic conversion of customer needs into a manufacturable product through 6 phases.
- The **6 phases** are: Planning → Concept Development → System-Level Design → Detail Design → Testing & Refinement → Production Ramp-up.
- **DFMA = DFM + DFA**; the **Boothroyd 3-question test** identifies parts for elimination.
- **DFA efficiency** is computed as $N_{min} / t_{actual} \times 100$.
- **RPN = S × O × D** with each parameter rated 1 to 10; threshold is typically **100**.
- **S ≥ 9** triggers mandatory action regardless of RPN.
- **Pugh Matrix** uses **+1, 0, −1** scoring against a **baseline (datum)** concept.
- **Weighted Decision Matrix** uses **$W_{score} = \sum w_i s_i$**.
- **QFD (House of Quality)** translates CRs (WHATS) into ECs (HOWS) with a 1/3/9 relationship matrix.
- **Value Engineering** follows the **5-phase Job Plan** (Information → Speculation → Evaluation → Development → Presentation).
- **Value (V) = F / C**; **Savings % = (C_old − C_new) / C_old × 100**.
- **Renard Preferred Numbers** use geometric progression: $R_N = 10^{1/N}$ for R5, R10, R20, R40 series.
- **Design for X (DFX)** includes DFA (Assembly), DFE (Environment), DFS (Sustainability), DFR (Reliability), DFC (Cost).
- **Concurrent Engineering** integrates design with manufacturing, marketing, and service planning from the start.
- **Standardization & Simplification** reduce variety using preferred numbers and grouping techniques.
- For **KTU 14-mark questions**, always include a **block diagram** and **numerical example** to score full marks.
- Always **box the final numerical answer** for examiner clarity.

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 New Product Development (NPD)

### Formal Definition
**New Product Development (NPD)** is the complete process of bringing a new product to the market, encompassing the conceptualization, design, development, and commercialization of a product that is either entirely novel or represents a significant improvement over existing offerings. As per KTU 2024 Industrial Engineering syllabus, NPD is treated as a systematic, cross-functional activity that integrates marketing, design, manufacturing, and quality engineering disciplines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**: "New Product Development is the set of activities that transforms a market opportunity into a product available for sale." It is NOT just inventing a product; it is the **entire cradle-to-market journey**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you want to start a small restaurant in Kerala serving *kappa* (tapioca) dishes in a new, modern avatar.
- **Idea Generation** = You think "Why not fusion kappa dishes?"
- **Screening** = You check with friends and family — do people want this?
- **Concept Development** = You create a sample menu and recipe.
- **Business Analysis** = You estimate cost of ingredients vs. selling price (profitability).
- **Product Development** = You actually cook and perfect the dishes in your test kitchen.
- **Test Marketing** = You open a small pop-up stall for one weekend and observe customer reactions.
- **Commercialization** = You open your full restaurant!

This is exactly how a product like a **new electric scooter (e.g., the latest electric two-wheeler in India)** is developed — from a spark of an idea to a showroom-ready vehicle.

### Categories of New Products
| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **New-to-the-World Products** | Truly innovative, never seen before | First iPhone (2007) |
| **New Product Lines** | New category for the company but exists in market | Tata entering EV market |
| **Additions to Existing Lines** | Variations of current products | New colour/feature variant |
| **Improvements/Revisions** | Enhanced performance of existing product | Updated software version |
| **Repositioned Products** | Existing product targeted to new market | Old SUV repackaged as "premium lifestyle vehicle" |
| **Cost Reductions** | Same product at lower cost through redesign | Budget variant of a phone |

---

## 1.2 Process Planning and Process Design

### Formal Definition
**Process Planning** is the systematic determination of the manufacturing operations, their sequence, the machines, tools, jigs, fixtures, and inspection methods required to convert raw material into a finished product that meets design specifications.

**Process Design** is the broader activity of determining the entire production system layout, workflow, equipment selection, and operational sequence required to manufacture a product at the desired quality, quantity, and cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU treats Process Planning as the **macro-level "WHAT to do and HOW to do"** and Process Design as the **macro-level "WHERE, with WHAT machinery, and by WHOM"** for the entire production system.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **Process Planning** as writing the *recipe* for a dish (boil 2 cups rice, add 1 cup dal, simmer 15 min) — it tells you *what* to do step-by-step.
**Process Design** is the *kitchen layout* — where is the stove, the sink, the storage, the workflow from chopping → cooking → plating → serving.

In a factory: Process Planning = Route Sheet; Process Design = Factory Floor Layout.

---

## 1.3 Value Engineering (VE)

### Formal Definition
**Value Engineering (VE)** is a systematic, function-oriented, team-based creative technique used to analyze the function of a product, system, or service with the objective of achieving the required function at the **lowest total cost** without sacrificing quality, reliability, performance, or safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Original Definition by Lawrence D. Miles (1947, General Electric)**: "Value Engineering is the systematic application of recognized techniques which identify the function of a product or service, establish a value for that function, and provide the necessary function reliably at the lowest overall cost."

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you buy a **gold-plated ₹50 coin holder** to hold a ₹10 coin. The "function" is to *hold a coin*. The cost is ₹2,000. 
- The **value** of the holder is **too low** relative to its cost.
- VE would say: "A simple ₹20 plastic clip holds the coin just as well — same function, much lower cost."

VE is the **engineering equivalent of removing unnecessary cost while preserving function**. It is NOT cost-cutting blindly; it is **intelligent function analysis**.

### Value Equation
The core concept is expressed as:

$$\text{Value} = \frac{\text{Function (Worth)}}}{\text{Cost}}$$

To increase value, you can: (1) increase function, (2) decrease cost, or (3) do both.

---

## 1.4 Standardization

### Formal Definition
**Standardization** is the process of establishing and implementing **technical standards** based on the consensus of all interested parties, to provide rules, guidelines, or characteristics for activities or their results, aimed at the promotion of optimal benefits and reduction of variety.

In Industrial Engineering, standardization refers specifically to the **deliberate reduction of variety in products, parts, processes, and procedures** to achieve uniformity, interchangeability, and economic efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU emphasizes that standardization is **NOT about reducing quality** — it is about establishing a **benchmark of quality and conformity**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **USB-C charging ports**. Every modern phone, tablet, and laptop uses the same USB-C port. Imagine the chaos if every brand had its own unique charger shape! Standardization brings **order, compatibility, and mass-production efficiency**.

---

## 1.5 Simplification

### Formal Definition
**Simplification** is the conscious effort to reduce the number of types, sizes, grades, and varieties of products, parts, components, and processes to a manageable minimum that still satisfies the functional requirements of users.

Simplification is a direct *consequence* and *tool* of standardization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Relationship**: Standardization = "Setting the standard"; Simplification = "Reducing variety TO that standard." They are inseparable twins in IE.

### Conceptual Analogy
A tailor offers **12 sizes of shirts** (S, M, L, XL, etc., each with 3 fits). If you ask "Why?" they say customer choice. But if 80% of sales come from only 3 sizes, **simplification says: keep just 3 sizes, reduce inventory, reduce cost, faster delivery**. Same customer satisfaction, much lower complexity.

---

## 1.6 Make or Buy Decisions

### Formal Definition
A **Make or Buy Decision** is a strategic choice in industrial management that determines whether a particular part, component, sub-assembly, or service should be manufactured in-house (made) or procured from an external vendor (bought).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **strategic, recurring, high-impact decision** — once you "buy" a critical component for years, rebuilding in-house capacity becomes extremely difficult and expensive.

### Conceptual Analogy
You run a bakery. Should you **bake your own bread** (Make) or **buy bread from another bakery** and just sell sandwiches (Buy)?
- If you bake, you need ovens, bakers, flour storage, quality control — high **fixed cost**.
- If you buy, you lose some control over recipe but save capital.
- This is exactly the dilemma faced by an automobile company deciding whether to manufacture its own **battery cells** (Make) or source from a supplier like CATL (Buy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Highlight**: The Make or Buy decision determines the **vertical integration** level of the firm. Total Make = Full Vertical Integration; Total Buy = Pure Outsourcing/Asset-Light Model.
