# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 New Product Development — The 8-Step Process (Detailed)

NPD is rarely linear — modern approaches use **Stage-Gate** or **Agile** models, but the **8 classical steps** remain the KTU board exam standard.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Idea Generation (Idea Stimulation)**
- **Why**: Innovation begins with a pool of raw ideas.
- **How**: Sources include customers, employees, R&D, competitors, suppliers, trade shows, brain-storming sessions.
- **Output**: Hundreds of unfiltered ideas.

**Step 2: Idea Screening**
- **Why**: Most ideas are technically or commercially unviable; quick elimination saves cost.
- **How**: Use screening criteria — market size, profitability, compatibility with company goals, technical feasibility.
- **Tool**: **Concept Screening Matrix**, R-W-W (Real Win Worth) screening.

**Step 3: Concept Development and Testing**
- **Why**: Abstract ideas must be converted into concrete product concepts.
- **How**: Develop 2–3 detailed concepts; test with target customers via focus groups/surveys.
- **Output**: Chosen concept with detailed description.

**Step 4: Business/Marketing Strategy Development**
- **Why**: The chosen concept must have a clear go-to-market plan.
- **How**: Define target market, positioning, pricing, promotion, distribution (4 Ps).
- **Output**: Preliminary marketing strategy document.

**Step 5: Product and Process Development (Technical Implementation)**
- **Why**: Strategy must be turned into a real, working product.
- **How**: R&D builds prototypes, design finalizes drawings, manufacturing develops the production process.
- **Output**: Working prototype, Bill of Materials (BOM), route sheets.

**Step 6: Test Marketing (Trial Marketing)**
- **Why**: National launch is expensive and risky; test in a small representative area first.
- **How**: Launch in select cities; measure sales, customer response, repeat purchase.
- **Output**: Go / No-Go decision for full launch.

**Step 7: Commercialization (Full-Scale Launch)**
- **Why**: Time to scale up.
- **How**: Full production, distribution, advertising, sales force training.
- **Critical decisions**: Timing (when to launch), Geography (where to launch first).

**Step 8: Post-Launch Review and Product Life Cycle Management**
- **Why**: To identify problems, gather feedback, and plan improvements or discontinuations.
- **How**: Track sales, customer complaints, warranty data, competitor response.

### Reasons for New Product Development
1. Changing customer needs and preferences.
2. Technological advancements and innovation.
3. Competitive pressure (survival and growth).
4. Saturation/decline of existing product (Product Life Cycle).
5. Utilization of surplus capacity or by-products.
6. Government regulations and standards.
7. Diversification to reduce business risk.
8. Improving company image and brand equity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Statistics (KTU favourite line)**: Studies show that out of every **7–8 new product ideas, only 1** becomes a commercial success. The other 6–7 fail at various stages. This is why **systematic NPD process** is critical.

---

## 2.2 Process Planning — Detailed Logic

### Steps in Process Planning

1. **Analyze the Part/Product Drawing** — Identify features, tolerances, surface finish, material.
2. **Determine the Operations Required** — List machining, forming, heat treatment, assembly, finishing operations.
3. **Select Machines and Equipment** — Match operations to available/procured machines.
4. **Select Tools, Jigs, Fixtures, Gauges** — Identify cutting tools, work-holding devices, inspection tools.
5. **Determine Operation Sequence** — Order the operations in the most efficient sequence.
6. **Calculate Time Standards** — Determine cycle time, setup time for each operation.
7. **Decide Inspection Points** — Define where and what to inspect for quality control.
8. **Prepare Route Sheet** — Document the entire process in a single **Process Route Sheet**.

### Route Sheet Format
| Operation No. | Operation Name | Machine | Tool | Fixture | Time (min) | Inspector |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | Face milling | CNC VMC | Face mill 100mm | V-block clamp | 2.5 | A |
| 20 | Drill 3 holes | Radial drill | HSS 10mm | Drill jig | 4.0 | B |
| 30 | Grind flat | Surface grinder | Wheel A46 | Magnetic chuck | 3.2 | C |

### Process Design Considerations
- **Volume of production** (Unit, Batch, Mass).
- **Flexibility** required (dedicated vs. flexible system).
- **Capital available** for equipment.
- **Available workforce skill level**.
- **Quality level** required.
- **Lead time** expected by customers.
- **Make or Buy** decisions (covered later).

### Types of Production Processes
| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Job Shop** | Low volume, high variety, skilled labour | Custom machinery |
| **Batch Production** | Medium volume, limited variety | Garment factory |
| **Mass Production** | High volume, low variety, assembly line | Car assembly |
| **Continuous Production** | 24x7, highly automated | Oil refinery, paper mill |
| **Project Production** | One-off, large scale | Ship-building, construction |

---

## 2.3 Value Engineering — The Job Plan

Lawrence Miles formalized a **7-phase Job Plan** that is a **direct KTU exam favourite**.

### The 7-Phase VE Job Plan

| Phase | Name | Activity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information Phase** | Gather all data about the product, function, cost, customer needs. |
| 2 | **Function Analysis Phase** | Define functions using **two-word verb-noun** format (e.g., "transmit torque", "support load"). Distinguish **Use Functions** (basic purpose) from **Aesthetic Functions** (appearance). |
| 3 | **Creative Phase** | Generate alternative ways to perform each function through brainstorming. |
| 4 | **Evaluation Phase** | Screen alternatives on cost, feasibility, reliability. |
| 5 | **Development Phase** | Develop the most promising alternative into a detailed proposal with cost-benefit analysis. |
| 6 | **Recommendation Phase** | Present proposal to management for approval. |
| 7 | **Implementation Phase** | Execute approved change; monitor savings and performance. |

### Function Analysis System Technique (FAST)
FAST diagrams are logic-driven graphical tools that show **"how"** and **"why"** relationships between functions. It helps in identifying **unnecessary functions** (cost without value).

### Cost and Worth Concepts in VE
- **Direct Cost**: Cost of materials, labour, overhead directly tied to the part.
- **Overhead Cost**: Indirect cost (administration, utilities, depreciation).
- **Use Value (Worth)**: The minimum cost to perform a function reliably.
- **Esteem Value**: Worth from aesthetic appeal, brand image.
- **Exchange Value**: Worth from resale or replacement value.

### Value Engineering Tests (for ideas)
A new VE idea must pass these tests, often remembered as a single mnemonic: **SPECS**
- **S** — Will it improve the idea (i.e., meet customer need)?
- **P** — Can it be implemented at present?
- **E** — Will it reduce cost?
- **C** — Will it reduce time?
- **S** — Will it reduce investment/space/manpower?

---

## 2.4 Standardization — Detailed Logic

### Types of Standardization

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimensional Standardization** | Standardization of sizes, dimensions, tolerances | M10 bolt, IS standards |
| **Material Standardization** | Specification of raw materials | IS 2062 for structural steel |
| **Parts Standardization** | Use of common/interchangeable parts | Common brake pads across car models |
| **Process Standardization** | Standard methods, routings, procedures | ISO 9001, SOPs |
| **Performance Standardization** | Output performance norms | BEE star ratings for ACs |
| **Quality Standardization** | Quality parameters and acceptance levels | ISO 9001:2015 |
| **Terminology Standardization** | Uniform technical vocabulary | ISI, BIS glossaries |
| **Design Standardization** | Standard product designs | Modular kitchen, LEGO bricks |

### Organizations Involved in Standardization
- **BIS** — Bureau of Indian Standards (India).
- **ISO** — International Organization for Standardization.
- **ASTM** — American Society for Testing and Materials.
- **DIN** — Deutsches Institut für Normung (Germany).
- **JIS** — Japanese Industrial Standards.

### Advantages of Standardization
1. Reduces variety → lower inventory and handling cost.
2. Enables mass production → lower unit cost.
3. Improves interchangeability → easier maintenance.
4. Simplifies purchasing and supplier management.
5. Improves quality through established benchmarks.
6. Facilitates training (workers learn standard methods quickly).
7. Reduces design effort (use of preferred numbers and standard parts).
8. Easier inspection and quality control.

### Disadvantages of Standardization
1. Reduces design flexibility and innovation.
2. May not satisfy all niche customer requirements.
3. Locked into old designs even when better technology emerges.
4. Initial setup of standards is time-consuming and costly.
5. Over-standardization → reduced product differentiation → loss of competitive edge.

---

## 2.5 Simplification — Detailed Logic

### Objectives of Simplification
- Reduce number of product variants.
- Reduce inventory and working capital tied up in stock.
- Reduce complexity in manufacturing and inspection.
- Improve product reliability (fewer parts, fewer failure points).
- Reduce lead time to customers.
- Enable automation (smaller variety → easier automation).

### Methods of Simplification
1. **Variety Reduction** — Eliminate slow-moving SKUs.
2. **Modular Design** — Group parts into standard modules.
3. **Group Technology** — Cluster similar parts to use common processes.
4. **Preferred Numbers (Renard Series)** — Use logarithmic series (1, 2, 4, 8; or 1, 1.5, 2, 3...) for size steps.
5. **Value Analysis** — Remove parts with low value-to-cost ratio.

### Preferred Number Series (Renard Series)
$$R_{n} = 10^{\left(\frac{n}{N}\right)} \quad \text{for} \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

For R5 series (n=0 to 5): 1, 1.6, 2.5, 4.0, 6.3, 10.0
For R10 series: 1, 1.25, 1.6, 2.0, 2.5, 3.15, 4.0, 5.0, 6.3, 8.0, 10.0

This ensures geometric progression — each step is a constant ratio.

---

## 2.6 Make or Buy Decision — Cost Analysis Framework

### Factors Influencing Make or Buy Decision

**A. Quantitative (Cost-related) Factors**
- Direct material cost.
- Direct labour cost.
- Variable overhead.
- Fixed overhead (only relevant if avoidable by buying).
- Transportation and packing cost (if buying).
- Purchase price of supplier.
- Cost of inspection and quality control.
- Inventory carrying cost of bought item.

**B. Qualitative (Strategic) Factors**
- Confidentiality of technology / IP.
- Dependence on supplier.
- Long-term supplier relationship.
- Flexibility and responsiveness.
- Core competency alignment.
- Trade union / labour considerations.
- Government policies (e.g., Make in India, tax incentives).
- Risk of supply disruption.

### Decision Rule (Simplified)

$$\text{Decision: BUY, if } \quad C_{\text{buy}} < C_{\text{make}}$$

where:

$$C_{\text{make}} = C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{labour}} + C_{\text{variable OH}} + C_{\text{allocated fixed OH}} - C_{\text{salvage}}$$

$$C_{\text{buy}} = C_{\text{purchase price}} + C_{\text{transport}} + C_{\text{inspection}} + C_{\text{inventory carry}}$$

### Break-Even Volume Analysis
If the buying price differs from making cost, the break-even point (in units) is:

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost to Make}}{\text{Variable Cost per unit (Buy)} - \text{Variable Cost per unit (Make)}}$$

If the denominator is negative (buying is cheaper at all volumes), then "Buy" is always preferred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Industry Trend**: Most auto and electronics companies follow the **80/20 rule** — they MAKE the 20% of components that are core/differentiating and BUY the 80% of standard parts. This is called **"Outsourcing the Non-Core, Insourcing the Core"**.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Value Equation | $V = F / C$ | VE: Increase V by increasing F or decreasing C |
| 2 | Preferred Number (Renard) | $R_n = 10^{(n/N)}$ | Standardization: Geometric step sizes |
| 3 | Make or Buy (Basic) | $C_{\text{buy}} < C_{\text{make}} \Rightarrow \text{BUY}$ | Strategic decision |
| 4 | Break-Even Volume | $Q_{BE} = \text{FC}_{\text{make}} \,/\, (\text{VC}_{\text{buy}} - \text{VC}_{\text{make}})$ | Find optimum production volume |
| 5 | Function Analysis (FAST) | "How-Why" linkage | VE: Identify critical functions |
| 6 | Cost Ratio (VE) | $\text{Cost Ratio} = \text{Cost} / \text{Worth}$ | VE: Identify improvement candidates |
| 7 | Economic Batch Quantity (related) | $EBQ = \sqrt{ \frac{2DS}{H} }$ | Production planning context |
| 8 | Standardization Variety Index | $\text{VR} = \text{No. of items used} \,/\, \text{No. of items designed}$ | IE metric for standard parts usage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the above formulas, especially **Value Equation, Make-or-Buy rule, and Renard Series** are **direct KTU board exam high-yield topics**. Memorize the formula, units, and one real-world example for each.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Value Engineering — Quantitative Function Analysis

**Scenario (KTU Style Numerical):** A component in a machine tool has the following data:
- Total manufacturing cost = ₹1,200
- Sales price = ₹2,000
- The component performs 2 functions: F1 (main function) and F2 (secondary function).
- Worth (Use Value) of F1 = ₹900
- Worth (Use Value) of F2 = ₹300
- Current cost split: F1 → ₹1,000; F2 → ₹200

**Step 1: Calculate the existing Value Index.**

The Value Index is defined as:

$$VI = \frac{\text{Function Worth (Use Value)}}{\text{Actual Cost}}$$

$$VI_{\text{current}} = \frac{900 + 300}{1000 + 200} = \frac{1200}{1200} = 1.00$$

Text logic: This means the component's cost exactly equals its worth — **no obvious waste, but no room for value addition**.

**Step 2: Apply a VE proposal.** R&D suggests a redesign that:
- Reduces cost of F1 from ₹1000 → ₹800.
- Maintains cost of F2 at ₹200.
- Worth remains ₹900 + ₹300 = ₹1200 (no loss of function).

$$VI_{\text{proposed}} = \frac{1200}{800 + 200} = \frac{1200}{1000} = 1.20$$

Text logic: Value Index increased from 1.00 to 1.20 — a **20% improvement in value**.

**Step 3: Calculate Cost Ratio (CR).**

$$CR = \frac{\text{Actual Cost}}{\text{Function Worth}}$$

$$CR_{\text{current}} = \frac{1200}{1200} = 1.00$$

$$CR_{\text{proposed}} = \frac{1000}{1200} = 0.833$$

Text logic: Cost Ratio < 1 means **cost is less than worth — good value**. < 0.85 is considered a strong candidate for adoption.

**Step 4: Calculate Annual Savings.**

If annual production is 5,000 units:

$$\text{Savings/unit} = 1200 - 1000 = \text{₹200}$$

$$\text{Annual Savings} = 200 \times 5000 = \text{₹}10,00,000$$

Text logic: A 16.67% cost reduction on a critical component yields ₹10 lakh annual savings — typically used in VE project sanction notes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Make or Buy Break-Even Analysis

**Scenario (KTU Style Numerical):** A firm needs 8,000 units/year of a component.

**Cost Data (Make):**
- Fixed cost to set up in-house production = ₹1,60,000/year.
- Variable cost per unit (material + labour) = ₹160/unit.

**Cost Data (Buy):**
- Purchase price = ₹190/unit.
- No additional fixed cost.
- Variable logistics cost = ₹10/unit (inspection, transport).

**Step 1: Calculate Total Cost of Make.**

$$C_{\text{make}}(Q) = FC_{\text{make}} + VC_{\text{make}} \times Q$$

$$C_{\text{make}}(8000) = 160000 + 160 \times 8000$$

$$C_{\text{make}}(8000) = 160000 + 1280000 = \text{₹}14,40,000$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Cost of Buy.**

$$C_{\text{buy}}(Q) = FC_{\text{buy}} + VC_{\text{buy}} \times Q$$

$$C_{\text{buy}}(8000) = 0 + (190 + 10) \times 8000 = 200 \times 8000 = \text{₹}16,00,000$$

**Step 3: Compare.**

$$C_{\text{make}} = 14,40,000 < C_{\text{buy}} = 16,00,000$$

Therefore: **MAKE** is preferred at this volume.

**Step 4: Calculate Break-Even Volume.**

Set $C_{\text{make}} = C_{\text{buy}}$:

$$160000 + 160 Q = 200 Q$$

$$160000 = 200 Q - 160 Q = 40 Q$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{160000}{40} = 4000 \text{ units}$$

Text logic: If annual demand is **above 4,000 units**, MAKE is cheaper. Below 4,000 units, BUY is cheaper. For our demand of 8,000 (> 4,000), MAKE wins.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Renard Preferred Number Series

**Step 1: Geometric progression base.**

For R10 series, $N = 10$. The ratio is:

$$r = 10^{1/N} = 10^{0.1} = 1.2589$$

**Step 2: Generate terms.**

$$R_0 = 10^{0/10} = 1.000$$

$$R_1 = 10^{1/10} = 1.259$$

$$R_2 = 10^{2/10} = 1.585$$

$$R_3 = 10^{3/10} = 1.995$$

$$R_4 = 10^{4/10} = 2.512$$

$$R_5 = 10^{5/10} = 3.162$$

$$R_6 = 10^{6/10} = 3.981$$

$$R_7 = 10^{7/10} = 5.012$$

$$R_8 = 10^{8/10} = 6.310$$

$$R_9 = 10^{9/10} = 7.943$$

$$R_{10} = 10^{10/10} = 10.000$$

Text logic: This gives 10 numbers from 1 to 10, each approximately **1.26 times** the previous. Standard dimensions are selected from this set to ensure **uniform proportional step sizes** — a foundation of standardization.

**Step 3: Application.** If a shaft is standardized in R20 (20 sizes from 10 to 100 mm), successive diameters will follow the geometric rule, ensuring **uniform variation** in strength and rigidity across the range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Comparison

| Decision Tool | Best Used When | Output | KTU Exam Favourite |
|---|---|---|---|
| VE Job Plan | Cost reduction in existing product | Function cost analysis & savings | ★★★★★ |
| Make/Buy Break-Even | Choosing between in-house and outsourced | Critical volume (Q_BE) | ★★★★★ |
| Renard Series | Standardizing sizes | Geometric size list | ★★★★ |
| Variety Reduction (Simplification) | Inventory and complexity issues | Reduced SKU count | ★★★★ |
| NPD Stage-Gate | New product introduction | Go/Kill decision at each gate | ★★★★ |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 New Product Development — Stage-Gate Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage1_IdeaGeneration] --> B[Gate1_Screening]
    B --> C[Stage2_ConceptDevelopment]
    C --> D[Gate2_ConceptTesting]
    D --> E[Stage3_BusinessAnalysis]
    E --> F[Gate3_ProjectApproval]
    F --> G[Stage4_ProductDevelopment]
    G --> H[Gate4_PrototypeReview]
    H --> I[Stage5_TestMarketing]
    I --> J[Gate5_LaunchDecision]
    J --> K[Stage6_Commercialization]
    K --> L[Stage7_PostLaunchReview]
    L --> M{NextAction}
    M -->|Improve| A
    M -->|Maintain| N[ProductInMarket]
    M -->|Decline| O[PhaseOut]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Stages** (rectangles) are activities where work is done.
- **Gates** (diamonds/rectangles with "Gate" prefix) are **Go / Kill / Recycle / Hold** decision points.
- This is the modern **Stage-Gate® Model** by Robert Cooper — it replaces the old linear 8-step model in advanced IE practice.
- For KTU 2024, students should know both: the **classical 8-step model** (for descriptive questions) and the **stage-gate concept** (for theory questions).

---

## 4.2 Process Planning Sequence — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ReceiveProductDrawing] --> P2[AnalyzeFeaturesAndTolerances]
    P2 --> P3[IdentifyManufacturingOperations]
    P3 --> P4[SelectMachinesAndTools]
    P4 --> P5[DetermineOperationSequence]
    P5 --> P6[CalculateSetupAndCycleTime]
    P6 --> P7[DefineInspectionPoints]
    P7 --> P8[GenerateRouteSheet]
    P8 --> P9{ProcessFeasible}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[ReleaseToProduction]
    P9 -->|No| P2
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Shows the **iterative nature of process planning** — feedback loop from feasibility check back to feature analysis.
- P8 (Route Sheet) is the **single output document** that travels to the shop floor along with the drawing.
- The feedback loop indicates that in real practice, planners revisit assumptions multiple times before finalizing.

---

## 4.3 Value Engineering — 7-Phase Job Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Phase1_Information_GatherData]
    V2[Phase2_FunctionAnalysis_DefineFunctionAndWorth]
    V3[Phase3_CreativePhase_BrainstormAlternatives]
    V4[Phase4_Evaluation_ScreenAndScore]
    V5[Phase5_Development_DetailBestIdea]
    V6[Phase6_Recommendation_PresentToManagement]
    V7[Phase7_Implementation_ExecuteAndMonitor]
    V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> V5 --> V6 --> V7
    V7 --> V8[CostSavingsAchieved]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Linear flow with a clear terminal outcome: **Cost Savings Achieved**.
- The bottleneck in real projects is usually **Phase 3 (Creative Phase)** — quantity and quality of ideas.
- Phase 2 (Function Analysis) is the **heart of VE** — the FAST diagram is built here.

---

## 4.4 FAST Diagram (Function Analysis System Technique) — Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[TransmitTorque] -->|How| F2[EngageGearTeeth]
    F2 -->|How| F3[ProvideHardenedSurface]
    F3 -->|How| F4[ApplyHeatTreatment]
    F1 -->|Why| F0[DriveLoad]
    F0 -->|Why| FG[OperateMachine]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **"How" questions** move from **left to right** (higher-level function → lower-level technical solution).
- **"Why" questions** move from **right to left** (low-level function → higher-level purpose).
- **Critical path** = leftmost function = the **basic function** the customer actually pays for.
- All functions to the **right of the critical path** are **supporting functions** — candidates for VE cost reduction.

---

## 4.5 Make or Buy Decision Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[DefineComponentOrService] --> M2[IdentifyCostDrivers]
    M2 --> M3[ComputeCostToMake]
    M2 --> M4[ComputeCostToBuy]
    M3 --> M5{CompareCosts}
    M4 --> M5
    M5 -->|Make Lower| M6[CheckQualitativeFactors]
    M5 -->|Buy Lower| M6
    M6 --> M7{StrategicFitOK}
    M7 -->|Yes| M8[FinalDecision_MakeOrBuy]
    M7 -->|No| M9[Reconsider]
    M9 --> M2
    M8 --> M10[MonitorAndReviewAnnually]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The diagram shows that **cost is necessary but not sufficient** — qualitative factors (IP, supplier risk, core competency) are critical.
- The final decision is **reviewed annually** because demand and supplier market conditions change.

---

## 4.6 Standardization & Simplification Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[IdentifyVarietyInProduct] --> S2[GroupSimilarItems]
    S2 --> S3[DefineCommonStandard]
    S3 --> S4[EstablishPreferredNumbers]
    S4 --> S5[ReduceVariety_Simplification]
    S5 --> S6[StandardComponents]
    S6 --> S7[LowerCostHigherQuality]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Standardization** sets the rule; **Simplification** applies the rule by removing variety.
- The output is **standardized, simplified components** that deliver **lower cost and higher quality** — the ultimate IE goal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define New Product Development (NPD). List any four reasons why organizations undertake NPD.**

**Model Answer:**
**Definition:** New Product Development is the systematic process of transforming a market opportunity into a product that is available for sale, encompassing idea generation, screening, concept development, business analysis, product development, test marketing, and commercialization.

**Four Reasons for NPD:**
1. **Changing customer needs** — To meet evolving consumer preferences and remain relevant.
2. **Competitive pressure** — To maintain or grow market share in the face of rivals.
3. **Technological advancement** — To leverage new technology (e.g., AI, EV, IoT) for innovative offerings.
4. **Decline of existing products** — To replace products that have reached the maturity or decline stage of their life cycle and replenish the revenue stream.

*(Other valid reasons: Diversification, better utilization of resources, government regulations, brand image.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Value Engineering? State the Value Equation and explain how value can be increased.**

**Model Answer:**
**Definition:** Value Engineering (VE), as defined by Lawrence D. Miles, is a systematic, function-oriented technique that identifies the function of a product or service, establishes a value for that function, and provides the necessary function reliably at the lowest overall cost.

**Value Equation:**

$$V = \frac{F}{C}$$

where $V$ = Value, $F$ = Function (Worth), $C$ = Cost.

**How to Increase Value:**
1. **Increase function** ($F$ increases) with same cost.
2. **Decrease cost** ($C$ decreases) with same function.
3. **Increase function AND decrease cost** — the ideal VE outcome.
4. **Small decrease in function with much larger decrease in cost** — when over-engineering exists.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **8 classical steps of New Product Development** with a suitable flow diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **Value Engineering Job Plan** as proposed by Lawrence Miles. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The 8 classical steps of NPD are:

1. **Idea Generation**: Multiple sources (customers, R&D, employees, competitors) generate a large pool of raw ideas.

2. **Idea Screening**: Unviable ideas are filtered using criteria such as market potential, technical feasibility, and strategic fit. The **R-W-W (Real Win Worth)** filter is often used.

3. **Concept Development and Testing**: Surviving ideas are developed into detailed product concepts and tested with target consumers through focus groups and surveys.

4. **Business/Marketing Strategy**: A preliminary marketing strategy is developed covering the 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion).

5. **Product and Process Development**: R&D builds a working prototype; manufacturing develops the production process; finance estimates cost and ROI.

6. **Test Marketing**: The product is launched in a small representative geographic area (e.g., one city) to gauge real customer response.

7. **Commercialization**: Full-scale launch with mass production, distribution build-up, and intensive marketing.

8. **Post-Launch Review**: Continuous monitoring of sales, customer satisfaction, and product performance; feeding improvements back into the next generation.

**Flow Diagram** (refer to SECTION 4.1 stage-gate figure above — students should reproduce a linear 8-step box diagram with arrows).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Lawrence Miles' 7-Phase Job Plan:**

| Phase | Activity |
|---|---|
| 1. Information | Collect all data — drawings, costs, customer requirements, manufacturing process. |
| 2. Function Analysis | Define every function using two-word verb-noun form (e.g., "transmit torque"). Distinguish **use functions** (basic) from **aesthetic functions**. Build a FAST diagram. |
| 3. Creative | Brainstorm alternative ways to perform each function. Aim for quantity of ideas; defer judgment. |
| 4. Evaluation | Screen and rate alternatives on cost, feasibility, and reliability. Eliminate weak ideas. |
| 5. Development | Take the best alternative(s) and develop a detailed engineering and cost proposal. |
| 6. Recommendation | Present to management with a clear cost-benefit analysis for approval. |
| 7. Implementation | Execute the approved change; monitor cost savings, quality, and performance against baseline. |

The outcome is a **structured, repeatable framework** that ensures cost reduction is achieved **without sacrificing function or quality** — the central philosophy of VE.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is **Standardization**? Explain its various types with examples. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A firm needs 8,000 units/year of a component. Cost data is as follows: **Make** — Fixed Cost = ₹1,60,000, Variable Cost = ₹160/unit. **Buy** — Variable Cost (purchase + logistics) = ₹200/unit. Determine the **Make or Buy decision** and the **break-even volume**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Standardization is the process of establishing and applying standards — rules, guidelines, or characteristics — for products, parts, processes, and procedures, with the objective of achieving uniformity, interchangeability, and economic efficiency.

**Types of Standardization:**

1. **Dimensional Standardization** — Standard sizes (e.g., M10, M12 bolts; IS standard pipe diameters).
2. **Material Standardization** — Specification of raw materials (e.g., IS 2062 for structural steel, SS 304 for stainless steel).
3. **Parts Standardization** — Common parts used across product variants (e.g., same headlight in two car models).
4. **Process Standardization** — Standard operating procedures (e.g., ISO 9001, Six Sigma procedures).
5. **Performance Standardization** — Output norms (e.g., BEE 5-star rating for ACs, IS ratings for cement).
6. **Quality Standardization** — Acceptable quality levels (e.g., AQL 1.0% in sampling inspection).
7. **Terminology Standardization** — Uniform vocabulary (e.g., BIS, ISO technical glossaries).
8. **Design Standardization** — Standard product designs (e.g., modular furniture, LEGO bricks).

**Advantages:**
- Reduces variety → lower inventory, simpler purchasing.
- Enables mass production → lower unit cost.
- Improves interchangeability → easier maintenance and repair.
- Improves quality through uniform benchmarks.
- Simplifies training and shop-floor operations.

**Disadvantages:**
- Reduces design flexibility and innovation.
- May not satisfy all customer-specific needs.
- Risk of design lock-in to outdated standards.
- Initial setup of standards is costly and time-consuming.
- Reduced product differentiation in the market.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Annual demand, $D = 8000$ units.
- $FC_{\text{make}} = \text{₹}1,60,000$; $VC_{\text{make}} = \text{₹}160/\text{unit}$.
- $FC_{\text{buy}} = 0$; $VC_{\text{buy}} = \text{₹}200/\text{unit}$ (purchase + transport + inspection).

**Step 1: Total Cost of Make at Q = 8000.**

$$C_{\text{make}} = 160000 + 160 \times 8000$$

$$C_{\text{make}} = 160000 + 1280000 = \text{₹}14,40,000$$

**Step 2: Total Cost of Buy at Q = 8000.**

$$C_{\text{buy}} = 0 + 200 \times 8000 = \text{₹}16,00,000$$

**Step 3: Compare.**

$$C_{\text{make}} = 14,40,000 < C_{\text{buy}} = 16,00,000$$

**Decision: MAKE** is more economical at this volume. **Savings = ₹1,60,000/year**.

**Step 4: Break-Even Volume.**

Set $C_{\text{make}} = C_{\text{buy}}$:

$$160000 + 160 Q = 200 Q$$

$$160000 = 40 Q$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{160000}{40} = 4000 \text{ units}$$

**Interpretation:** For demand **> 4,000 units/year**, MAKE is cheaper; for demand **< 4,000 units/year**, BUY is cheaper. Since current demand (8,000) > 4,000, MAKE is the recommended decision.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
>
> 1. **In NPD questions**: Students often **write all 8 steps in one paragraph** without numbering or headings — KTU examiners deduct **up to 2 marks** for poor structure. Always use **numbered headings** and a **diagram**.
>
> 2. **In VE questions**: A common mistake is to write **"Value = Cost / Function"** — this is **WRONG**. The correct equation is $V = F/C$. Confusing these **costs 1 full mark**.
>
> 3. **In Make/Buy questions**: Students often forget to include **variable logistics cost in the buy option** — if the question states "logistics cost ₹10/unit", it **must be added to VC_buy**. Skipping this loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **In Standardization questions**: Many students list only 2–3 types and stop. KTU expects **at least 5 types** for full marks.
>
> 5. **In Process Planning questions**: Always mention the **Route Sheet** as the final output document — failing to do so loses a mark.
>
> 6. **Currency & Units**: Always write **₹** before numerical values; do not write "160000" without a unit. KTU deducts marks for sloppy unit notation.
>
> 7. **Internal Choice**: When given "either/or", write the **full question number and label** clearly (e.g., "11(a)" or "11(b)") — wrong numbering can lead to the examiner missing your answer.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ✅ New Product Development
- [ ] 8 classical steps: Idea Gen → Screening → Concept Dev → Strategy → Product Dev → Test Marketing → Commercialization → Post-Launch.
- [ ] Modern approach: **Stage-Gate** (with Go/Kill/Recycle decisions).
- [ ] Failure rate: 7 out of 8 ideas fail → systematic process is essential.
- [ ] Three layers: Strategic, Tactical, Operational NPD.

### ✅ Process Planning and Design
- [ ] Final output: **Route Sheet** (operation-wise document).
- [ ] Process Planning = **WHAT to do**; Process Design = **WHERE and WITH WHAT** to do it.
- [ ] Types: Job Shop, Batch, Mass, Continuous, Project.
- [ ] Process Planning steps: Analyze → List operations → Select machines → Sequence → Time standards → Inspection.

### ✅ Value Engineering
- [ ] **Definition** by Lawrence Miles (1947, GE).
- [ ] **Value Equation**: $V = F/C$.
- [ ] **7-Phase Job Plan**: Info → Function Analysis → Creative → Evaluation → Development → Recommendation → Implementation.
- [ ] Function written in **two-word verb-noun** format.
- [ ] **FAST diagram**: How (L→R), Why (R→L).
- [ ] **VE Tests mnemonic**: SPECS (idea, present, cost, time, space).

### ✅ Standardization
- [ ] Definition: Establishing and applying standards for uniformity and economy.
- [ ] Types: Dimensional, Material, Parts, Process, Performance, Quality, Terminology, Design.
- [ ] Organizations: BIS, ISO, ASTM, DIN, JIS.
- [ ] Both advantages (cost, quality, interchangeability) and disadvantages (loss of flexibility).

### ✅ Simplification
- [ ] Reduction of variety in products, parts, and processes.
- [ ] **Preferred Number (Renard) Series**: $R_n = 10^{(n/N)}$.
- [ ] R5, R10, R20, R40 — geometric progression with ratio $10^{1/N}$.
- [ ] Closely linked to standardization; simplification is the *act*, standardization is the *rule*.

### ✅ Make or Buy Decision
- [ ] Decision rule: BUY if $C_{\text{buy}} < C_{\text{make}}$.
- [ ] **Break-even volume formula**: $Q_{BE} = FC_{\text{make}} \,/\, (VC_{\text{buy}} - VC_{\text{make}})$.
- [ ] Quantitative factors (cost) AND qualitative factors (IP, strategy, supplier risk) — both must be evaluated.
- [ ] Modern trend: **80/20 Rule** — Make core 20%, Buy standard 80%.
- [ ] Decision must be reviewed **annually** (demand and supply markets change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: When you see a question on this module, **always start with a labelled diagram** (Stage-Gate, FAST, or Make/Buy flow), then follow with **numbered headings** and **clear units**. A well-structured 7-mark answer with diagram + bullets will outscore a paragraph-style 14-mark answer without structure. **Time management: 12 minutes for 7-mark question, 25 minutes for 14-mark question.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ergonomics in Product Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ergonomics in Product Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ergonomics (Human Factors Engineering)** is the scientific discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among humans and other elements of a system, and the profession that applies theory, principles, data, and methods to design in order to optimise human well-being and overall system performance.

In the context of **Product Design**, ergonomics is the systematic application of knowledge about human physical, cognitive, and sensory capabilities to the design of products so that they:
- **Fit the user** (anthropometric compatibility)
- **Reduce fatigue, injury, and discomfort** (biomechanical safety)
- **Enhance usability and productivity** (cognitive and perceptual fit)
- **Improve user satisfaction and aesthetics** (affective/emotional ergonomics)

The International Ergonomics Association (IEA) categorises ergonomics into three broad domains:

| Domain | Focus Area | Product Design Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Ergonomics** | Body posture, biomechanics, anthropometry | Chair height, tool grip, handle diameter |
| **Cognitive Ergonomics** | Mental workload, perception, decision-making | Dashboard layout, warning symbols |
| **Organisational Ergonomics** | Work scheduling, teamwork, communication | Workflow-integrated product features |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spoon That Hurts"

Imagine a child trying to eat soup with an adult-sized steel serving spoon. The handle is too long, the bowl too deep, and the metal too heavy. The child struggles, spills, and eventually refuses to eat. Now imagine giving the same child a small, lightweight, colour-coded spoon with a soft silicone grip. The meal becomes effortless.

That transformation — from **adult-shaped object** to **human-shaped object** — is exactly what **Ergonomics in Product Design** accomplishes at scale. Whether it is a spoon, a steering wheel, a stethoscope, or a CNC control panel, the design philosophy shifts from *"what can the product do?"* to *"who will use the product, and how does their body and mind actually work?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Ergonomics is NOT about making a product "comfortable" alone. It is the engineering discipline that mathematically maps human body dimensions, muscle forces, joint ranges, reaction times, and decision patterns into the geometry, controls, displays, and feedback mechanisms of a product.

---

## 1.3 Why Ergonomics in Product Design is a KTU High-Priority Topic

Modern industry views poor ergonomics as a direct contributor to:
- **Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs)** — back pain, carpal tunnel syndrome, tendinitis
- **Lost workday injuries** (OSHA reports MSDs account for ~30% of all workplace injuries)
- **Product recalls and warranty costs** due to grip failure or unintended use
- **Cognitive errors** in safety-critical products (medical devices, vehicles)

Hence, KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists ergonomics under Industrial Engineering with emphasis on **anthropometric data application, design of controls/displays, and human-machine system integration**.

> [!TIP]
> **Three Golden Questions** every ergonomic product must answer:
> 1. *Can the user reach it, hold it, and operate it without strain?*
> 2. *Can the user understand what it is telling them within their reaction time?*
> 3. *Will the user want to use it again tomorrow?*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ergonomic Product Design Framework

A rigorous ergonomic design process follows a **user-centred iterative cycle**, generally described by **ISO 9241-210 (Human-Centred Design)**:

1. **Context of Use Analysis** — Identify users, tasks, equipment, and environments.
2. **User & Task Requirements Specification** — Translate needs into measurable design constraints.
3. **Anthropometric Data Selection** — Pick body dimensions and percentile ranges.
4. **Design Solution Generation** — Produce multiple concepts respecting constraints.
5. **Prototype & Usability Evaluation** — Test with real users, measure errors and time.
6. **Iteration Until Acceptance Criteria Are Met** — Refine and re-test.

---

## 2.2 Anthropometry — The Science of Human Body Measurement

**Anthropometry** is the cornerstone of physical ergonomics. It deals with the measurement of static (standing/seated dimensions) and dynamic (range of motion, reach envelopes) body parameters.

### 2.2.1 Percentile Concept

Human body dimensions follow an approximately **normal distribution**. Designers cannot build a product for "the average person" because the average hand cannot be held by 50% of users.

- **5th Percentile (P5)** — Small female / youth dimension. Used for **minimum reach** and **minimum clearance** designs (e.g., reach to a pedal, glove compartment latch).
- **50th Percentile (P50)** — Median dimension. Used for **average fit**.
- **95th Percentile (P95)** — Large male dimension. Used for **maximum clearance** designs (e.g., door width, cockpit width).

### 2.2.2 Types of Anthropometric Data

| Type | Description | Example in Product Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Static** | Body measurements in fixed posture | Stature, sitting height, hand length |
| **Dynamic** | Measurements during motion | Forward reach envelope, shoulder flexion angle |
| **Functional** | Measurements during task performance | Grip diameter inside a functional hand |

### 2.2.3 Percentile Calculation Formula

Given a body dimension dataset with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ (assumed normal), the k-th percentile is computed as:

$$P_k = \mu + (Z_k \times \sigma)$$

where $Z_k$ is the standard normal deviate corresponding to percentile $k$:

| Percentile $k$ | 1 | 2.5 | 5 | 50 | 95 | 97.5 | 99 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Z_k$ | -2.33 | -1.96 | -1.645 | 0 | +1.645 | +1.96 | +2.33 |

For small samples, use **percentile rank formula**:

$$P = \frac{(N+1) \times k}{100}$$

with linear interpolation between adjacent ordered values.

---

## 2.3 Biomechanics in Product Design

Biomechanics applies mechanics (statics, dynamics, strength of materials) to the human body. Key concepts:

- **Lever systems** of the body: most skeletal muscles operate at mechanical disadvantage (low mechanical advantage).
- **Posture analysis** using tools like **RULA** (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment) and **REBA** (Rapid Entire Body Assessment).
- **NIOSH Lifting Equation** for manual material handling tasks.

### 2.3.1 Static Biomechanics — Joint Torque

When a person holds a load at a given joint, the torque exerted at the joint is:

$$T_j = F_{load} \times d_{load} - F_{muscle} \times d_{muscle}$$

In simplified form for a single-joint system:

$$T_j = F \times d$$

where:
- $F$ = applied force (N)
- $d$ = moment arm from joint centre to force line of action (m)

### 2.3.2 NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991)

The **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** under ideal conditions is **23 kg**. For non-ideal conditions:

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

| Multiplier | Description | Range |
|---|---|---|
| $LC$ | Load Constant | **23 kg** |
| $HM$ | Horizontal Multiplier | 0 (H > 63 cm) to 1 (H ≤ 25 cm) |
| $VM$ | Vertical Multiplier | 0 (V > 175 cm) to 1 (V ≈ 75 cm) |
| $DM$ | Distance Multiplier | 0 (D > 175 cm) to 1 (D ≤ 25 cm) |
| $AM$ | Asymmetry Multiplier | 0 (A > 135°) to 1 (A = 0°) |
| $FM$ | Frequency Multiplier | 0.12 to 1.00 |
| $CM$ | Coupling Multiplier | 0.90 to 1.00 |

The **Lifting Index (LI)** indicates risk:

$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL}$$

- $LI \leq 1.0$ → Acceptable for most workers
- $LI > 1.0$ → Increased risk; redesign task or product
- $LI > 3.0$ → Unacceptable; engineering controls mandatory

### 2.3.3 RULA Scoring (Conceptual)

RULA produces a **Grand Score from 1 to 7**, indicating required action level:

| Grand Score | Risk Level | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1–2 | Negligible | Acceptable |
| 3–4 | Low | May need change |
| 5–6 | Medium | Change soon |
| 7 | Very High | Change immediately |

---

## 2.4 Cognitive Ergonomics in Product Design

Cognitive ergonomics governs how users **perceive, think, and decide**. Key parameters:

| Parameter | Typical Adult Value | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Reaction Time (simple) | 0.2–0.3 s | Minimum signal duration |
| Reaction Time (choice) | 0.5–0.7 s | Decision-aid requirements |
| Visual Acuity | 1 arc-minute (20/20) | Display character size |
| Auditory Threshold | 0 dB SPL | Alarm loudness design |
| Short-term Memory | 7 ± 2 items | Menu depth limitation |
| Fitts' Law Time | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(1 + \frac{D}{W})$ | Button size & spacing |

### 2.4.1 Fitts' Law

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

where $D$ is distance to target and $W$ is target width. Implies: **bigger, closer targets are faster to acquire** — directly informs button placement in product design.

### 2.4.2 Signal Detection Theory (Concept)

$$d' = Z(H) - Z(F)$$

where $d'$ is sensitivity, $H$ is hit rate, $F$ is false-alarm rate. Higher $d'$ means better signal discrimination — guides alarm and indicator design.

---

## 2.5 Principles of Ergonomic Product Design

Drawing from **Nielsen's Heuristics, Don Norman's Affordances, and ISO 9241**:

1. **Visibility of System Status** — Feedback within 0.1 s for tactile, 1 s for cognitive.
2. **Match Between System and Real World** — Use familiar icons (skull = poison).
3. **User Control and Freedom** — Undo functions, escape routes.
4. **Consistency and Standards** — Same colour for same function throughout.
5. **Error Prevention** — Design out the possibility of error (e.g., asymmetric USB).
6. **Recognition Rather Than Recall** — Display options, don't make users memorise.
7. **Flexibility and Efficiency** — Allow expert shortcuts.
8. **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design** — Avoid irrelevant information.
9. **Help Users Recognise, Diagnose, Recover from Errors** — Plain language messages.
10. **Help and Documentation** — Concise, searchable, task-oriented.

### 2.5.1 Anthropometric Design Rules of Thumb

| Design Element | Recommended Approach |
|---|---|
| **Handle diameter (tools)** | 30–50 mm; grip length ≥ 100 mm |
| **Push button diameter** | 10–25 mm |
| **Toggle switch length** | 50–100 mm |
| **Display-to-eye distance** | 500–700 mm |
| **Character height** | $H = \frac{D \times \theta}{3438}$ (D in mm, $\theta$ in arc-min) |
| **Maximum reach (forward)** | Use **P5** stature minus 100 mm |
| **Minimum clearance** | Use **P95** body breadth |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers ~90% of numerical questions on Ergonomics in Product Design.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Standard Values / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Percentile (Normal dist.) | $P_k = \mu + Z_k \cdot \sigma$ | $Z_{95} = 1.645$ |
| 2 | Percentile Rank | $P = \frac{(N+1)k}{100}$ | Linear interp. |
| 3 | Joint Torque | $T_j = F \cdot d$ | $F$ in N, $d$ in m, $T$ in N·m |
| 4 | Muscle Mechanical Advantage | $MA = \frac{d_{muscle}}{d_{load}}$ | Usually $< 0.1$ |
| 5 | NIOSH RWL | $RWL = 23 \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | in kg |
| 6 | Lifting Index | $LI = L/RWL$ | $> 1$ indicates risk |
| 7 | Fitts' Law Time | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ | $a,b$ are empirical constants |
| 8 | Character Height | $H = D \cdot \theta / 3438$ | $H$ in mm, $D$ in mm, $\theta$ in arc-min |
| 9 | Visual Angle | $\theta = 2 \arctan(H / 2D)$ | in radians → convert to arc-min |
| 10 | SDT Sensitivity | $d' = Z(H) - Z(F)$ | Higher = better |
| 11 | RULA Grand Score | Sum of posture tables A & B | Range 1–7 |
| 12 | Energy Expenditure (approx) | $EE = 1.5 \cdot BM + 2.0 \cdot M$ | kcal/min; $BM$ basal, $M$ work |
| 13 | Reaction Time (Hick's Law) | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $N$ = number of choices |
| 14 | Hand Force (Age-adjusted) | $F_{adj} = F_{20} \cdot (1 - 0.01 \cdot (Age - 20))$ | Grip strength decline |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Ergonomic Product Design Intervention |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Adjustable pedals, lumbar support, reach-adapted steering |
| **Medical Devices** | Surgical instrument grip (pencil vs. pistol), haptic feedback |
| **Consumer Electronics** | One-handed phone reach, thumb-friendly touch zones |
| **Industrial Tools** | Anti-vibration gloves, balanced power tools (reduce MSD) |
| **Furniture** | Sit-stand desks, contoured chairs with lumbar curve |
| **Aerospace** | G-force tolerant seating, multi-modal warning displays |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, you can score high marks by **linking every formula to a product design decision**. Always state: *"This formula is used to size the handle so that 95% of users can grip comfortably..."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Percentile-Based Dimension Selection for Product Design

### Problem Setup

The standing elbow height of Indian male operators is reported as **normally distributed** with:
- Mean $\mu = 105$ cm
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 6$ cm

A workbench is to be designed. Determine the bench height such that:
- 95% of users can rest their elbows comfortably at bench top.
- 5% of users (smallest stature) can also reach it.

---

### Step 1: Identify the Design Requirement

To accommodate the **maximum comfortable height** for 95% of users, we use the **P95** value of elbow height.
To accommodate the **minimum reachable height** for 5% of users, we use the **P5** value.

The bench height is conventionally set **5–10 cm below the elbow** for precision work, and **0–5 cm below** for heavy work.

For this design, take bench top = elbow height **− 5 cm** for general assembly.

---

### Step 2: Compute Z-values from Standard Normal Table

From the standard normal distribution table:

$$Z_{95} = +1.645$$

$$Z_{5} = -1.645$$

---

### Step 3: Apply Percentile Formula

$$P_k = \mu + (Z_k \times \sigma)$$

For $k = 95$:

$$P_{95} = 105 + (1.645 \times 6)$$

$$P_{95} = 105 + 9.87$$

$$P_{95} = 114.87 \text{ cm}$$

For $k = 5$:

$$P_{5} = 105 + (-1.645 \times 6)$$

$$P_{5} = 105 - 9.87$$

$$P_{5} = 95.13 \text{ cm}$$

---

### Step 4: Determine Design Bench Height Range

Maximum elbow height (P95) = **114.87 cm**
Minimum elbow height (P5) = **95.13 cm**

Workbench height for **upper bound** (P95 elbow − 5 cm):

$$H_{max} = 114.87 - 5 = 109.87 \text{ cm}$$

Workbench height for **lower bound** (P5 elbow − 5 cm):

$$H_{min} = 95.13 - 5 = 90.13 \text{ cm}$$

---

### Step 5: Final Design Selection

A single fixed bench cannot satisfy both extremes. **Engineering Recommendation:**

> Design an **adjustable workbench** with height range **90 cm to 110 cm**, with detents every 5 cm. This satisfies the full user population from the 5th to 95th percentile.

Alternatively, if fixed design is mandatory, choose the **median-based optimum**:

$$H_{design} = P_{50} - 5 = 105 - 5 = 100 \text{ cm}$$

This accommodates approximately 50% of users exactly, with the remaining 50% within ±10 cm deviation — still acceptable for most assembly tasks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NIOSH Lifting Equation Application

### Problem Statement

A worker lifts a **load of 18 kg** from a shelf. The lifting parameters are:
- Horizontal distance $H = 40$ cm
- Vertical origin $V = 75$ cm
- Vertical destination $V' = 125$ cm
- Asymmetry angle $A = 30°$
- Frequency = 1 lift per minute, duration < 1 hour
- Coupling = Fair

Determine if this lifting task is acceptable.

---

### Step 1: Compute Vertical Travel Distance

$$D = \vert V' - V \vert = \vert 125 - 75 \vert = 50 \text{ cm}$$

---

### Step 2: Compute Individual Multipliers

#### Horizontal Multiplier (HM)

$$HM = \frac{25}{H}$$

$$HM = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

#### Vertical Multiplier (VM)

$$VM = 1 - \frac{\vert V - 75 \vert}{75}$$

For V = 75 cm (at reference point):

$$VM = 1 - \frac{\vert 75 - 75 \vert}{75} = 1.000$$

#### Distance Multiplier (DM)

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D}$$

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{50} = 0.82 + 0.09 = 0.91$$

#### Asymmetry Multiplier (AM)

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$$

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904$$

#### Frequency Multiplier (FM)

For 1 lift/min, duration < 1 hour, the **NIOSH table** gives $FM = 0.94$ (for V < 75 cm reference; here assume close enough).

$$FM = 0.94$$

#### Coupling Multiplier (CM)

Coupling = Fair (hand-hold possible but not optimal):

$$CM = 0.95$$

---

### Step 3: Compute Recommended Weight Limit

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 \times 1.000 \times 0.91 \times 0.904 \times 0.94 \times 0.95$$

$$RWL = 23 \times (0.625 \times 1.000 \times 0.91 \times 0.904 \times 0.94 \times 0.95)$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.4574$$

$$RWL \approx 10.52 \text{ kg}$$

---

### Step 4: Compute Lifting Index

$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{18}{10.52} = 1.711$$

---

### Step 5: Interpret the Result

| LI Range | Interpretation | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|
| $\leq 1.0$ | Acceptable | No action |
| 1.0 – 1.5 | Marginal | Monitor; consider improvements |
| 1.5 – 2.0 | **Increased Risk** | **Redesign required** ← **We are here (1.711)** |
| 2.0 – 3.0 | High Risk | Engineering controls mandatory |
| $> 3.0$ | Unacceptable | Immediate task redesign |

---

### Step 6: Ergonomic Redesign Recommendation

To reduce LI to ≤ 1.0, options include:
1. **Reduce load** to ≤ 10.5 kg.
2. **Add handles** (Coupling = Good → $CM = 1.0$).
3. **Bring origin closer** ($H \leq 25$ cm) → $HM$ rises.
4. **Eliminate asymmetry** by repositioning shelf.
5. **Use mechanical lift assist** (eliminate manual lift entirely).

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem** — practice writing out every multiplier's derivation explicitly. Marks are awarded for showing each formula substitution.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fitts' Law Application for Touch Interface Design

### Problem Setup

A touchscreen interface has icons arranged in a grid. The **distance between centres** is $D = 20$ mm and the **icon width** is $W = 10$ mm. Empirical constants are $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit.

Find:
1. Index of difficulty $ID$
2. Movement time $T$
3. New $T$ if icon size doubles

---

### Step 1: Index of Difficulty

$$ID = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

$$ID = \log_2\left(\frac{20}{10} + 1\right) = \log_2(3) \approx 1.585 \text{ bits}$$

---

### Step 2: Movement Time

$$T = a + b \times ID$$

$$T = 50 + 150 \times 1.585$$

$$T = 50 + 237.75 = 287.75 \text{ ms}$$

---

### Step 3: If Icon Doubles ($W' = 20$ mm)

$$ID' = \log_2\left(\frac{20}{20} + 1\right) = \log_2(2) = 1 \text{ bit}$$

$$T' = 50 + 150 \times 1 = 200 \text{ ms}$$

**Time saved per touch:** $287.75 - 200 = 87.75$ ms.

---

### Step 4: Engineering Insight

A **30% reduction in selection time** is achieved by doubling icon size without changing layout. For a user making 100 touches/hour, this saves ~8.8 seconds/hour — significant for high-volume tasks. Justifies investment in larger UI elements on industrial HMIs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Required Character Height for Display

### Problem Setup

An instrument panel display is to be read at a viewing distance of $D = 700$ mm. The minimum visual acuity required is $\theta = 5$ arc-minutes (default industrial standard for legible digits).

Find the minimum character height $H$.

---

### Step 1: Apply the Visual Angle Formula

$$H = \frac{D \times \theta}{3438}$$

$$H = \frac{700 \times 5}{3438}$$

$$H = \frac{3500}{3438} \approx 1.018 \text{ mm}$$

---

### Step 2: Apply Safety Factor

Industry practice: **add 50% safety margin** for vibration and lighting variability.

$$H_{design} = 1.018 \times 1.5 \approx 1.53 \text{ mm}$$

Round up to **2 mm character height** for the actual product display.

---

### Step 3: Engineering Decision

> [!TIP]
> Many low-cost consumer products use 1 mm characters at 700 mm distance. Under stress or vibration, legibility fails — a documented cause of industrial accidents. KTU expects you to **justify design margins**, not just compute raw values.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Types

| Problem Type | Key Formulae Used | Marks Allocation Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Anthropometric bench design** | Percentile formula, range table | 7 marks |
| **NIOSH Lifting risk** | RWL, LI, multipliers | 7 marks |
| **Handle/control sizing** | Percentile + grip diameter table | 3–4 marks |
| **Display legibility** | Visual angle formula | 3–4 marks |
| **Cognitive task time** | Fitts' Law / Hick's Law | 3 marks |
| **Posture scoring** | RULA / REBA table lookup | 4–5 marks |
| **Work-rest cycle** | Energy expenditure, fatigue curves | 3 marks |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> Define **anthropometry**. Explain the difference between **5th percentile** and **95th percentile** design with one product example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Anthropometry is the branch of ergonomics that deals with the systematic measurement of human body dimensions, including static, dynamic, and functional measures, to provide data for product and workspace design.

**P5 Design (1 mark):**
The 5th percentile dimension represents the small-stature end of the population. It is used when the design must be reachable or operable by the smallest users. *Example:* The reach distance to a car's accelerator pedal is designed for the P5 female leg length so that even short-stature drivers can comfortably operate it.

**P95 Design (1 mark):**
The 95th percentile dimension represents the large-stature end. It is used when the design must accommodate the largest body size without interference. *Example:* The width of an aircraft seat or a car door aperture is designed using P95 hip breadth / shoulder width so that the largest user can fit without discomfort.

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> What is the **NIOSH Lifting Equation**? State its six multipliers and the recommended weight limit under ideal conditions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **NIOSH Lifting Equation** (1991) is an ergonomic risk-assessment tool used to evaluate manual lifting tasks in workplaces and to design safer lifting products.

**Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) under ideal conditions = 23 kg (1 mark)**

**Six Multipliers (2 marks — any 3 named with description acceptable):**
- **HM** — Horizontal Multiplier
- **VM** — Vertical Multiplier
- **DM** — Distance Multiplier
- **AM** — Asymmetry Multiplier
- **FM** — Frequency Multiplier
- **CM** — Coupling Multiplier

**Formula:** $RWL = 23 \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully**
> For **anthropometric design problems**, students often lose 2–3 marks by:
> 1. Using **mean (P50)** for all dimensions without justifying why.
> 2. Forgetting to **convert units** (cm to m, kg to N).
> 3. **Not stating the design recommendation** at the end (e.g., "Bench should be adjustable because fixed height fails P5 / P95").
> 4. Confusing **P5** (small) with **P95** (large). Memorise: **P5 = small end, P95 = large end.**
> For **NIOSH problems**, students lose marks by:
> 1. **Skipping multiplier derivation** — always show the numerical substitution for each.
> 2. Using the **wrong HM formula** — for V outside 75 cm range, use $1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$, not $\frac{25}{H}$.
> 3. Failing to provide **engineering redesign recommendations** even when LI > 1.

---

### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Define ergonomics. Explain with a **block diagram** how the human-machine-environment system interacts in the context of an **automotive driver cockpit**. List at least four ergonomic design interventions used in modern cars. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The **stature** of Indian male workers is normally distributed with mean $\mu = 167$ cm and standard deviation $\sigma = 7$ cm. Design a **doorway height** such that:
- 95% of users can pass without ducking.
- The minimum clearance also accommodates the 5th percentile user with safety margin.

Compute the recommended door height. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Ergonomics is the scientific discipline that studies human capabilities and limitations and applies this knowledge to design products, systems, and environments that optimise human performance, safety, comfort, and well-being.

**Block Diagram (3 marks) — Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the general Human-Machine-Environment framework. For the automotive cockpit specifically:**

- **Human Input Layer:** Driver's anthropometry (P5/P95 stature), reach envelope, vision field.
- **Cockpit Interface:** Steering wheel (P5 grip diameter 32 mm), pedals (P95 foot length reach), seat (P5 to P95 adjustability).
- **Cockpit Internal System:** Vehicle dynamics, electronic control units, sensor fusion.
- **Environment:** Road illumination, ambient temperature, cabin noise.

**Four Ergonomic Interventions (2 marks):**
1. **Power-adjustable seat** with memory function (accommodates multiple drivers).
2. **Reach-and-rake adjustable steering column** for optimal hand position.
3. **Heads-up display (HUD)** projecting speed into driver's line of sight.
4. **Lumbo-pelvic seat contour** matched to P50 spine curvature.
5. (Bonus) **Climate-controlled zones** allowing individual thermal preference.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Identify Z-values (1 mark)**

$$Z_{95} = +1.645, \quad Z_{5} = -1.645$$

**Step 2: Compute P95 Stature (2 marks)**

$$P_{95} = \mu + Z_{95} \times \sigma = 167 + (1.645 \times 7)$$

$$P_{95} = 167 + 11.515 = 178.515 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Compute P5 Stature (1 mark)**

$$P_{5} = 167 + (-1.645 \times 7) = 167 - 11.515 = 155.485 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Determine Door Height (2 marks)**

For a doorway, design must accommodate the **tallest user without ducking**. Recommended clearance over head height = **+10 cm safety margin** (for shoes, helmet, hair).

$$H_{door} = P_{95} + 10 = 178.515 + 10 = 188.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5: Verify against P5 (1 mark)**

Even at P5 (155.5 cm), the door height (188.5 cm) is well above the head — no interference. Clearance for the shortest user:

$$\Delta H = 188.5 - 155.5 = 33 \text{ cm (ample)}$$

**Final Design Recommendation:**
$$\boxed{H_{door} \geq 188.5 \text{ cm, standardise at } 190 \text{ cm}}$$

---

### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain **RULA** (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment) with its grand score interpretation. How is RULA used in ergonomic redesign of a **hand-tool product**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker performs **manual lifting** on a packaging line. Lifting parameters are:
- Load $L = 15$ kg
- Horizontal distance $H = 30$ cm
- Vertical lift origin $V = 50$ cm, destination $V' = 150$ cm
- Asymmetry $A = 45°$
- Frequency = 2 lifts/min, duration = 2 hours
- Coupling = Poor (no handles)

Compute the **Lifting Index (LI)** and recommend ergonomic improvements if LI exceeds 1.0. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**RULA Definition (2 marks):**
RULA (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment) is a survey method developed by McAtamney and Corlett (1993) for assessing the postural loading on the musculoskeletal system, particularly the upper limbs. It evaluates:
- **Arm and forearm postures**
- **Wrist posture and force**
- **Neck, trunk, and leg postures**

The final score (1–7) indicates the level of intervention required.

**Grand Score Interpretation (2 marks):**

| Grand Score | Risk Level | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| 1–2 | Negligible | Acceptable posture |
| 3–4 | Low risk | May need further investigation |
| 5–6 | Medium risk | **Change soon** |
| 7 | Very high risk | **Change immediately** |

**Application to Hand-Tool Redesign (3 marks):**
1. **Observe current use** — Worker scores 6 (medium risk) when using a straight screwdriver overhead.
2. **Identify problematic posture** — Wrist deviation > 15°, shoulder abduction > 90°.
3. **Redesign interventions:**
   - Add **pistol-grip handle** to keep wrist neutral.
   - Use **angled shaft** to reduce wrist deviation.
   - Introduce **low-vibration grip material** to lower required grip force.
   - Provide **spring-loaded ratchet** to reduce sustained muscle contraction.
4. **Re-evaluate** — New score drops to 3 → acceptable.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Compute Travel Distance (1 mark)**

$$D = \vert V' - V \vert = \vert 150 - 50 \vert = 100 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2: Compute Each Multiplier (3 marks)**

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{30} = 0.833$$

For $V = 50$ cm (below 75 cm reference):

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \times 25 = 0.925$$

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{100} = 0.82 + 0.045 = 0.865$$

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A = 1 - 0.0032 \times 45 = 1 - 0.144 = 0.856$$

For 2 lifts/min, 2-hour duration → **NIOSH Table gives $FM = 0.91$**

$$FM = 0.91$$

Coupling = Poor → $CM = 0.90$

$$CM = 0.90$$

**Step 3: Compute RWL (1 mark)**

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.833 \times 0.925 \times 0.865 \times 0.856 \times 0.91 \times 0.90$$

Computing step by step:

$$RWL = 23 \times (0.833 \times 0.925) = 23 \times 0.7705 = 17.72$$

$$RWL = 17.72 \times 0.865 = 15.33$$

$$RWL = 15.33 \times 0.856 = 13.12$$

$$RWL = 13.12 \times 0.91 = 11.94$$

$$RWL = 11.94 \times 0.90 = 10.75 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Compute Lifting Index (1 mark)**

$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{15}{10.75} = 1.395$$

**Step 5: Interpretation and Recommendations (1 mark)**

Since $LI = 1.395 > 1.0$, the task poses **marginal-to-increased risk** and requires redesign.

**Engineering Recommendations:**
1. **Improve coupling** — Add proper handles → $CM$ rises to 1.00, new RWL ≈ 11.94 kg → LI drops to 1.26 (still > 1).
2. **Reduce load to ≤ 10.75 kg** — split into two boxes.
3. **Reduce asymmetry** — reposition worker to face load → $AM = 1.00$, new RWL ≈ 12.55, LI = 1.195.
4. **Reduce vertical travel** — reposition shelves so V and V' are within 25 cm range → $DM$ improves.
5. **Best long-term solution:** Install **mechanical lift assist / conveyor** between stations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this checklist before every KTU exam on Ergonomics in Product Design.**

### **Core Concepts (MUST KNOW)**

- ☐ Ergonomics = science of fitting product to user (not user to product).
- ☐ Three domains: **Physical, Cognitive, Organisational.**
- ☐ **Anthropometry** = body measurement science; uses **Normal Distribution** assumption.
- ☐ **P5** = small end (reachability); **P95** = large end (clearance).
- ☐ **P50** = median; rarely used alone; usually with adjustability.
- ☐ ISO 9241-210 governs human-centred design process.
- ☐ **Static vs Dynamic vs Functional** anthropometry differ in measurement context.

### **Numerical Formulae (MUST KNOW)**

- ☐ $P_k = \mu + Z_k \cdot \sigma$ (percentile of normal distribution).
- ☐ $RWL = 23 \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ (NIOSH).
- ☐ $LI = L/RWL$ → if > 1, redesign.
- ☐ $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ (Fitts' Law).
- ☐ $H = D \cdot \theta / 3438$ (character height).
- ☐ $T_j = F \times d$ (joint torque).
- ☐ $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ (Hick's Law).

### **Design Heuristics (MUST KNOW)**

- ☐ Handle diameter **30–50 mm**.
- ☐ Push button diameter **10–25 mm**.
- ☐ Display distance **500–700 mm**.
- ☐ Adjustable workbench range: P5 to P95 elbow height minus 5 cm.
- ☐ RULA grand score ≥ 5 = redesign immediately.
- ☐ LI > 1 = redesign mandatory.
- ☐ NIOSH ideal lift = **23 kg**.

### **Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid**

- ☐ Never use **P50 alone** without justification.
- ☐ Never forget to **include safety margin** (typically 5–10 cm or 25%).
- ☐ Never skip **engineering recommendations** at the end of a calculation.
- ☐ Never confuse **percentile direction** (P5 = small, P95 = large).
- ☐ Never skip **units** in final answer (state cm, kg, mm explicitly).
- ☐ Never use $RWL = 23$ kg unconditionally — always multiply by all six multipliers.

### **Memory Aid: The Six NIOSH Multipliers**

> **"H**and **V**ery **D**aring **A**nimals **F**eed **C**arefully"
> HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM

### **Memory Aid: Percentile Usage**

> **"5 = REACH small, 95 = FIT large"**
> P5 used for **reaching** to small people; P95 used for **fitting** large people.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In KTU Part B questions, **always structure** your answer as:
> 1. **Given data** (restate values)
> 2. **Formula selection** (state which formula you are applying and why)
> 3. **Step-by-step substitution** (show each multiplier or percentile calculation)
> 4. **Numerical result with units** (box the final answer)
> 5. **Engineering interpretation** (what does this mean for design?)
> 6. **Recommendation** (what change should be made?)
>
> Examiners award ~40% of marks for the interpretation + recommendation even if the numerical answer has a small error.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Concurrent Engineering (CE)

### Formal Academic Definition
Concurrent Engineering is a **systematic, integrated, parallel approach** to the design, development, and implementation of products, processes, and supporting systems in which **cross-functional teams** work simultaneously on different phases of a product's life cycle to optimize the entire process from concept through disposal, with the primary objective of minimizing time-to-market while simultaneously improving quality, reducing cost, and enhancing manufacturability, serviceability, and recyclability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Concurrent Engineering (also called **Simultaneous Engineering** or **Integrated Product Development (IPD)**) is a *philosophy* rather than a *tool*. It is one of the most frequently tested 2-mark and 14-mark questions under Module 2 of PERUT526.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of building a house:
- **Traditional (Sequential) Engineering:** Architect designs → waits for approval → then civil engineer plans → waits → then electrician → then plumber. Total time = **18 months**.
- **Concurrent Engineering:** All teams (architect, civil, electrical, plumbing, interior) sit in **one room from Day 1**, sharing a 3D model. The electrician spots a wiring conflict while the architect is still drawing the wall layout and fixes it *instantly*. Total time = **8 months**.

The house gets built **faster, cheaper, and with fewer rework cycles** because every stakeholder's constraints are considered *simultaneously* rather than *one after the other*.

### Key Principles of CE (Remember for KTU Exams)
1. **Parallel task execution** – Activities performed concurrently, not sequentially.
2. **Cross-functional integration** – Design, manufacturing, quality, marketing, service work together.
3. **Early supplier involvement (ESI)** – Vendors are part of design from start.
4. **Up-front planning** – More time spent in *front-end* (concept phase) reduces downstream rework.
5. **Design for X (DFX)** – Design for Manufacturing (DFM), Assembly (DFA), Test (DFT), Service (DFS), Environment (DFE).

### Standard Metrics Used in CE
- **Time-to-Market (TTM)** – measured in *months*
- **First-Time-Right (FTR) Ratio** – measured in *percentage (%)
- **Design Cycle Time** – measured in *weeks*
- **Number of Engineering Change Orders (ECOs)** – *count per project*
- **DFA Index** (Boothroyd-Dewhurst) – *assembly efficiency in %*

### CE vs. Traditional Engineering (Quick Comparison)

| Aspect | Traditional (Sequential) | Concurrent Engineering |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | One phase finishes before next starts | All phases overlap and proceed in parallel |
| Team Structure | Functional silos | Cross-functional, integrated teams |
| Information Flow | Handed over, often lost | Shared continuously via common databases |
| Time-to-Market | Long (12–24 months typical) | Reduced by **30–70%** |
| Cost of Changes | Very high after design freeze | Low, since issues caught early |
| Customer Focus | Reactive | Proactive, voice of customer integrated |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost-of-Change Rule (Barry Boehm's Curve):** A defect fixed at the *concept stage* costs **1 unit**; the same defect fixed at the *production stage* costs **100 units**, and at the *field-deployment stage* it can cost **1000 units**. CE attacks this exponential cost rise by catching defects *upfront*.

---

## 1.2 Forecasting – The Gateway to Planning

### Formal Academic Definition
**Forecasting** is the process of estimating future demand for a product, service, or resource based on **historical data, trend analysis, and mathematical/statistical models**, used as the primary input for production planning, inventory control, capacity planning, and budgetary decisions in industrial and operations management.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you run a tea stall near a KTU college. Over the last 6 weeks, you've sold these cups per day: **80, 95, 110, 100, 130, 145**. The number is going *upward* over time. Forecasting says: *"Based on this pattern, I expect you'll need around **150–160 cups** next week."* You buy milk and sugar *accordingly*. Forecasting is essentially **data-driven educated guessing** for the future.

### Classification of Forecasting Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition Pair:** Examiners love asking the difference between *Qualitative* and *Quantitative* forecasting. Memorize this exact wording.

| Type | Sub-Type | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative** | Jury of Executive Opinion, Delphi Method, Sales Force Composite, Market Research | No historical data; new product launch; volatile markets |
| **Quantitative – Time Series** | Moving Averages, Exponential Smoothing, Trend Projection, Decomposition | Stable demand patterns; sufficient historical data |
| **Quantitative – Associative (Causal)** | Regression Analysis (Linear, Multiple), Econometric Models, Leading Indicators | Demand depends on identifiable external variables (price, advertising, GDP) |

### Time Series Components (Foundation for All Moving Averages)
Every demand series $D_t$ can be decomposed into:
- **Level ($L_t$)** – the baseline value
- **Trend ($T_t$)** – long-term direction (up/down)
- **Seasonality ($S_t$)** – periodic short-term fluctuations
- **Cyclical ($C_t$)** – multi-year waves (recession, boom)
- **Random/Noise ($R_t$)** – unpredictable variation

$$D_t = L_t + T_t + S_t + C_t + R_t$$

The four methods covered in this module (SMA, WMA, Double MA, SES) belong to the **Time Series family** and assume that the future is a continuation of the past pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forecasting Horizon Rule:** Short-term (< 3 months) → Simple methods (SMA, SES) work best. Long-term (> 1 year) → Causal/Regression methods dominate. KTU 14-mark problems almost always test the *short-term quantitative* family.

---

## 1.3 Quick Overview of the Four Quantitative Methods

| Method | One-Line Definition | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** | Average of the last *n* actual demands, equal weight to each | Stable demand, no trend |
| **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | Weighted average of last *n* demands, recent data given higher weight | Mild changes, recency matters |
| **Double Moving Average (DMA)** | Two passes of SMA used to **capture and extrapolate trend** | Data with clear upward/downward trend |
| **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)** | Weighted average of *all* past data with exponentially decaying weights, controlled by smoothing constant $\alpha$ | Stable demand with minimum data storage |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU):** Applying SMA to data with a strong trend. SMA *lags* behind trend, producing systematic forecast error. Use DMA instead. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question every year.
