## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Linear Regression Method** of forecasting with a neat diagram. Derive the expressions for slope `b` and intercept `a`. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:** Linear regression fits a straight line $\hat{Y} = a + bX$ through scatter data using the **Least Squares Criterion** — minimizing $\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$.

**Diagram:** (Students should draw X-Y axes with scatter points and the best-fit regression line passing through the centroid $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$.)

```
       Y
       |
   Y3 -|- - - - - . . . . . 
       |          .     .
   Y2 -|- - . . . . . . . . .  
       |   .     . 
   Y1 -|. . . . . 
       |______________________ X
          X1  X2  X3 ... Xn
```

**Derivation:** Let $S = \sum (Y_i - a - bX_i)^2$ be the sum of squared errors.

To minimize, take partial derivatives and set them to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2 \sum (Y_i - a - bX_i) = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2 \sum X_i(Y_i - a - bX_i) = 0$$

From the first equation:
$$\sum Y_i = na + b\sum X_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{\sum Y - b\sum X}{n}$$

From the second equation:
$$\sum X_i Y_i = a \sum X_i + b \sum X_i^2$$

Substituting `a` and solving yields:
$$b = \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$$

**Conclusion:** These formulas give the best-fit line that minimizes total squared error. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** The sales of a product over the last **6 months** are: **120, 135, 142, 156, 168, 175** units. Using the **Linear Regression Method**, forecast the sales for the **7th and 8th months**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulation**

Let X = Month number, Y = Sales.

| Month X | Sales Y | XY | X² |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | 120 | 1 |
| 2 | 135 | 270 | 4 |
| 3 | 142 | 426 | 9 |
| 4 | 156 | 624 | 16 |
| 5 | 168 | 840 | 25 |
| 6 | 175 | 1050 | 36 |
| **Σ** | **896** | **3330** | **91** |

So: $n = 6$, $\sum X = 21$, $\sum Y = 896$, $\sum XY = 3330$, $\sum X^2 = 91$.

**Step 2 — Slope (b)**
$$b = \frac{6(3330) - (21)(896)}{6(91) - (21)^2}$$
$$b = \frac{19980 - 18816}{546 - 441} = \frac{1164}{105} = 11.086$$

**Step 3 — Intercept (a)**
$$a = \frac{896 - (11.086)(21)}{6} = \frac{896 - 232.8}{6} = \frac{663.2}{6} = 110.53$$

**Step 4 — Trend Equation**
$$\hat{Y} = 110.53 + 11.086X$$

**Step 5 — Forecasts**
$$\hat{Y}_7 = 110.53 + 11.086(7) = 110.53 + 77.60 = 188.13 \text{ units}$$
$$\hat{Y}_8 = 110.53 + 11.086(8) = 110.53 + 88.69 = 199.22 \text{ units}$$

> **Final Answer:** Month 7 = **188 units**, Month 8 = **199 units**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Semi-Average Method** of forecasting. Mention its advantages and limitations. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** The Semi-Average Method divides the time-series into two equal halves, computes the arithmetic mean of each half, and treats these means as two points to derive a linear trend line.

**Procedure:**
1. Split the series into two halves (drop middle term if n is odd).
2. Calculate the average of each half → $\bar{Y}_1$ and $\bar{Y}_2$.
3. Determine the midpoints $X_1$ and $X_2$.
4. Calculate the slope: $b = (\bar{Y}_2 - \bar{Y}_1) / (X_2 - X_1)$.
5. Calculate intercept: $a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$.
6. Form the trend equation: $\hat{Y} = a + bX$.

**Advantages:**
- **Simplicity** — minimal computation, easy to apply.
- **Speed** — quick for preliminary forecasts.
- **Intuitive** — easy to explain to non-technical stakeholders.

**Limitations:**
- **Ignores recent data** — treats all observations within a half equally.
- **Only linear trends** — fails for non-linear patterns.
- **Odd-n adjustment** — dropping the middle value introduces error.
- **No error metric** — does not provide confidence intervals. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** The demand (in units) for the last **8 years** is given below. Use the **Semi-Average Method** to forecast the demand for the **9th year**. **\[7 Marks\]**

| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 50 | 55 | 60 | 58 | 65 | 70 | 72 | 78 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Split into Two Halves**
- **First Half (Years 1–4):** 50, 55, 60, 58
- **Second Half (Years 5–8):** 65, 70, 72, 78

**Step 2 — Compute Semi-Averages**
$$\bar{Y}_1 = \frac{50 + 55 + 60 + 58}{4} = \frac{223}{4} = 55.75$$
$$\bar{Y}_2 = \frac{65 + 70 + 72 + 78}{4} = \frac{285}{4} = 71.25$$

**Step 3 — Identify Midpoints**
- Midpoint of first half: $X_1 = 2.5$
- Midpoint of second half: $X_2 = 6.5$

**Step 4 — Compute Slope (b)**
$$b = \frac{71.25 - 55.75}{6.5 - 2.5} = \frac{15.5}{4} = 3.875$$

**Step 5 — Compute Intercept (a)**
$$\bar{X} = \frac{36}{8} = 4.5, \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{508}{8} = 63.5$$
$$a = 63.5 - (3.875)(4.5) = 63.5 - 17.44 = 46.06$$

**Step 6 — Trend Equation**
$$\hat{Y} = 46.06 + 3.875X$$

**Step 7 — Forecast for Year 9 (X = 9)**
$$\hat{Y}_9 = 46.06 + 3.875(9) = 46.06 + 34.875 = 80.94 \approx 81 \text{ units}$$

> **Final Answer:** Year 9 forecast ≈ **81 units**. **(7 marks)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Module:**
>
> 1. **AES Sequencing Error** — Students often compute the trend `bₜ` *before* updating the level `Lₜ`. The correct order is: **update Lₜ first, then bₜ**. Reversing this loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Regression Sign Mistake** — Always carry out the full subtraction in the numerator; do not pre-assume the sign of `b`. A negative `b` is valid and indicates declining trend.
>
> 3. **Semi-Average Midpoint Blunder** — Many students use the *first* and *last* data points instead of the *midpoints of the two halves*. This is a fundamental error costing full marks.
>
> 4. **Delphi as "Survey"** — The Delphi Method is **not** a simple survey. Examiners look for keywords: *anonymity*, *iteration*, *feedback*, *consensus*. Missing any two of these caps marks at 50%.
>
> 5. **Unit Mismatch** — Always state the **unit** of the forecast (e.g., ₹ lakhs, units, kg). Skipping units costs 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 6. **No Formula Citation** — Even if the answer is correct, failing to write the governing formula before substitution loses 1 mark per sub-question.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adjusted Exponential Smoothing** uses **two constants (α, β)** and models both **level** and **trend** recursively; ideal for trending series without seasonality.
- **Initialization rule** for AES: $L_1 = Y_1$, $b_1 = Y_2 - Y_1$ (or 0).
- **AES forecast** extrapolates linearly: $F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot b_t$.
- **Linear Regression** minimizes the **sum of squared errors** to derive the best-fit line.
- **Slope formula**: $b = [n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y] / [n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2]$.
- **Intercept formula**: $a = [\sum Y - b\sum X] / n$.
- **Semi-Average Method** uses **two semi-means** as anchors for a straight trend line.
- For Semi-Average with **even n**, split exactly in half; with **odd n**, drop the middle value first.
- **Slope in Semi-Average** = (difference of semi-means) ÷ (gap between midpoints).
- **Delphi Method** is **qualitative**, used when **no historical data** exists.
- Delphi is characterized by **anonymity**, **iteration**, **structured feedback**, and **consensus**.
- Typical Delphi panel size: **10–30 experts**; typical rounds: **3–5**.
- All four methods are **complementary** — choose based on data availability, trend presence, and forecast horizon.
- **Always cite the formula** before substitution in KTU answers.
- **Always state units** in the final answer.
- **Always carry out the recursion step-by-step** in AES — examiners reward explicit intermediate values.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection and Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inspection and Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Quality

**Quality** is formally defined as the **fitness for use** at an affordable cost, conforming to established standards and satisfying customer expectations. The most academically accepted and rigorous definitions come from the quality gurus of the 20th century.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Juran's Trilogy Definition):** Quality is defined as *"fitness for purpose/intended use."* This is the operative definition KTU examiners expect when 2-mark definition questions are posed.

### Classical Definitions of Quality Gurus

| Quality Guru | Definition of Quality | Emphasis |
|---|---|---|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Fitness for use | Customer-oriented, end-use focus |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability at low cost | Statistical, reduction of variation |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Conformance to requirements (specifications) | Zero-defect philosophy |
| **Walter A. Shewhart** | The outcome of a stable system | Statistical process control origin |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | A way of managing to assure customer satisfaction | Total Quality Control (TQC) |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss imparted to society from the time a product is shipped | Quality loss function |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Continuous customer satisfaction | Company-wide quality control |

### Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Product Quality (KTU Frequently Asked)

David Garvin (1984) identified **eight dimensions** that comprehensively define product quality. These are extremely high-yield for KTU short-answer questions.

1. **Performance** – Primary operating characteristics (e.g., engine power, fuel efficiency).
2. **Features** – Secondary characteristics that add value (e.g., touchscreen in a car).
3. **Reliability** – Probability of failure-free operation over a specified period.
4. **Conformance** – Degree to which a product matches pre-defined specifications.
5. **Durability** – Useful life of the product until it requires replacement.
6. **Serviceability** – Speed, ease, and cost of repairs and maintenance.
7. **Aesthetics** – Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound, taste).
8. **Perceived Quality** – Subjective impression based on brand, image, advertising.

## 1.2 Inspection – Formal Definition

**Inspection** is the process of measuring, examining, testing, or gauging one or more characteristics of a product or service and comparing the results with specified requirements to determine conformity. In KTU parlance, inspection is the operational arm of quality control.

## 1.3 Quality Control (QC) – Formal Definition

**Quality Control (QC)** is the operational technique and the set of activities used to fulfil requirements for quality. It involves **operational techniques and activities** that are used to measure, monitor, and regulate the quality of a product or service. QC is reactive and inspection-based.

## 1.4 Quality Assurance (QA) – Formal Definition

**Quality Assurance (QA)** is the planned and systematic set of activities implemented within the quality system that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. QA is **proactive**, system-oriented, and process-focused.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Differentiator:**
> - **QC (Quality Control)** → Reactive, **detects** defects after they occur, focuses on the **product**.
> - **QA (Quality Assurance)** → Proactive, **prevents** defects from occurring, focuses on the **process**.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> 
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen preparing meals.
> 
> - **Inspection** is the **chef tasting the soup** before it leaves the kitchen — measuring and comparing to the recipe.
> - **Quality Control** is the **head chef checking finished plates** against a standard before serving — a reactive activity.
> - **Quality Assurance** is the **kitchen manager redesigning the workflow** so that the soup *cannot* come out wrong — installing a process that prevents the defect at the source.
> - **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** is the **digital thermometer** continuously monitoring the soup's temperature, raising an alarm if it deviates from 95 °C.
> - **Six Sigma** is the **mathematical commitment** that the restaurant will serve no more than **3.4 defective meals per million** — a metric of near-perfection.

This analogy makes clear: **inspection is a snapshot**, **QC is the corrective action**, and **QA is the systemic prevention**.

## 1.6 Inspection vs. Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance

| Parameter | Inspection | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Measuring & comparing | Operational techniques | Planned activities |
| **Focus** | Product | Product (output) | Process (system) |
| **Approach** | Reactive | Reactive | Proactive / Preventive |
| **Objective** | Sort good vs bad | Meet quality standards | Build confidence in system |
| **Stage** | Pre-delivery | During production | Throughout the value chain |
| **Scope** | Single unit / batch | Production line | Entire organization |
| **Tools used** | Gauges, calipers, jigs | Control charts, sampling | SOPs, audits, training |
| **KTU Key Tag** | 100% / Sampling | SQC | TQM / ISO 9000 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Inspection (KTU High-Yield Classification)

Inspections are classified based on **when** and **where** they are performed.

### A. Classification Based on Stage of Production

1. **Incoming / Receiving Inspection** – Done on raw materials and purchased components before they enter production.
2. **In-Process / Floor Inspection** – Conducted at strategic points on the shop floor to detect deviations early.
3. **Final Inspection** – Performed on the finished product before dispatch to the customer.

### B. Classification Based on Extent of Inspection

1. **100% Inspection (Screening Inspection)**
   - Every single unit is examined.
   - Used when: (i) defect is critical (safety), (ii) sample size is small, (iii) inspection cost is low.
   - **Drawbacks:** costly, time-consuming, prone to inspector fatigue and errors.

2. **Sampling Inspection**
   - Only a sample is drawn from the lot; decision (accept/reject) is based on the sample.
   - Used when: (i) inspection is destructive, (ii) lot size is large, (iii) cost of 100% inspection is prohibitive.

### C. Classification Based on Approach

- **Centralized Inspection** – All inspection performed at a central location.
- **Floor / Line Inspection** – Inspection carried out at the workstation by line inspectors.
- **Combined / Group Inspection** – Hybrid of the above two.

## 2.2 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) – Framework

SQC applies **statistical methods** to monitor and control the quality of a process. It has two principal branches:

1. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** → Uses **control charts** to monitor a *process* in real-time.
2. **Acceptance Sampling** → Uses **sampling plans** to decide whether to accept or reject a *lot* of incoming material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Link:** SQC operates on the foundational **Central Limit Theorem**, which states that regardless of the population distribution, the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size $n$ increases.

## 2.3 Control Charts – Operational Theory

A **control chart** is a graphical tool used to study how a process changes over time. It has:

- A **Centre Line (CL)** representing the process mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$ or $\bar{p}$.
- An **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** at $\bar{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma$.
- A **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** at $\bar{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma$.
- The region between UCL and LCL is the **Zone of Common Cause Variation (3σ belt)**.

### Why **3σ**? The 3-Sigma Rule

For a normally distributed variable, approximately **99.73%** of all observations lie within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$. Therefore, the probability of a point falling outside the control limits purely by chance is only **0.27%**. This is the **Western Electric / Shewhart** basis for control limits.

$$
P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973
$$

## 2.4 Types of Control Charts

### A. Control Charts for Variables (Measurable Characteristics)

These monitor continuous data (length, weight, diameter, temperature).

| Chart | Statistic Plotted | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **$\bar{X}$ – R Chart** | Mean and Range | Most common, $n \le 10$ |
| **$\bar{X}$ – σ Chart** | Mean and Std. Dev. | $n > 10$ |
| **X-MR Chart (Individuals & Moving Range)** | Individual value & Moving range | $n = 1$ (slow processes) |
| **CUSUM Chart** | Cumulative sum | Detect small, persistent shifts |
| **EWMA Chart** | Exponentially Weighted Moving Average | Detect small shifts, smoother |

### B. Control Charts for Attributes (Discrete / Count Data)

| Chart | Statistic | Defect Type | Sample Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **p-chart** | Fraction defective | % defective (variable $n$) | Variable |
| **np-chart** | Number of defectives | Count of defectives (fixed $n$) | Constant |
| **c-chart** | Defects per unit | Total number of defects | Unit / area |
| **u-chart** | Defects per unit (variable) | Average defects per unit | Variable |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formulas. The vertical pipe symbol is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

### A. Process Control Limits

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$

$$
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

$$
\text{UCL}_{R} = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad \text{CL}_{R} = \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL}_{R} = D_3 \bar{R}
$$

### B. Attribute Chart Limits

$$
\text{UCL}_{p} = \bar{p} + 3 \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}, \quad \text{LCL}_{p} = \bar{p} - 3 \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
$$

$$
\text{UCL}_{np} = n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}, \quad \text{LCL}_{np} = n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}
$$

$$
\text{UCL}_{c} = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}, \quad \text{LCL}_{c} = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}
$$

$$
\text{UCL}_{u} = \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}, \quad \text{LCL}_{u} = \bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}
$$

### C. Process Capability Indices

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{\text{Tolerance Width}}{\text{Process Width}}
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

$$
C_{pm} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}
$$

### D. Six Sigma Metrics

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}} \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{Yield} = e^{-DPMO/10^6} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{2} \quad \text{(when centred)}
$$

### E. Control Chart Constants

| Sample Size $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ | $E_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 2.660 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 | 1.772 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 1.457 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 | 1.290 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 1.184 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 1.109 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 | 1.054 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 | 1.010 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 | 0.975 |

## 2.6 Process Capability Interpretation

| $C_p$ / $C_{pk}$ Value | Process Capability | Six-Sigma Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| $< 1.00$ | Not capable | $< 3\sigma$ |
| $1.00 - 1.33$ | Marginally capable | $3\sigma$ to $4\sigma$ |
| $1.33 - 1.67$ | Capable | $4\sigma$ to $4.5\sigma$ |
| $\ge 1.67$ | Highly capable (Industry standard for new processes) | $\ge 5\sigma$ |
| $\ge 2.00$ | World-class | $6\sigma$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** $C_p$ only checks spread; $C_{pk}$ checks **both spread AND centering**. Always compute $C_{pk}$ when the process mean is off-target.

## 2.7 Acceptance Sampling – Theory and Mechanics

Acceptance sampling is a **statistical procedure** in which a sample is taken from a lot, and based on the sample result, a decision is made to either **accept** or **reject** the entire lot. It is **NOT** a substitute for quality; it is a *quality audit*.

### Why Acceptance Sampling?

1. Inspection is **destructive** (e.g., light bulb life test).
2. **Cost of 100% inspection is prohibitive**.
3. **Fatigue and inspection errors** make 100% inspection unreliable.
4. Lot must be **destroyed** to be inspected.
5. **In-process control is not possible**.

### Risk Measures in Acceptance Sampling

| Risk | Type | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Producer's Risk (α)** | Type I | Probability of rejecting a **GOOD** lot | 0.05 (5%) |
| **Consumer's Risk (β)** | Type II | Probability of accepting a **BAD** lot | 0.10 (10%) |
| **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)** | Producer's | Worst tolerable quality | α point |
| **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD)** | Consumer's | Worst acceptable incoming | β point |
| **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** | — | Graph of $P_a$ vs $p_d$ | Tool |

## 2.8 Types of Acceptance Sampling Plans

| Type | Description | KTU Favourite? |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Sampling Plan** | One sample, accept if $d \le c$, reject if $d > c$. | **Yes** |
| **Double Sampling Plan** | Two-stage: first sample, then second if inconclusive. | Yes |
| **Multiple Sampling Plan** | More than two stages, with cumulative decision rules. | Less common |
| **Sequential Sampling Plan** | Item-by-item testing; decision after each draw. | Advanced |
| **Rectifying Inspection Plan** | All rejected lots are 100% inspected and defects replaced. | Avg Outgoing Quality (AOQ) |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry (Toyota, Ford)**: SQC charts in *every* press shop, weld shop, and paint line.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Acceptance sampling mandatory; *zero* tolerance for non-conformance in critical drugs.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus)**: AS9100 standard mandates process capability $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ on critical characteristics.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication**: Six-Sigma drives yield; the entire fab is a $6\sigma$ environment.
- **Food Processing**: HACCP + c-charts for monitoring foreign object counts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why 3σ? The Shewhart 3-Sigma Limit

**Statement:** The probability that a sample mean will fall outside $\mu \pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ is approximately 0.0027 (or 0.27%).

### Step 1: Standardize the Sample Mean

We are interested in the random variable $\bar{X}$, which has a mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$.

The standard normal variable is:

$$
Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule)

For any normal distribution, the probability within $k$ standard deviations is:

$$
P(\mu - k\sigma \le \bar{X} \le \mu + k\sigma) = \Phi(k) - \Phi(-k) = 2\Phi(k) - 1
$$

For $k = 3$, using the standard normal table:

$$
\Phi(3) = 0.99865
$$

### Step 3: Compute Probability Inside Limits

$$
P(\mu - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} \le \bar{X} \le \mu + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}) = 2 \times 0.99865 - 1 = 0.9973
$$

This corresponds to **99.73%** of all sample means lying within the control limits.

### Step 4: Compute Probability Outside (Type I Error, $\alpha$)

$$
\alpha = 1 - 0.9973 = 0.0027
$$

This is equivalent to an Average Run Length (ARL) of:

$$
\text{ARL}_0 = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370 \text{ samples}
$$

So, on average, a false alarm will occur once every 370 samples even when the process is in control.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: $\bar{X}$-Chart Control Limits from $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$

**Given:** $k$ preliminary samples of size $n$ each, with sample means $\bar{X}_i$ and ranges $R_i$.

### Step 1: Grand Mean

The grand average of the sample means is the centre line:

$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i
$$

### Step 2: Average Range

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i
$$

### Step 3: Estimate of Process Standard Deviation

Using the average range method (Shewhart's approximation):

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
$$

where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant depending only on $n$ (from the table in §2.5).

### Step 4: Standard Deviation of Sample Mean

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\hat{\sigma}}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}
$$

### Step 5: 3-Sigma Control Limits

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}
$$

Define the constant $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$. Therefore:

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

This derivation is the **foundation of every KTU $\bar{X}$-R chart problem**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$

**Given:** Specification limits USL, LSL, process mean $\mu$, and standard deviation $\sigma$.

### Step 1: Tolerance Width

The total tolerance band is:

$$
T = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}
$$

### Step 2: Process Spread

The natural 6-sigma spread of the process is:

$$
6\sigma
$$

### Step 3: $C_p$ (Capability – Spread Only)

$$
C_p = \frac{T}{6\sigma} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

This measures how well the **process spread fits within the spec limits**, ignoring the mean.

### Step 4: $C_{pk}$ (Capability – Centred and Spread)

To account for off-centre processes, we use:

$$
C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)
$$

where $k$ is the centering index:

$$
k = \frac{2 \vert \mu - M \vert}{T}, \quad M = \frac{\text{USL} + \text{LSL}}{2}
$$

A more useful direct form is:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

If $\mu$ is **dead-centre** at $M$, then $C_{pk} = C_p$.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Complete KTU-Style Problem (Variables Chart)

> **Question:** The following data are obtained from 5 samples of size 4 from a manufacturing process. Construct the $\bar{X}$ and $R$ charts and comment on the state of control.
> 
> | Sample | Observations | | | |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | 1 | 12 | 14 | 13 | 15 |
> | 2 | 11 | 13 | 14 | 12 |
> | 3 | 15 | 13 | 14 | 16 |
> | 4 | 13 | 12 | 11 | 14 |
> | 5 | 14 | 15 | 13 | 12 |

### Step 1: Compute Sample Mean $\bar{X}_i$ and Range $R_i$

For **Sample 1**: $\bar{X}_1 = (12 + 14 + 13 + 15)/4 = 54/4 = 13.5$, $R_1 = 15 - 12 = 3$.

For **Sample 2**: $\bar{X}_2 = (11 + 13 + 14 + 12)/4 = 50/4 = 12.5$, $R_2 = 14 - 11 = 3$.

For **Sample 3**: $\bar{X}_3 = (15 + 13 + 14 + 16)/4 = 58/4 = 14.5$, $R_3 = 16 - 13 = 3$.

For **Sample 4**: $\bar{X}_4 = (13 + 12 + 11 + 14)/4 = 50/4 = 12.5$, $R_4 = 14 - 11 = 3$.

For **Sample 5**: $\bar{X}_5 = (14 + 15 + 13 + 12)/4 = 54/4 = 13.5$, $R_5 = 15 - 12 = 3$.

### Step 2: Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$

$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{13.5 + 12.5 + 14.5 + 12.5 + 13.5}{5} = \frac{66.5}{5} = 13.3
$$

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 3}{5} = 3.0
$$

### Step 3: Look Up Constants for $n = 4$

From the table in §2.5:

$$
A_2 = 0.729, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.282
$$

### Step 4: $\bar{X}$-Chart Control Limits

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 13.3 + (0.729)(3.0) = 13.3 + 2.187 = 15.487
$$

$$
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 13.3
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 13.3 - 2.187 = 11.113
$$

### Step 5: $R$-Chart Control Limits

$$
\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R} = 2.282 \times 3.0 = 6.846
$$

$$
\text{CL}_R = 3.0
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0
$$

### Step 6: Plot and Interpret

All five sample means (12.5, 13.5, 14.5, 12.5, 13.5) lie between LCL = 11.113 and UCL = 15.487. All five ranges equal 3.0, lying between 0 and 6.846.

**Conclusion:** The process is **in statistical control** (no points outside control limits, no run/pattern violations).

## 3.5 Worked Example: Process Capability Problem

> **Question:** A shaft is produced with a target diameter of 50 mm. The specification limits are USL = 50.05 mm and LSL = 49.95 mm. From past data, the process mean is $\mu = 49.99$ mm with $\sigma = 0.02$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and comment.

### Step 1: Tolerance and Process Width

$$
T = 50.05 - 49.95 = 0.10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
6\sigma = 6 \times 0.02 = 0.12 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2: $C_p$

$$
C_p = \frac{T}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.10}{0.12} = 0.833
$$

### Step 3: $C_{pk}$

$$
\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{50.05 - 49.99}{0.06} = \frac{0.06}{0.06} = 1.0
$$

$$
\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{49.99 - 49.95}{0.06} = \frac{0.04}{0.06} = 0.667
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.0, 0.667) = 0.667
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

Since $C_p = 0.833 < 1.0$, the process is **not capable** in terms of spread. Since $C_{pk} = 0.667 < C_p$, the process is **off-centre** (mean is closer to LSL). Process must be re-centred and variation reduced.

## 3.6 Worked Example: p-Chart Construction

> **Question:** 25 samples of size 50 each are inspected. The total number of defectives is 75. Construct the $p$-chart.

### Step 1: Average Fraction Defective

$$
\bar{p} = \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}} = \frac{75}{25 \times 50} = \frac{75}{1250} = 0.06
$$

### Step 2: Standard Deviation of $p$

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.06 \times 0.94}{50}} = \sqrt{0.001128} = 0.03359
$$

### Step 3: Control Limits

$$
\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sigma_p = 0.06 + 3(0.03359) = 0.06 + 0.1008 = 0.1608
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_p = 0.06 - 0.1008 = -0.0408 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Set to 0}
$$

(Standard practice: if LCL is negative, set it to zero because fraction defective cannot be negative.)

## 3.7 Worked Example: Single Sampling Plan OC Curve

> **Question:** A single sampling plan has $n = 100$, $c = 2$. Compute $P_a$ for $p = 0.01$ and $p = 0.05$.

The Operating Characteristic (OC) for a single sampling plan is the cumulative binomial:

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

For $n = 100$, $c = 2$:

$$
P_a = (1-p)^{100} + 100p(1-p)^{99} + 4950 p^2 (1-p)^{98}
$$

### Case A: $p = 0.01$

$$
P_a(0.01) = (0.99)^{100} + 100(0.01)(0.99)^{99} + 4950(0.0001)(0.99)^{98}
$$

$$
= 0.3660 + 100(0.01)(0.3697) + 4950(0.0001)(0.3735)
$$

$$
= 0.3660 + 0.3697 + 0.1849 = 0.9206
$$

So for a lot that is 1% defective, the probability of acceptance is **92.06%** — the plan is lenient at low defect rates.

### Case B: $p = 0.05$

$$
P_a(0.05) = (0.95)^{100} + 100(0.05)(0.95)^{99} + 4950(0.0025)(0.95)^{98}
$$

$$
= 0.00592 + 0.03117 + 0.08119 = 0.1183
$$

So for a lot that is 5% defective, the probability of acceptance is only **11.83%** — the plan correctly rejects bad lots.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define Quality Assurance and Quality Control. Distinguish between them.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Quality Assurance (QA)** is the planned and systematic set of activities implemented in the quality system that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. QA is **proactive** and focuses on the **process** to prevent defects.
- **Quality Control (QC)** is the operational technique used to fulfil quality requirements, focusing on **detecting defects** in the finished product. QC is **reactive** and inspection-based.

| Parameter | QA | QC |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Proactive (Prevention) | Reactive (Detection) |
| Focus | Process / System | Product / Output |
| Scope | Entire organization | Production floor |
| Cost | Low in long run | Higher due to rework/scrap |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write *"QA is before, QC is after"* — this is **partially correct but incomplete**. The KTU board expects the **prevention vs detection** distinction as the primary differentiator. Marks are lost if the systems approach of QA is not mentioned.

### Question 2: What is Statistical Process Control? List any four control charts used in SPC.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

Statistical Process Control (SPC) is the application of statistical methods (especially control charts) to monitor, control, and improve a manufacturing process by studying process variation over time. The goal is to distinguish between **common cause variation** (inherent) and **special cause variation** (assignable).

**Four control charts:**

1. **$\bar{X}$-R Chart** – For variables with sample size $n \le 10$.
2. **p-chart** – For fraction defective with variable sample size.
3. **c-chart** – For number of defects per unit (constant area).
4. **X-MR Chart** – For individual measurements when $n = 1$.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Construction of Control Charts and Process Capability Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A process produces piston rings with a target inner diameter of 50 mm. The specification limits are USL = 50.20 mm and LSL = 49.80 mm. From 20 samples of size 5, the grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.02$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.10$ mm. The process standard deviation is estimated to be $\sigma = 0.045$ mm.

(i) Construct the $\bar{X}$-chart and $R$-chart. (7 Marks)
(ii) Calculate the process capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and comment on the process capability. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Part (i): Control Chart Construction (7 Marks)**

For $n = 5$, from the control chart constants table:

$$
A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.114
$$

**$\bar{X}$-Chart Limits:**

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.02 + (0.577)(0.10) = 50.02 + 0.0577 = 50.0777
$$

$$
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 50.02
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 50.02 - 0.0577 = 49.9623
$$

**R-Chart Limits:**

$$
\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.10) = 0.2114
$$

$$
\text{CL}_R = 0.10
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0
$$

**Part (ii): Process Capability (7 Marks)**

**$C_p$ calculation:**

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{50.20 - 49.80}{6 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.40}{0.27} = 1.481
$$

**$C_{pk}$ calculation:**

$$
\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{50.20 - 50.02}{3 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.18}{0.135} = 1.333
$$

$$
\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{50.02 - 49.80}{0.135} = \frac{0.22}{0.135} = 1.630
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.333, 1.630) = 1.333
$$

**Comment:** $C_p = 1.481$ indicates a **capable process** in terms of spread. $C_{pk} = 1.333 < C_p$ indicates the process mean ($\mu = 50.02$) is **slightly off-centre** (the midpoint is $M = 50.00$; the mean is shifted upward by 0.02 mm). The process is **capable but not optimally centred**. Recommendation: re-centre the process to bring $\mu = 50.00$.

---

### Question B: Acceptance Sampling and OC Curve

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define Operating Characteristic (OC) curve. What is Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk? (4 Marks)
**(b)** A single sampling plan has $n = 50$ and $c = 1$. Compute the probability of acceptance $P_a$ when the lot fraction defective is (i) $p = 0.01$ and (ii) $p = 0.08$. Comment on whether the plan is satisfactory. (10 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) and (b):

**Part (a) – Definitions (4 Marks)**

- **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** is a graphical plot of the probability of acceptance ($P_a$) of a lot as a function of the incoming lot fraction defective ($p$) for a given sampling plan. It shows the **discriminating power** of the plan.
- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$)** is the probability of **rejecting a good lot** (whose quality is at or better than AQL). It is the Type I error. Typical value: $\alpha = 0.05$.
- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$)** is the probability of **accepting a bad lot** (whose quality is at LTPD). It is the Type II error. Typical value: $\beta = 0.10$.

**Part (b) – Probability of Acceptance (10 Marks)**

The single sampling plan acceptance probability is the cumulative binomial:

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d} = (1-p)^n + np(1-p)^{n-1}
$$

For $n = 50$, $c = 1$:

$$
P_a(p) = (1-p)^{50} + 50p(1-p)^{49}
$$

**Case (i): $p = 0.01$**

$$
P_a(0.01) = (0.99)^{50} + 50(0.01)(0.99)^{49}
$$

$$
= 0.6050 + 50(0.01)(0.6112)
$$

$$
= 0.6050 + 0.3056 = 0.9106
$$

**Probability of acceptance = 0.9106 or 91.06%**

This means there is a **8.94% producer's risk** (rejection of a 1% defective lot). For a good-quality lot, this risk is moderately high.

**Case (ii): $p = 0.08$**

$$
P_a(0.08) = (0.92)^{50} + 50(0.08)(0.92)^{49}
$$

$$
= 0.0155 + 50(0.08)(0.01685)
$$

$$
= 0.0155 + 0.0674 = 0.0829
$$

**Probability of acceptance = 0.0829 or 8.29%**

This means there is a **91.71% rejection** of a lot that is 8% defective — the plan correctly rejects bad lots.

**Comment on the plan:**

- At $p = 0.01$ (good quality): $P_a = 91.06\%$ → Producer's risk is unacceptably high (8.94%). Strict producers would want $P_a \ge 95\%$.
- At $p = 0.08$ (bad quality): $P_a = 8.29\%$ → Plan is good at rejecting poor lots.
- The OC curve is **discriminating** but the producer's risk at low $p$ is borderline. To improve, increase $n$ or raise $c$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** When asked to "comment on the plan," students must discuss **both** the producer's and consumer's perspectives, not just one. Marks are deducted if a directional recommendation (e.g., "increase $n$ to 80") is missing. Also, students frequently confuse $P_a$ (probability of acceptance) with the complement of $\alpha$ — verify the calculation before writing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### Definitions and Fundamentals
- Quality = **Fitness for use** (Juran) or **Conformance to requirements** (Crosby).
- **Inspection** = measuring & comparing; **QC** = reactive, product-based; **QA** = proactive, process-based.
- Deming's **14 Points** emphasize statistical thinking, supplier partnership, and elimination of numerical quotas.
- **Garvin's 8 Dimensions** (Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality) — *very high yield for 2-mark questions.*

### Statistical Process Control (SPC)
- 3σ rule gives $\alpha = 0.0027$ and $\text{ARL}_0 = 370$ samples.
- $\bar{X}$-R chart is the most common; constants $A_2, D_3, D_4$ depend on $n$.
- **Western Electric Rules** detect non-random patterns: 2 of 3 beyond 2σ, 4 of 5 beyond 1σ, 8 consecutive on one side.
- **p-chart** for variable $n$ attribute data; **c-chart** for defects per unit.

### Process Capability
- $C_p$ = (Spec Width) / (Process Width) → spread only.
- $C_{pk}$ = min(USL-μ, μ-LSL) / 3σ → spread + centering.
- $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ is industry-acceptable; $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ is **highly capable**; $C_{pk} \ge 2.00$ is **Six-Sigma world-class.**

### Acceptance Sampling
- **AQL** protects the producer ($\alpha = 0.05$); **LTPD** protects the consumer ($\beta = 0.10$).
- **AOQ** (Average Outgoing Quality) = $\dfrac{(N - n)p_a \cdot p}{N}$ for rectifying plans.
- **ATI** (Average Total Inspection) = $n + (1 - P_a)N$ for non-rectifying plans.
- OC curve plots $P_a$ vs $p_d$ — **steepness = discrimination power.**

### Six Sigma
- $3.4$ DPMO = $6\sigma$ level (with 1.5σ shift allowance).
- **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) is the problem-solving framework.
- **DMADV** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify) is the design framework.

### Common Numerical Pitfalls
- Always use $d_2$ correctly: $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R} / d_2$.
- If $\text{LCL}$ is negative for p-chart, set to **0** (not negative!).
- For $n = 1$, use **X-MR** (Individuals & Moving Range) chart.
- **Six Sigma shift of 1.5σ** is the Motorola convention — not 0σ.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — The Academic Definition

**Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is the systematic application of statistical techniques — probability theory, sampling distributions, hypothesis testing, and analysis of variance (ANOVA) — to the **measurement, monitoring, diagnosis, and improvement** of product quality and process performance throughout the stages of production.

It is the engineering discipline that converts raw manufacturing data into **defect-prevention intelligence**, rather than relying solely on post-production defect detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Critical):**
> *SQC is the branch of quality management that uses statistical methods to monitor and control a process, ensuring it operates at its full potential to manufacture conforming products. It encompasses both **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — for controlling the process — and **Acceptance Sampling** — for controlling the product lot.*

---

## 1.2 Purpose of SQC (The "Why" — KTU Board Favorite)

SQC is deployed in industry for **seven major engineering purposes**:

| # | Purpose | Engineering Significance |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Process Monitoring** | Detect drift in mean (X-bar) or spread (R, σ) before defects are produced |
| 2 | **Defect Reduction** | Replace 100% inspection (slow, costly, error-prone) with intelligent sampling |
| 3 | **Variance Reduction** | Quantify and minimize common-cause vs. special-cause variation |
| 4 | **Specification Conformance** | Verify that the process capability index (Cp, Cpk) meets design tolerance |
| 5 | **Cost Minimization** | Lower rejection, rework, warranty, and inspection labor costs |
| 6 | **Supplier Quality Assurance** | Statistically validate incoming raw material lots |
| 7 | **Continuous Improvement** | Provide quantitative feedback for Kaizen, Six Sigma, TQM initiatives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2-Mark Board Question Hook:** *SQC does not eliminate variation — it **identifies, quantifies, and reduces** assignable causes of variation, leaving only random (chance) variation inherent to the process.*

---

## 1.3 Types of SQC — The Master Classification

SQC is broadly divided into **two complementary branches**:

### Type 1: Statistical Process Control (SPC) — *The Process Doctor*
- Applies **control charts** (X̄-R, X̄-σ, p, c, etc.) to a *flow* of in-process output.
- **Goal:** Keep the process *in control* and *capable*.
- **Cadence:** Real-time / continuous.
- **Decision Maker:** The shop-floor engineer.

### Type 2: Acceptance Sampling — *The Lot Gatekeeper*
- Inspects a **random sample** taken from a *finished lot* to decide **Accept / Reject / Rectify**.
- **Goal:** Protect the consumer without 100% inspection.
- **Cadence:** Lot-by-lot.
- **Decision Maker:** The quality assurance department.

### Extended Sub-Typology (KTU Module 3 Coverage)

| Branch | Sub-Type | Application |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **SPC** | Variables Control Charts (X̄-R, X̄-σ, Median, Moving Range) | Measurable data (length, weight, tensile strength) |
| **SPC** | Attributes Control Charts (p, np, c, u) | Go/No-Go data (defective, pass/fail) |
| **Acceptance Sampling** | Single, Double, Multiple, Sequential Plans | Lot acceptance with various sample-size economics |
| **Acceptance Sampling** | Rectifying Inspection Plans | Rejected lots are 100% screened |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital ICU:** *SPC is like a heart monitor in the ICU — it watches the process continuously and beeps the moment something abnormal appears. Acceptance Sampling is like the admission blood test — a single diagnostic sample decides whether a patient (lot) is admitted to the general ward (accepted) or sent to ICU (rejected/rectified).*

---

## 1.4 Probability Concepts — The Mathematical Engine of SQC

SQC rests entirely on **probability theory**. The foundational concepts a KTU student must internalize are:

1. **Random Experiment & Sample Space (S):** A repeatable process with uncertain outcomes.
2. **Event (E):** Any subset of S for which a probability can be defined.
3. **Probability Axioms (Kolmogorov):**
   - $0 \le P(E) \le 1$
   - $P(S) = 1$
   - For mutually exclusive events: $P(E_1 \cup E_2) = P(E_1) + P(E_2)$
4. **Conditional Probability:** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$
5. **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)}$
6. **Random Variable:** A function mapping sample points to real numbers (discrete or continuous).
7. **Probability Distributions** critical to SQC:
   - **Normal (Gaussian):** Backbone of variables control charts.
   - **Binomial:** Basis of the *p-chart* and *np-chart*.
   - **Poisson:** Basis of the *c-chart* and *u-chart* (rare defects over area/time).
   - **Hypergeometric:** Used when sample size ≥ 10% of lot.

---

## 1.5 Estimation — Inferring the Unknown from the Known

**Statistical Estimation** is the procedure of using **sample statistics** to approximate **population parameters** — a critical SQC operation because the entire process universe is rarely measured.

Two fundamental types:
- **Point Estimation:** A single value approximates the parameter (e.g., $\bar{X}$ estimates $\mu$, $S$ estimates $\sigma$).
- **Interval Estimation:** A range (confidence interval) within which the parameter is believed to lie with a stated probability.

The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** is the cornerstone:
> Regardless of the parent population's shape, the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ approaches a **Normal distribution** with mean $\mu$ and standard error $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ as $n \ge 30$.

---

## 1.6 Significance Testing — Decision-Making Under Uncertainty

A **Significance Test (Hypothesis Test)** is a formal procedure to decide, based on sample evidence, whether a hypothesis about a population parameter should be **rejected** or **not rejected**.

Standard 5-step KTU framework:
1. State $H_0$ (Null) and $H_1$ (Alternative)
2. Choose significance level $\alpha$ (typically **0.05**)
3. Compute test statistic (Z, t, F, χ²)
4. Determine critical region / p-value
5. Decide and interpret in SQC context (Type I / Type II error)

---

## 1.7 ANOVA — Comparing Multiple Means Simultaneously

**Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)** partitions total observed variation into *between-group* and *within-group* components to test whether **three or more population means are equal**.

In SQC, ANOVA is used to:
- Compare the mean output of **multiple machines / operators / shifts / batches**.
- Identify the **largest source of variation** in a multi-stage process.
- Validate **Design of Experiments (DoE)** outcomes.

---

## 1.8 Sampling Inspection — The Industrial Application of SQC

**Sampling Inspection** is the practical act of drawing a **random sample** from a lot and using the sample defect rate (or mean) to make a **decision rule-based accept/reject judgment** about the entire lot.

Used because:
- 100% inspection is **economically infeasible** for destructive testing (e.g., light bulb life).
- 100% inspection suffers from **inspector fatigue** and is itself error-prone.
- A well-designed sampling plan provides **statistical protection** to both producer and consumer at known risk levels ($\alpha$ and $\beta$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Probability Distributions — The Heartbeat of SQC

### 2.1.1 Normal Distribution (Backbone of Variables Charts)

The continuous random variable $X$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ if its probability density function (PDF) is:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}\right], \quad -\infty < x < \infty
$$

**Symmetric bell curve** governed by the famous **Empirical (68-95-99.7) Rule**:
- $\mu \pm 1\sigma$ contains **68.27%** of observations
- $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ contains **95.45%**
- $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ contains **99.73%** — the basis of all "3-sigma" control limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3σ limit is not arbitrary:** It corresponds to a probability of **0.0027** (or 27 in 10,000) that a point falls outside purely by chance — the foundation of Shewhart's control chart.

### 2.1.2 Binomial Distribution (Discrete Defective Counting)

The number of defectives $X$ in $n$ trials, with probability $p$ of being defective:

$$
P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{n-x}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

- **Mean:** $\mu = np$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^{2} = np(1-p)$

### 2.1.3 Poisson Distribution (Rare Defects / Unit-Area Counting)

The number of defects $c$ occurring in a fixed area, time, or volume:

$$
P(X = c) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{c}}{c!}, \quad c = 0, 1, 2, \dots
$$

- **Mean:** $\lambda$
- **Variance:** $\lambda$ (mean = variance, a defining signature)

---

## 2.2 Estimation — Confidence Intervals for Process Parameters

### 2.2.1 Confidence Interval for the Mean ($\mu$)

**Case A — σ known (use Z):**

$$
\bar{X} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le \mu \le \bar{X} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Case B — σ unknown (use t, df = n − 1):**

$$
\bar{X} - t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}} \le \mu \le \bar{X} + t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

### 2.2.2 Confidence Interval for a Proportion ($p$)

$$
\hat{p} - Z_{\alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} \le p \le \hat{p} + Z_{\alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}
$$

---

## 2.3 Significance Testing — The Decision Calculus

### 2.3.1 One-Sample Z-Test (σ known)

$$
Z_{calc} = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}
$$

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$.

### 2.3.2 One-Sample t-Test (σ unknown)

$$
t_{calc} = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S / \sqrt{n}}, \quad df = n - 1
$$

### 2.3.3 Two-Sample t-Test (Comparing Two Means)

$$
t_{calc} = \frac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{S_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}}
$$

where

$$
S_p^{2} = \frac{(n_1 - 1)S_1^{2} + (n_2 - 1)S_2^{2}}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}
$$

### 2.3.4 Chi-Square Test for Variance

$$
\chi^{2}_{calc} = \frac{(n-1)S^{2}}{\sigma_0^{2}}
$$

---

## 2.4 ANOVA — One-Way Classification

The total sum of squares is partitioned:

$$
SS_{Total} = SS_{Between} + SS_{Within}
$$

Formulas:

$$
SS_{Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..})^{2}
$$

$$
SS_{Between} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..})^{2}
$$

$$
SS_{Within} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})^{2}
$$

**Test Statistic (F-ratio):**

$$
F_{calc} = \frac{MS_{Between}}{MS_{Within}} = \frac{SS_{Between} / (k-1)}{SS_{Within} / (N-k)}
$$

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{\alpha,\, k-1,\, N-k}$.

---

## 2.5 Sampling Inspection — Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve Mathematics

For a **single sampling plan** (n, c) under binomial lot inspection:

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^{x}(1-p)^{n-x}
$$

- $P_a$ = probability of acceptance given fraction defective $p$
- $n$ = sample size, $c$ = acceptance number
- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):** $P_a$ at the Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)
- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):** $1 - P_a$ at the Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD)

Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ):

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

Average Total Inspection (ATI):

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)
$$

---

## 2.6 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the ESE — every numerical problem in Module 3 is solved from this list.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Use / Condition |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Z-Score | $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ | Standardization to N(0,1) |
| 2 | CLT Standard Error | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ | Distribution of sample mean |
| 3 | Binomial Mean / Var | $np$ ; $np(1-p)$ | Defective counts |
| 4 | Poisson Mean / Var | $\lambda$ ; $\lambda$ | Rare defects |
| 5 | CI for $\mu$ ($\sigma$ known) | $\bar{X} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ | Large samples |
| 6 | CI for $\mu$ ($\sigma$ unknown) | $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,n-1} \cdot S/\sqrt{n}$ | Small samples |
| 7 | CI for proportion | $\hat{p} \pm Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}$ | Attribute data |
| 8 | One-Sample Z | $Z = (\bar{X} - \mu_0) / (\sigma/\sqrt{n})$ | Mean test |
| 9 | One-Sample t | $t = (\bar{X} - \mu_0) / (S/\sqrt{n})$ | Small-sample mean test |
| 10 | Two-Sample t | $t = (\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) / (S_p\sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2})$ | Two means |
| 11 | $\chi^2$ for variance | $\chi^2 = (n-1)S^{2}/\sigma_0^{2}$ | Variance test |
| 12 | F-ratio (ANOVA) | $F = MS_B / MS_W$ | k ≥ 3 means |
| 13 | df for ANOVA | $k-1$ ; $N-k$ | Between ; Within |
| 14 | $P_a$ Binomial | $\sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$ | OC curve |
| 15 | AOQ | $P_a \cdot p (N-n)/N$ | Post-inspection quality |
| 16 | ATI | $n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$ | Inspection cost basis |

---

## 2.7 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering

- **Process Capability Analysis** links the **normal distribution** and **estimation** to tolerance design — a core mechanical / production engineering concern.
- **Six Sigma Black Belt** projects (widely adopted in Kerala's electronics and IT-hardware industries) depend entirely on **ANOVA, hypothesis testing, and confidence intervals**.
- **Outgoing product quality** to OEMs (e.g., NeST, V-Guard, and Kerala-based rubber/tyre plants) is certified through **sampling inspection plans** conforming to ISO 2859-1 (derived from MIL-STD-105E).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the OC Curve for a Single Sampling Plan

**Given:** A lot of $N$ items contains $D$ defectives. A sample of $n$ items is drawn randomly without replacement. Acceptance number is $c$ (accept lot if defectives in sample $\le c$).

The probability of acceptance, $P_a$, is governed by the **hypergeometric distribution**:

$$
P_a = P(X \le c) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{D}{x}\binom{N-D}{n-x}}{\binom{N}{n}}
$$

**Step 1:** When $n/N \le 0.1$ (the standard SQC assumption for "large lot"), the hypergeometric collapses into the **binomial** approximation:

$$
P_a \approx \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^{x}(1-p)^{n-x}
$$

where $p = D/N$ is the lot fraction defective.

**Step 2:** When $n$ is large and $p$ is small, binomial further converges to the **Poisson** form:

$$
P_a \approx \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{x}}{x!}
$$

**Step 3:** Plot $P_a$ against $p$ (or fraction defective). The resulting **S-shaped curve** is the OC curve.

**Step 4:** Locate two critical points:
- At $p_1$ = AQL, $P_a = 1 - \alpha$ (Producer's protection).
- At $p_2$ = LTPD, $P_a = \beta$ (Consumer's protection).

**Step 5:** Read off the steepest portion — this is the **Indifference Quality Level (IQL)** at $P_a = 0.50$.

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation of One-Way ANOVA F-Ratio

**Setup:** $k$ independent samples, each of size $n_i$, with grand mean $\bar{X}_{..}$.

**Step A — Decompose each observation** using the identity:

$$
X_{ij} = \bar{X}_{..} + (\bar{X}_{i.} - \bar{X}_{..}) + (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i.})
$$

- Grand mean + Treatment effect + Residual error

**Step B — Square and sum** to obtain:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij}-\bar{X}_{..})^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i(\bar{X}_{i.}-\bar{X}_{..})^{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(X_{ij}-\bar{X}_{i.})^{2}
$$

Hence:

$$
SS_{Total} = SS_{Between} + SS_{Within}
$$

**Step C — Degrees of Freedom:**

$$
df_{Total} = N - 1; \quad df_{Between} = k - 1; \quad df_{Within} = N - k
$$

**Step D — Mean Squares:**

$$
MS_{Between} = \frac{SS_{Between}}{k-1}, \qquad MS_{Within} = \frac{SS_{Within}}{N-k}
$$

**Step E — F-Ratio:**

$$
F = \frac{MS_{Between}}{MS_{Within}}
$$

**Step F — Decision:** If $F > F_{\alpha,\,k-1,\,N-k}$ then $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \dots = \mu_k$ is **rejected**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Confidence Interval (KTU Typical 7-mark)

**Problem:** A random sample of $n = 25$ piston rings has mean diameter $\bar{X} = 74.035$ mm and standard deviation $S = 0.02$ mm. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean diameter $\mu$. Assume σ is unknown.

**Solution Path:**

Step 1 — Identify the applicable formula. Since $\sigma$ is unknown and $n = 25 < 30$, use the **t-distribution** with $df = 24$.

$$
CI = \bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

Step 2 — Look up the critical value:

$$
t_{0.025,\, 24} = 2.064
$$

Step 3 — Compute the standard error:

$$
SE = \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.02}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{0.02}{5} = 0.004 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the margin of error:

$$
ME = t \cdot SE = 2.064 \times 0.004 = 0.00826 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 5 — Build the interval:

$$
74.035 - 0.00826 \le \mu \le 74.035 + 0.00826
$$

$$
\boxed{74.0267 \text{ mm} \le \mu \le 74.0433 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Interpretation (board-expected answer):** We are 95% confident that the true mean piston-ring diameter lies between 74.0267 mm and 74.0433 mm.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — ANOVA (KTU Typical 7-mark)

**Problem:** Three machines produce the same component. Five samples from each are tested for tensile strength (kg/cm²). Data:

| Machine 1 | Machine 2 | Machine 3 |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 45 | 42 | 48 |
| 47 | 44 | 50 |
| 49 | 46 | 52 |
| 46 | 45 | 49 |
| 48 | 43 | 51 |

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean strengths differ.

**Step 1 — State hypotheses:**

$$
H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 \quad ; \quad H_1: \text{Not all means equal}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute column means:**

$$
\bar{X}_{1.} = \frac{45+47+49+46+48}{5} = 47.0
$$

$$
\bar{X}_{2.} = \frac{42+44+46+45+43}{5} = 44.0
$$

$$
\bar{X}_{3.} = \frac{48+50+52+49+51}{5} = 50.0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the grand mean:**

$$
\bar{X}_{..} = \frac{47.0 + 44.0 + 50.0}{3} = 47.0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $SS_{Between}$:**

$$
SS_B = 5(47 - 47)^{2} + 5(44 - 47)^{2} + 5(50 - 47)^{2}
$$

$$
SS_B = 5(0) + 5(9) + 5(9) = 0 + 45 + 45 = 90
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $SS_{Within}$:**

For Machine 1: $(45-47)^{2} + (47-47)^{2} + (49-47)^{2} + (46-47)^{2} + (48-47)^{2} = 4+0+4+1+1 = 10$

For Machine 2: $(42-44)^{2} + (44-44)^{2} + (46-44)^{2} + (45-44)^{2} + (43-44)^{2} = 4+0+4+1+1 = 10$

For Machine 3: $(48-50)^{2} + (50-50)^{2} + (52-50)^{2} + (49-50)^{2} + (51-50)^{2} = 4+0+4+1+1 = 10$

$$
SS_W = 10 + 10 + 10 = 30
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Mean Squares:**

$$
MS_B = \frac{SS_B}{k-1} = \frac{90}{3-1} = 45
$$

$$
MS_W = \frac{SS_W}{N-k} = \frac{30}{15-3} = \frac{30}{12} = 2.5
$$

**Step 7 — F-ratio:**

$$
F_{calc} = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W} = \frac{45}{2.5} = 18.0
$$

**Step 8 — Critical value:** From F-tables, $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$.

**Step 9 — Decision:** Since $18.0 > 3.89$, **reject $H_0$**. The mean tensile strengths produced by the three machines differ significantly.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Single Sampling Plan OC Curve (KTU 7-mark)

**Problem:** For a single sampling plan $n = 50, c = 1$, calculate $P_a$ when $p = 0.02$ using the binomial model. Comment on producer's vs consumer's risk.

**Step 1 — Apply the binomial acceptance formula:**

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{x=0}^{c}\binom{n}{x}p^{x}(1-p)^{n-x}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute $n = 50$, $c = 1$, $p = 0.02$:

$$
P_a = \binom{50}{0}(0.02)^{0}(0.98)^{50} + \binom{50}{1}(0.02)^{1}(0.98)^{49}
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate each term:

$$
(0.98)^{50} \approx 0.3642
$$

$$
(0.98)^{49} \approx 0.3717
$$

Step 4 — Compute:

$$
P_a = 1 \times 1 \times 0.3642 + 50 \times 0.02 \times 0.3717
$$

$$
P_a = 0.3642 + 0.3717 = 0.7359
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** If 2% of items are defective, this plan accepts the lot about 73.6% of the time. A 73.6% acceptance rate at LTPD 2% would expose the consumer to high risk; the plan must be tightened by reducing $c$ or increasing $n$ to achieve the desired $\beta$.

## 4.2 Hypothesis Testing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 State H0 and H1] --> B[Step 2 Select Significance Level alpha]
    B --> C{Step 3 Choose Test}
    C -->|Sigma known| D1[Z Test]
    C -->|Sigma unknown| D2[t Test]
    C -->|Variance test| D3[Chi Square Test]
    C -->|3 or more means| D4[ANOVA F Test]
    C -->|Two proportions| D5[Z Test for Proportions]
    D1 --> E[Step 4 Compute Test Statistic]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F{Step 5 Compare}
    F -->|Stat exceeds critical| G1[Reject H0]
    F -->|Stat within bounds| G2[Do not reject H0]
    G1 --> H1[Interpret Type I or II Error in SQC]
    G2 --> H2[Process Considered in Control]
    H1 --> I[Take Corrective Action]
    H2 --> J[Continue Monitoring]
```

## 4.3 Sampling Inspection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Lot Size N] --> B[Draw Random Sample Size n]
    B --> C[Inspect Sample Items]
    C --> D{Defectives d vs Acceptance Number c}
    D -->|d is less or equal to c| E1[Accept Lot]
    D -->|d is greater than c| E2[Reject Lot]
    E1 --> F1[Ship to Customer]
    E2 --> F2[Rectifying Inspection 100 percent Screen]
    F2 --> G{Defective items in lot}
    G -->|Discovered| H1[Replace or Rework]
    G -->|Not Discovered| H2[Dispose]
    E1 --> I[Plot OC Curve]
    E2 --> I
    I --> J[Evaluate Producer Risk Alpha]
    I --> K[Evaluate Consumer Risk Beta]
    J --> L[Tune Plan n and c]
    K --> L
```

## 4.4 ANOVA Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Observations Xij] --> B[Compute Grand Mean X double dot]
    A --> C[Compute Group Means X i dot]
    B --> D[SS Total Sum of squared deviations from grand mean]
    C --> E[SS Between Sum of squared deviations of group means from grand mean]
    A --> F[SS Within Sum of squared deviations within each group]
    D --> G[Identity SS Total equals SS Between plus SS Within]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute MS Between equals SS Between over k minus 1]
    G --> I[Compute MS Within equals SS Within over N minus k]
    H --> J[F Ratio equals MS Between over MS Within]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Compare F calc with F critical alpha k minus 1 N minus k}
    K -->|F calc exceeds F critical| L[Reject H0 Means Differ]
    K -->|F calc not exceed F critical| M[Fail to Reject H0 Means Equal]
```
