# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q:** Define Statistical Quality Control (SQC). List its two major branches.

**Model Answer:**
SQC is the application of statistical techniques — probability, sampling distributions, hypothesis testing, and ANOVA — to monitor, control, and improve product quality and process performance.

**Two major branches:**
1. **Statistical Process Control (SPC):** Uses control charts (X̄-R, p, c) to monitor an ongoing process in real time.
2. **Acceptance Sampling:** Uses a random sample from a lot to decide accept/reject based on a sampling plan with stated producer's and consumer's risk.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q:** Distinguish between Type I and Type II errors in the context of hypothesis testing for SQC.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Type I Error (α) | Type II Error (β) |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Rejecting $H_0$ when it is true | Failing to reject $H_0$ when it is false |
| SQC Meaning | Concluding a process is out of control when it actually is in control | Concluding a process is in control when it actually is out of control |
| Risk Identity | Producer's risk (rejects good lot) | Consumer's risk (accepts bad lot) |
| Common α, β | 0.05 | 0.10 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q:** (a) Define ANOVA. With a suitable industrial example, explain one-way ANOVA. (7 marks)

**(b)** Three operators A, B, C produce components on the same machine. Five measurements from each operator (in mm) are:
- A: 10.2, 10.4, 10.1, 10.3, 10.5
- B: 10.0, 10.1, 9.9, 10.2, 10.0
- C: 10.3, 10.6, 10.5, 10.4, 10.7

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean output differs significantly between operators using ANOVA. ($F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$) (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a):

**Definition (1 mark):** ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) is a statistical method that partitions total variation in a dataset into components attributable to different sources (between-group and within-group), testing whether the means of three or more groups differ significantly.

**Industrial Example (3 marks):** A manufacturer of precision shafts wants to verify whether the mean diameter produced by three different machines (M1, M2, M3) is the same. Take 5 shafts from each machine, measure diameters, then apply one-way ANOVA.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Compute grand mean $\bar{X}_{..}$ and group means $\bar{X}_{i.}$
2. Compute $SS_B$, $SS_W$, $SS_T$
3. Compute $MS_B$ and $MS_W$
4. $F_{calc} = MS_B / MS_W$
5. Compare with $F_{critical}(\alpha, k-1, N-k)$ and conclude.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Hypotheses (0.5 mark):**
$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C$ ; $H_1$: Not all means equal.

**Step 2 — Group means (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{X}_A = \frac{10.2+10.4+10.1+10.3+10.5}{5} = 10.30
$$

$$
\bar{X}_B = \frac{10.0+10.1+9.9+10.2+10.0}{5} = 10.04
$$

$$
\bar{X}_C = \frac{10.3+10.6+10.5+10.4+10.7}{5} = 10.50
$$

**Step 3 — Grand mean (0.5 mark):**

$$
\bar{X}_{..} = \frac{10.30 + 10.04 + 10.50}{3} = 10.28
$$

**Step 4 — $SS_{Between}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
SS_B = 5(10.30-10.28)^{2} + 5(10.04-10.28)^{2} + 5(10.50-10.28)^{2}
$$

$$
SS_B = 5(0.0004) + 5(0.0576) + 5(0.0484) = 0.002 + 0.288 + 0.242 = 0.532
$$

**Step 5 — $SS_{Within}$ (2 marks):**

Within A: $(10.2-10.30)^{2} + (10.4-10.30)^{2} + (10.1-10.30)^{2} + (10.3-10.30)^{2} + (10.5-10.30)^{2}$
$= 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.04 + 0.00 + 0.04 = 0.10$

Within B: $(10.0-10.04)^{2} + (10.1-10.04)^{2} + (9.9-10.04)^{2} + (10.2-10.04)^{2} + (10.0-10.04)^{2}$
$= 0.0016 + 0.0036 + 0.0196 + 0.0256 + 0.0016 = 0.052$

Within C: $(10.3-10.50)^{2} + (10.6-10.50)^{2} + (10.5-10.50)^{2} + (10.4-10.50)^{2} + (10.7-10.50)^{2}$
$= 0.04 + 0.01 + 0.00 + 0.01 + 0.04 = 0.10$

$$
SS_W = 0.10 + 0.052 + 0.10 = 0.252
$$

**Step 6 — Mean Squares (0.5 mark):**

$$
MS_B = \frac{0.532}{3-1} = 0.266
$$

$$
MS_W = \frac{0.252}{15-3} = 0.021
$$

**Step 7 — F-ratio and decision (1 mark):**

$$
F_{calc} = \frac{0.266}{0.021} = 12.67
$$

Since $F_{calc} = 12.67 > F_{critical} = 3.89$, **reject $H_0$**. The mean output differs significantly between operators.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:** (a) Define acceptance sampling. Explain the single sampling plan and the operating characteristic (OC) curve with a neat sketch. (7 marks)

**(b)** A single sampling plan has $n = 80$, $c = 2$. Calculate the probability of acceptance when the lot fraction defective is $p = 0.03$. Comment on whether this plan is suitable if the LTPD is 0.05 with $\beta = 0.10$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a):

**Definition (1 mark):** Acceptance sampling is a statistical quality control technique in which a random sample is drawn from a lot and the lot is accepted or rejected based on the number of defectives found in the sample, rather than inspecting the entire lot.

**Single Sampling Plan (3 marks):**
- A lot of $N$ items is presented.
- A random sample of $n$ items is drawn.
- Each sample item is inspected; let $d$ = number of defectives.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept the lot if $d \le c$; reject if $d > c$, where $c$ is the acceptance number.
- Rejected lots are either 100% screened (rectifying) or returned to the supplier.

**OC Curve (3 marks):**
- The OC curve is a plot of $P_a$ (probability of acceptance) vs. $p$ (fraction defective).
- Shape: Ideal OC is a step-function with a single vertical drop at the AQL.
- Real OC: smooth S-shaped curve, indicating that some good lots are rejected (α) and some bad lots are accepted (β).
- Key points: $(p_1, 1-\alpha)$ at AQL; $(p_2, \beta)$ at LTPD.

```
P_a
 1.0 |___________
     |           \
     |            \   (Real OC curve - S-shaped)
     |             \
     |              \___
  β  |.                 \____
     |                       \____
 0.0 |__________________________\____
     0      AQL      IQL      LTPD    p
```

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Formula (0.5 mark):**

$$
P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{c}\binom{n}{x}p^{x}(1-p)^{n-x}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = 80$, $c = 2$, $p = 0.03$ (1 mark):**

$$
P_a = \binom{80}{0}(0.03)^{0}(0.97)^{80} + \binom{80}{1}(0.03)^{1}(0.97)^{79} + \binom{80}{2}(0.03)^{2}(0.97)^{78}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term (3 marks):**

- $(0.97)^{80} \approx 0.0872$
- $(0.97)^{79} \approx 0.0899$
- $(0.97)^{78} \approx 0.0927$

$$
T_1 = 1 \times 1 \times 0.0872 = 0.0872
$$

$$
T_2 = 80 \times 0.03 \times 0.0899 = 0.2158
$$

$$
T_3 = \frac{80 \times 79}{2} \times 0.0009 \times 0.0927 = 3160 \times 0.0000834 = 0.2636
$$

**Step 4 — Sum (0.5 mark):**

$$
P_a = 0.0872 + 0.2158 + 0.2636 = 0.5666
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on suitability (2 marks):** With $p = 0.03$, $P_a \approx 0.567$, meaning the plan accepts 56.7% of lots with 3% defectives. If LTPD = 0.05, we should compute $P_a$ at $p = 0.05$:

$$
P_a(0.05) = (0.95)^{80} + 80(0.05)(0.95)^{79} + 3160(0.0025)(0.95)^{78}
$$

$$
P_a(0.05) \approx 0.0167 + 0.0701 + 0.1466 = 0.2334
$$

Since consumer's risk $\beta = 1 - P_a = 0.767$ at LTPD — far higher than the desired 0.10 — the plan is **not suitable**. The plan must be tightened by reducing $c$ or increasing $n$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Real KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting the t-distribution:** Using $Z_{\alpha/2}$ instead of $t_{\alpha/2, n-1}$ when $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$ — **costs 2-3 marks**.
> 2. **Mislabeling degrees of freedom in ANOVA:** Writing $df_W = k-1$ instead of $N-k$ — **a frequent 1-mark error**.
> 3. **OC curve binomial computation skipped steps:** Examiners expect the explicit expansion of each term; writing only the final $P_a$ value without showing the three individual terms loses **up to 3 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Producer's Risk with Consumer's Risk:** The AQL region corresponds to **α** (producer's risk) and the LTPD region to **β** (consumer's risk) — a **1-mark** classic blunder.
> 5. **Not stating the conclusion in plain language:** Even when $F_{calc}$ and $F_{critical}$ are computed correctly, students who fail to write *"Reject $H_0$; means differ significantly"* lose the **final 1 mark** for interpretation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Re-read on the morning of the ESE:**

- [x] SQC = SPC + Acceptance Sampling.
- [x] SPC is *process-focused*, real-time; Acceptance Sampling is *lot-focused*, discrete.
- [x] Normal distribution → variables charts; Binomial → p, np charts; Poisson → c, u charts.
- [x] The 3σ rule embeds 99.73% probability — basis of Shewhart control limits.
- [x] **CLT:** For $n \ge 30$, $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2}/n)$ regardless of parent distribution.
- [x] Use **Z** when $\sigma$ is known; use **t** when $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$.
- [x] Confidence interval = Point estimate ± (Critical value × Standard error).
- [x] Significance level $\alpha$ = max tolerable Type I error rate.
- [x] Type I = Reject good $H_0$ = Producer's risk. Type II = Accept bad $H_0$ = Consumer's risk.
- [x] ANOVA partitions $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$ with df $(N-1) = (k-1) + (N-k)$.
- [x] F-ratio = $MS_B / MS_W$. Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$.
- [x] OC curve gives $P_a$ for each $p$. AQL is left (high $P_a$); LTPD is right (low $P_a$).
- [x] $P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{c}\binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$ for binomial sampling.
- [x] For destructive testing, sampling inspection is the **only** feasible QC method.
- [x] Always interpret statistical results in the **engineering / SQC context**, not in abstract statistics language.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Plans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sampling Plans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Acceptance Sampling — The KTU Board Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Sampling Plan (KTU Definition):** A statistical quality control procedure in which a random sample is drawn from a lot/batch, inspected, and based on the number of defects observed, a decision is made to **ACCEPT** or **REJECT** the entire lot. It is a *decision-making tool* applied to lots, not to individual items.

In simple words, sampling plans are the *judge-and-jury system* of industrial quality control. Instead of inspecting every single item (which is expensive and time-consuming), we inspect a small portion and use statistics to make a smart bet about the whole lot.

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — "The Mango Basket Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you buy a huge basket of 1,000 mangoes from a vendor. You cannot taste all 1,000. So you randomly pick **10 mangoes** and cut them open. If **more than 3 are rotten**, you reject the whole basket. If **3 or fewer are rotten**, you accept the basket. This rule (n=10, c=3) is your **Single Sampling Plan**.

**Key insight:** The mango rule is *sampling*. You did not 100% inspect; you *inferred* the lot quality from the sample.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Sampling Plans?

| Inspection Type | When Used | Limitation |
|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **100% Inspection** | Critical safety items (aircraft engines, pacemakers) | Costly, slow, inspector fatigue causes errors |
| **No Inspection** | Very cheap non-critical items | Risky, defective items reach customer |
| **Sampling Inspection** ✅ | Most mass-produced goods | Compromise between cost and risk |

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary — Must Memorize for KTU

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Lot Size | **N** | Total number of items in the batch |
| Sample Size | **n** | Number of items randomly drawn from lot |
| Acceptance Number | **c** | Maximum defects allowed to accept lot |
| Reject Number | — | > c defects → lot rejected |
| Probability of Acceptance | **P_a** | Chance of accepting a lot with fraction defective p |
| Operating Characteristic Curve | **OC Curve** | Graph of P_a vs p (incoming quality) |
| Producer's Risk | **α** | P(rejecting a good lot) — usually **5%** |
| Consumer's Risk | **β** | P(accepting a bad lot) — usually **10%** |
| Acceptable Quality Level | **AQL** | Quality level acceptable to producer |
| Lot Tolerance Percent Defective | **LTPD** | Worst quality consumer will tolerate |

## 1.5 Types of Sampling Plans (KTU Syllabus Map)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 3 mandates coverage of **all four** types below:
>
> 1. **Single Sampling Plan** — one sample decides
> 2. **Double Sampling Plan** — two chances to decide
> 3. **Multiple Sampling Plan** — several chances
> 4. **Sequential Sampling Plan** — inspect item-by-item until decision
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single Sampling Plan — The Logic Chain

A **Single Sampling Plan** is the simplest scheme. One random sample of size *n* is drawn. If defects *d ≤ c*, accept; if *d > c*, reject. The decision is **final**.

### Why Single Sampling?
- Easiest to administer
- Easy to compute P_a
- Foundation for all other plans

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Draw **n** items from lot of size **N** (usually N ≥ 10n)
2. Count number of defectives **d**
3. If **d ≤ c**, ACCEPT the lot
4. If **d > c**, REJECT the lot
5. If rejected → either return to supplier or **rectify** (100% inspect & replace defectives)

### Mathematical Foundation — The Hypergeometric / Binomial Distribution

If N is large (≥ 10n), binomial approximation is valid. The probability of finding exactly *x* defectives in a sample of size *n* when true fraction defective is *p* is:

$$
P(x) = \binom{n}{x} \cdot p^x \cdot (1-p)^{n-x}
$$

The **probability of acceptance** P_a is the sum of probabilities of all outcomes 0, 1, 2, ..., c:

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} \cdot p^x \cdot (1-p)^{n-x}
$$

This is the **cumulative binomial distribution function** — the heart of sampling plan analysis.

---

## 2.2 OC Curve — The Visual Decision Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve:** A plot of probability of acceptance (P_a) versus the incoming lot fraction defective (p).

| p Region | P_a Value | Interpretation |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| p = 0 (perfect lot) | P_a = 1.0 | Always accept |
| p = AQL | P_a = 1 - α ≈ 0.95 | High chance of acceptance (good lots) |
| p = LTPD | P_a = β ≈ 0.10 | Low chance of acceptance (bad lots) |
| p = 1.0 (all defective) | P_a = 0 | Always reject |

**Ideal OC curve:** A vertical step function at p = AQL (impossible in practice due to sampling variability).

---

## 2.3 Producer's Risk & Consumer's Risk

- **Producer's Risk (α)** = Probability of rejecting a lot that is at AQL.
  $$
  \alpha = 1 - P_a(\text{AQL})
  $$

- **Consumer's Risk (β)** = Probability of accepting a lot that is at LTPD.
  $$
  \beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})
  $$

The two points (AQL, 1-α) and (LTPD, β) define the "**risk corridor**" the OC curve must pass through.

---

## 2.4 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) & AOQL

After a rejected lot undergoes **rectifying inspection** (100% inspection + replacement of defectives), the average quality of all outgoing items is called **AOQ**.

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

Where:
- (N - n) = unsampled items going out as-is
- If lot accepted, all N items (including sample) ship out, containing np defectives on average

**Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL):** The **maximum** value of AOQ over all possible values of *p*. This is the worst-case outgoing quality.

$$
\text{AOQL} = \max_{0 \leq p \leq 1} \text{AOQ}(p)
$$

**Operational significance:** AOQL is a **guarantee** to the consumer — no matter how bad incoming quality gets, outgoing quality never exceeds AOQL.

---

## 2.5 Average Total Inspection (ATI)

Average number of items inspected **per lot** under rectifying scheme:

$$
\text{ATI} = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a)
$$

When P_a = 1 (all lots accepted): ATI = n (only sample inspected).
When P_a = 0 (all lots rejected): ATI = N (full rectifying inspection).

---

## 2.6 Double Sampling Plan — The Two-Chance System

A Double Sampling Plan involves **two samples and two acceptance numbers**.

**Parameters:**
- n₁ = first sample size
- c₁ = acceptance number for first sample
- c₂ = combined acceptance number (c₂ > c₁)
- n₂ = second sample size

### Decision Logic Chain
1. Draw n₁ items
2. If defectives d₁ ≤ c₁ → **ACCEPT**
3. If d₁ > c₂ → **REJECT**
4. If c₁ < d₁ ≤ c₂ → draw second sample of n₂ items
5. Combined defectives d₁ + d₂ ≤ c₂ → **ACCEPT**; else **REJECT**

### Probability of Acceptance Formula

$$
P_{a1} = \sum_{x=0}^{c_1} \binom{n_1}{x} \cdot p^x \cdot (1-p)^{n_1-x}
$$

$$
P_{r1} = \sum_{x=c_2+1}^{n_1} \binom{n_1}{x} \cdot p^x \cdot (1-p)^{n_1-x}
$$

$$
P_{1} = \sum_{x=c_1+1}^{c_2} \binom{n_1}{x} \cdot p^x \cdot (1-p)^{n_1-x}
$$

$$
P_{a2} = P_1 \cdot \sum_{y=0}^{c_2-x} \binom{n_2}{y} \cdot p^y \cdot (1-p)^{n_2-y}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2}}
$$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Formula | Where Used |
|---|---------|------------|
| 1 | $P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$ | Single sampling acceptance probability |
| 2 | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(\text{AQL})$ | Producer's risk |
| 3 | $\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})$ | Consumer's risk |
| 4 | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Average outgoing quality (rectifying) |
| 5 | $\text{AOQL} = \max \text{AOQ}(p)$ | Worst-case outgoing quality |
| 6 | $\text{ATI} = n P_a + N (1 - P_a)$ | Average inspection per lot |
| 7 | $P_a = P_{a1} + P_1 \cdot P_{a2}$ | Double sampling acceptance |
| 8 | $\text{ASN} = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_1$ | Average Sample Number (double plan) |
| 9 | $n = \dfrac{z_{\alpha} \cdot z_{\beta}}{p_1 - p_2} \cdot \sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}$ (approx.) | Sample size from AQL/LTPD |
| 10 | $\sigma_n^2 = np(1-p)$ | Variance of binomial count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Sampling plans are used in **incoming inspection** (raw materials), **in-process inspection** (sub-assemblies), and **final inspection** (finished goods). They form the backbone of **ISO 2859-1 / ANSI Z1.4** industrial standards.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility Across Industries

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| Automobile | Accepting batches of bolts, brake pads |
| Pharmaceutical | Tablet batch release |
| Electronics | PCB acceptance |
| Defence (MIL-STD) | Munitions quality assurance |
| Food Processing | Consignment acceptance |

---

## 2.9 Standard Sampling Plan Tables

> [!NOTE]
> **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 (formerly MIL-STD-105E)** provides ready-made tables with sample sizes and acceptance numbers for various combinations of lot size, AQL, and inspection level. KTU examiners often give parameters and ask students to **look up n and c from the table**, then **calculate P_a** using the binomial formula.

Inspection levels: **I, II, III, S-1, S-2, S-3, S-4** (Level II is most common).
Tightened and reduced inspection options exist for shift-pattern quality monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Single Sampling P_a Computation

**Problem:** A single sampling plan has n = 50, c = 2. If the incoming lot fraction defective is p = 0.04, calculate the probability of acceptance.

### Step 1: Identify parameters
- n = 50, c = 2, p = 0.04, q = 1 - p = 0.96

### Step 2: Apply binomial summation

$$
P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \binom{50}{x} (0.04)^x (0.96)^{50-x}
$$

$$
P_a = \binom{50}{0}(0.04)^0(0.96)^{50} + \binom{50}{1}(0.04)^1(0.96)^{49} + \binom{50}{2}(0.04)^2(0.96)^{48}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate each term

**Term 1:** x = 0

$$
T_1 = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot (0.96)^{50}
$$

$$
(0.96)^{50} = e^{50 \ln(0.96)} = e^{50 \times (-0.04082)} = e^{-2.0410} = 0.1300
$$

$$
T_1 = 0.1300
$$

**Term 2:** x = 1

$$
T_2 = 50 \cdot 0.04 \cdot (0.96)^{49}
$$

$$
(0.96)^{49} = \frac{0.1300}{0.96} = 0.1354
$$

$$
T_2 = 50 \times 0.04 \times 0.1354 = 0.2708
$$

**Term 3:** x = 2

$$
T_3 = \frac{50 \times 49}{2} \cdot (0.04)^2 \cdot (0.96)^{48}
$$

$$
\binom{50}{2} = 1225
$$

$$
(0.96)^{48} = \frac{0.1354}{0.96} = 0.1411
$$

$$
T_3 = 1225 \times 0.0016 \times 0.1411 = 0.2765
$$

### Step 4: Sum

$$
P_a = 0.1300 + 0.2708 + 0.2765 = 0.6773
$$

### Result
$$
\boxed{P_a(0.04) = 0.6773 \approx 67.73\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** With 4% defectives in incoming lots, this plan accepts about 68% of lots — not very protective for the consumer.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Designing a Plan from AQL & LTPD

**Problem:** Design a single sampling plan with **AQL = 1% (p₁ = 0.01)**, **LTPD = 5% (p₂ = 0.05)**, **α = 0.05**, **β = 0.10**.

### Step 1: Use normal approximation for large samples

For binomial approximation to normal:

$$
P_a(p) \approx \Phi\left(\frac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}}\right)
$$

### Step 2: Apply boundary conditions

At p = p₁ = 0.01, P_a = 0.95:
$$
\frac{c + 0.5 - 0.01n}{\sqrt{0.01 \times 0.99 \cdot n}} = z_{0.05} = 1.645
$$

At p = p₂ = 0.05, P_a = 0.10:
$$
\frac{c + 0.5 - 0.05n}{\sqrt{0.05 \times 0.95 \cdot n}} = z_{0.10} = -1.28
$$

### Step 3: Solve the system of equations

Let σ₁ = √(0.0099n) and σ₂ = √(0.0475n).

Equation A: c + 0.5 = 0.01n + 1.645 σ₁
Equation B: c + 0.5 = 0.05n - 1.28 σ₂

Subtract B - A:
$$
0 = 0.04n - 1.645 \sigma_1 - 1.28 \sigma_2
$$

$$
0.04n = 1.645 \sqrt{0.0099n} + 1.28 \sqrt{0.0475n}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical iteration

Assume n = 250:
- σ₁ = √(2.475) = 1.573
- σ₂ = √(11.875) = 3.446
- RHS = 1.645 × 1.573 + 1.28 × 3.446 = 2.588 + 4.411 = 6.999
- LHS = 0.04 × 250 = 10.000

Too high (LHS > RHS); try n = 200:
- σ₁ = √(1.98) = 1.407
- σ₂ = √(9.50) = 3.082
- RHS = 1.645 × 1.407 + 1.28 × 3.082 = 2.314 + 3.945 = 6.259
- LHS = 8.000

Try n = 175:
- σ₁ = √(1.7325) = 1.316
- σ₂ = √(8.3125) = 2.883
- RHS = 1.645 × 1.316 + 1.28 × 2.883 = 2.165 + 3.690 = 5.855
- LHS = 7.000

Try n = 165:
- σ₁ = √(1.6335) = 1.278
- σ₂ = √(7.8375) = 2.799
- RHS = 1.645 × 1.278 + 1.28 × 2.799 = 2.103 + 3.583 = 5.686
- LHS = 6.600

Try n = 155:
- σ₁ = √(1.5345) = 1.239
- σ₂ = √(7.3625) = 2.713
- RHS = 1.645 × 1.239 + 1.28 × 2.713 = 2.038 + 3.473 = 5.511
- LHS = 6.200

Try n = 145:
- LHS = 5.800, RHS = 5.336 → close enough

Try n = 150:
- σ₁ = 1.219, σ₂ = 2.669
- RHS = 1.645 × 1.219 + 1.28 × 2.669 = 2.005 + 3.416 = 5.421
- LHS = 6.000

Try n = 140:
- σ₁ = 1.177, σ₂ = 2.579
- RHS = 1.936 + 3.301 = 5.237
- LHS = 5.600

Converging to n ≈ 143:
- LHS ≈ 5.72, RHS ≈ 5.30 — accept n = 145.

### Step 5: Find acceptance number c

Using Equation A with n = 145:
$$
c + 0.5 = 0.01(145) + 1.645 \times 1.239 = 1.45 + 2.038 = 3.488
$$

$$
c = 2.988 \approx 3
$$

### Final Plan
$$
\boxed{n = 145,\ c = 3}
$$

Verify c = 3 satisfies: 0.01(145) + 1.645(1.239) = 1.45 + 2.04 = 3.49 ✓
Check: 0.05(145) - 1.28(2.713) = 7.25 - 3.47 = 3.78 ✓ (consistent)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — AOQ and AOQL Calculation

**Problem:** Single sampling plan n = 100, c = 2, lot size N = 2000, rectifying inspection. Find AOQ for p = 0.03 and the AOQL.

### Step 1: Calculate P_a at p = 0.03

$$
P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \binom{100}{x}(0.03)^x(0.97)^{100-x}
$$

Term 1: $(0.97)^{100} = e^{100 \ln 0.97} = e^{-3.048} = 0.04755$

Term 2: $100 \times 0.03 \times (0.97)^{99} = 3.0 \times 0.04902 = 0.14706$

Term 3: $\binom{100}{2} \times (0.03)^2 \times (0.97)^{98} = 4950 \times 0.0009 \times 0.05054 = 0.22516$

$$
P_a(0.03) = 0.04755 + 0.14706 + 0.22516 = 0.41977
$$

### Step 2: Calculate AOQ at p = 0.03

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} = \frac{0.41977 \times 0.03 \times (2000 - 100)}{2000}
$$

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.41977 \times 0.03 \times 1900}{2000} = \frac{23.926}{2000} = 0.01196
$$

So AOQ ≈ 1.20% at p = 0.03.

### Step 3: Calculate ATI at p = 0.03

$$
\text{ATI} = n P_a + N(1 - P_a) = 100 \times 0.41977 + 2000 \times 0.58023
$$

$$
\text{ATI} = 41.98 + 1160.46 = 1202.44 \text{ items per lot}
$$

### Step 4: AOQL Determination

Compute AOQ for multiple p values and find maximum:

| p | P_a | AOQ |
|---|-----|-----|
| 0.01 | 0.9203 | 0.00881 |
| 0.02 | 0.6767 | 0.01299 |
| 0.03 | 0.4198 | 0.01209 |
| 0.04 | 0.2388 | 0.00917 |
| 0.05 | 0.1247 | 0.00598 |

Maximum AOQ occurs near p ≈ 0.02, AOQL ≈ **1.30%**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Double Sampling Plan P_a

**Problem:** Double sampling plan with n₁ = 50, c₁ = 1, n₂ = 50, c₂ = 4. Compute P_a at p = 0.02.

### Step 1: P_a1 (accept on first sample)

$$
P_{a1} = \sum_{x=0}^{1}\binom{50}{x}(0.02)^x(0.98)^{50-x}
$$

Term 0: $(0.98)^{50} = e^{-1.010} = 0.3642$

Term 1: $50 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{49} = 1.0 \times 0.3716 = 0.3716$

$$
P_{a1} = 0.3642 + 0.3716 = 0.7358
$$

### Step 2: P_r1 (reject on first sample)

$$
P_{r1} = \sum_{x=5}^{50}\binom{50}{x}(0.02)^x(0.98)^{50-x} = 1 - \sum_{x=0}^{4}\binom{50}{x}(0.02)^x(0.98)^{50-x}
$$

After computing terms for x = 0,1,2,3,4: sum = 0.7358 + 0.1846 + 0.0456 + 0.00751 + 0.000926 ≈ 0.9744

$$
P_{r1} = 1 - 0.9744 = 0.0256
$$

### Step 3: P_1 (go to second sample — i.e., 2 ≤ d₁ ≤ 4)

$$
P_1 = 0.1846 + 0.0456 + 0.00751 + 0.000926 = 0.2387
$$

Check: P_a1 + P_1 + P_r1 = 0.7358 + 0.2387 + 0.0256 = 1.0001 ≈ 1.0 ✓

### Step 4: P_a2 (accept after second sample)

Given d₁ = x (where 2 ≤ x ≤ 4), probability of d₁+d₂ ≤ 4 is:

$$
P_{a2} = \sum_{x=2}^{4} \binom{50}{x}(0.02)^x(0.98)^{50-x} \cdot \sum_{y=0}^{4-x} \binom{50}{y}(0.02)^y(0.98)^{50-y}
$$

Computing each combination:
- x=2: P(x=2) = 0.1846, P(y≤2) = 0.7358, product = 0.1358
- x=3: P(x=3) = 0.00751, P(y≤1) = 0.7358, product = 0.00552
- x=4: P(x=4) = 0.000926, P(y≤0) = 0.3642, product = 0.000337

$$
P_{a2} = 0.1358 + 0.00552 + 0.000337 = 0.1417
$$

### Step 5: Total P_a

$$
\boxed{P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2} = 0.7358 + 0.1417 = 0.8775}
$$

### Step 6: Average Sample Number (ASN)

$$
\text{ASN} = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_1 = 50 + 50 \times 0.2387 = 50 + 11.94 = 61.94
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, only 62 items are inspected (less than single plan n = 100), yet 87.75% of 2% defective lots are accepted.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table

| Metric | Single Plan (n=100, c=2) | Double Plan (n₁=n₂=50, c₁=1, c₂=4) |
|--------|--------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| P_a at p = 0.02 | ~0.677 | 0.878 |
| ASN at p = 0.02 | 100 | 61.94 |
| ASN at p = 0 (good lots) | 100 | 50 |
| Admin complexity | Low | Medium |
| Information used | One sample | Up to two samples |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Double plans save inspection effort when incoming quality is good, but require more computation. KTU often asks students to **prove** the ASN formula.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow — Acceptance Sampling Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot Arrives N items] --> B[Draw Random Sample of Size n]
    B --> C[Inspect Each Item]
    C --> D{Number of Defects d}
    D -->|d ≤ c| E[ACCEPT LOT]
    D -->|d > c| F[REJECT LOT]
    E --> G[Ship All N Items to Customer]
    F --> H{Choose Action}
    H --> I[Return to Supplier]
    H --> J[Rectifying Inspection]
    J --> K[100% Inspect Replace Defectives]
    K --> G
    G --> L[Calculate AOQ ATI Feedback to Vendor]
```

## 4.2 Single vs Double vs Multiple Sampling Plan Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[Single Sampling Plan]
        SP1[Draw n items] --> SP2{d ≤ c?}
        SP2 -->|Yes| SP3[ACCEPT]
        SP2 -->|No| SP4[REJECT]
    end

    subgraph DP[Double Sampling Plan]
        DP1[Draw n1 items] --> DP2{d1 ≤ c1?}
        DP2 -->|Yes| DP3[ACCEPT]
        DP2 -->|No| DP4{d1 > c2?}
        DP4 -->|Yes| DP5[REJECT]
        DP4 -->|No| DP6[Draw n2 items]
        DP6 --> DP7{d1+d2 ≤ c2?}
        DP7 -->|Yes| DP8[ACCEPT]
        DP7 -->|No| DP9[REJECT]
    end

    subgraph MP[Multiple Sampling Plan]
        MP1[Stage 1 n1 c1] --> MP2[Decision]
        MP2 -->|Accept| MP3[ACCEPT]
        MP2 -->|Reject| MP4[REJECT]
        MP2 -->|Continue| MP5[Stage 2 n2 c2]
        MP5 --> MP2
    end
```

## 4.3 OC Curve — The Decision Map (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Incoming Quality p] --> Y[OC Curve P_a function]
    Y --> Z[Decision Region]
    Z --> Z1[Accept Region P_a near 1]
    Z --> Z2[Indifference Region around AQL]
    Z --> Z3[Reject Region P_a near 0]
    Z1 -.->|Producer happy| A[Good Lots Pass]
    Z2 -.->|Risk alpha| B[Producer Risk Area]
    Z3 -.->|Consumer protected| C[Bad Lots Rejected]
```

## 4.4 Sampling Plan Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Cost of Inspection per Item] --> Q2{Compared to Defect Cost}
    Q2 -->|Inspection Cheap| R1[Use 100% Inspection]
    Q2 -->|Inspection Expensive| R2[Use Sampling]
    R2 --> S1{Destructive Test?}
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Forced to Sample]
    S1 -->|No| S3[Sampling Optional]
    S3 --> T1{Lot Size N}
    T1 -->|Small| U1[Single Plan]
    T1 -->|Medium| U2[Double Plan]
    T1 -->|Large| U3[Multiple Sequential Plan]
```

## 4.5 AOQ Curve vs OC Curve — Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming p] --> B[OC Curve P_a]
    B --> C[AOQ = P_a x p x N-n / N]
    C --> D[AOQ Curve Peak = AOQL]
    D --> E[Consumer Guarantee Maximum Defect Level]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define acceptance sampling. List its advantages over 100% inspection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Acceptance sampling is a statistical quality control technique in which a random sample is drawn from a lot, inspected, and a decision to accept or reject the entire lot is made based on the number of defectives found in the sample.

**Advantages over 100% inspection (2 marks):**
1. **Economical** — Less inspection time, lower labor cost
2. **Suitable for destructive testing** — e.g., testing life of bulbs, explosive items
3. **Reduces inspector fatigue** — leading to fewer errors
4. **Fewer items handled** — less chance of damage during inspection
5. **Applicable to bulk homogeneous lots** — statistical inference is reliable

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**What is an OC curve? Explain the terms AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk, and Consumer's Risk.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** is a graphical plot of the probability of acceptance (P_a) of a lot versus the incoming lot fraction defective (p). It describes the discriminatory power of a sampling plan.

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** The maximum percentage of defectives that is considered tolerable as a process average. Usually P_a = 1 - α at AQL.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** The worst quality level a consumer is willing to tolerate. Usually P_a = β at LTPD.
- **Producer's Risk (α):** Probability of rejecting a good lot (at AQL). Typically 0.05.
- **Consumer's Risk (β):** Probability of accepting a bad lot (at LTPD). Typically 0.10.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the procedure of a **Single Sampling Plan** with a neat flowchart. For a single sampling plan with n = 80, c = 2, and incoming fraction defective p = 0.05, calculate the probability of acceptance.

**(b) [7 marks]** Define **AOQ and AOQL**. For a single sampling plan with n = 50, c = 1, N = 1000, find the AOQ at p = 0.04 and the AOQL approximately.

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**Procedure:** (3 marks)
1. Lot of N items arrives
2. Random sample of n items drawn
3. Inspect sample → count defects d
4. If d ≤ c → ACCEPT lot
5. If d > c → REJECT lot

**Calculation:** (4 marks)

$$
P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{2}\binom{80}{x}(0.05)^x(0.95)^{80-x}
$$

Term x=0: $(0.95)^{80} = e^{80 \ln 0.95} = e^{-4.105} = 0.01650$

Term x=1: $80 \times 0.05 \times (0.95)^{79} = 4.0 \times 0.01737 = 0.06948$

Term x=2: $\binom{80}{2}(0.05)^2(0.95)^{78} = 3160 \times 0.0025 \times 0.01829 = 0.1445$

$$
P_a = 0.01650 + 0.06948 + 0.1445 = 0.2305 \approx 23.05\%
$$

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**Definitions:** (2 marks)

**AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality)** is the expected fraction defective in outgoing product after rectifying inspection of rejected lots.

**AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit)** is the maximum value of AOQ over all possible values of p.

**Calculation:** (5 marks)

First compute P_a at p = 0.04:

$$
P_a(0.04) = \sum_{x=0}^{1}\binom{50}{x}(0.04)^x(0.96)^{50-x}
$$

Term x=0: $(0.96)^{50} = 0.1299$
Term x=1: $50 \times 0.04 \times (0.96)^{49} = 2.0 \times 0.1353 = 0.2707$

$$
P_a(0.04) = 0.1299 + 0.2707 = 0.4006
$$

AOQ at p = 0.04:

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.4006 \times 0.04 \times (1000-50)}{1000} = \frac{0.4006 \times 0.04 \times 950}{1000} = 0.01522
$$

So AOQ ≈ 1.52%.

For AOQL, compute AOQ at various p:

| p | P_a | AOQ |
|---|-----|-----|
| 0.01 | 0.9197 | 0.00873 |
| 0.02 | 0.7357 | 0.01398 |
| 0.03 | 0.5553 | 0.01582 |
| 0.04 | 0.4006 | 0.01522 |
| 0.05 | 0.2779 | 0.01320 |

Maximum AOQ occurs at p ≈ 0.03, so **AOQL ≈ 1.58%**.

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 marks]** What is a **Double Sampling Plan**? Explain its operation with a flowchart. For a double sampling plan with n₁ = 60, c₁ = 1, n₂ = 60, c₂ = 3, p = 0.03, compute the probability of acceptance.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the concept of **Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk** with a neat diagram. A sampling plan has n = 100, c = 3. Compute α when AQL = 0.02 and β when LTPD = 0.08.

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition & Flow:** (3 marks)

A double sampling plan uses two samples and two acceptance numbers (c₁ and c₂, c₂ > c₁). The first sample of n₁ is drawn; if d₁ ≤ c₁, the lot is accepted; if d₁ > c₂, it is rejected. If c₁ < d₁ ≤ c₂, a second sample of n₂ is drawn and combined defects decide.

**Calculation:** (4 marks)

With n₁ = 60, c₁ = 1, n₂ = 60, c₂ = 3, p = 0.03, q = 0.97.

P_a1 (accept on first sample):

$$
P_{a1} = \sum_{x=0}^{1}\binom{60}{x}(0.03)^x(0.97)^{60-x}
$$

Term 0: $(0.97)^{60} = e^{-1.836} = 0.1592$
Term 1: $60 \times 0.03 \times (0.97)^{59} = 1.8 \times 0.1641 = 0.2954$

$$
P_{a1} = 0.1592 + 0.2954 = 0.4546
$$

P_1 (continue to second sample — 2 ≤ d₁ ≤ 3):

Term x=2: $\binom{60}{2}(0.03)^2(0.97)^{58} = 1770 \times 0.0009 \times 0.1692 = 0.2695$

Term x=3: $\binom{60}{3}(0.03)^3(0.97)^{57} = 34220 \times 0.000027 \times 0.1745 = 0.1612$

$$
P_1 = 0.2695 + 0.1612 = 0.4307
$$

P_a2 (accept after second sample):

For x = 2 (need d₂ ≤ 1):
$$
P(\text{accept} \mid x=2) = \sum_{y=0}^{1}\binom{60}{y}(0.03)^y(0.97)^{60-y} = 0.4546
$$
Contribution: 0.2695 × 0.4546 = 0.1225

For x = 3 (need d₂ ≤ 0):
$$
P(\text{accept} \mid x=3) = (0.97)^{60} = 0.1592
$$
Contribution: 0.1612 × 0.1592 = 0.02566

$$
P_{a2} = 0.1225 + 0.02566 = 0.1482
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a = 0.4546 + 0.1482 = 0.6028 \approx 60.28\%}
$$

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**Concept with Diagram:** (3 marks)

- **Producer's Risk (α):** Probability that a good lot (at AQL) gets rejected by the sampling plan. This hurts the producer.
- **Consumer's Risk (β):** Probability that a poor lot (at LTPD) gets accepted by the sampling plan. This hurts the consumer.

```
P_a
1.0 |■■■■■■■■■■■■■
    |              ■
0.95|               ■  ← 1 - α at AQL (Producer's Risk α = 5%)
    |                 ■
    |                   ■■■
    |                       ■■■■■■
0.10|                              ■■■■■■  ← β at LTPD (Consumer's Risk β = 10%)
    |                                     ■■■■■■■■■
0.0 |________________________________________________________
    0          AQL=0.02                LTPD=0.08            1.0
                                              Incoming p
```

**Calculation:** (4 marks)

At AQL = 0.02, p = 0.02, n = 100, c = 3:

$$
P_a(0.02) = \sum_{x=0}^{3}\binom{100}{x}(0.02)^x(0.98)^{100-x}
$$

Term 0: $(0.98)^{100} = e^{-2.020} = 0.1326$
Term 1: $100 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{99} = 2 \times 0.1353 = 0.2706$
Term 2: $\binom{100}{2}(0.02)^2(0.98)^{98} = 4950 \times 0.0004 \times 0.1381 = 0.2734$
Term 3: $\binom{100}{3}(0.02)^3(0.98)^{97} = 161700 \times 0.000008 \times 0.1409 = 0.1823$

$$
P_a(0.02) = 0.1326 + 0.2706 + 0.2734 + 0.1823 = 0.8589
$$

$$
\alpha = 1 - 0.8589 = 0.1411 \approx 14.11\%
$$

At LTPD = 0.08, p = 0.08, n = 100, c = 3:

$$
P_a(0.08) = \sum_{x=0}^{3}\binom{100}{x}(0.08)^x(0.92)^{100-x}
$$

Term 0: $(0.92)^{100} = e^{-8.339} = 0.000238$
Term 1: $100 \times 0.08 \times 0.000259 = 0.002072$
Term 2: $4950 \times 0.0064 \times 0.000281 = 0.00890$
Term 3: $161700 \times 0.000512 \times 0.000305 = 0.02526$

$$
P_a(0.08) = 0.000238 + 0.002072 + 0.00890 + 0.02526 = 0.03647
$$

$$
\beta = 0.0365 \approx 3.65\%
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the (N-n)/N factor** in AOQ — full 2-mark penalty
> 2. **Mixing up α and β** — α = 1 - P_a(AQL); β = P_a(LTPD). Reversing these gives wrong numerical answer.
> 3. **Ignoring double sampling P_1 term** — many students compute only P_a1 and skip the second sample acceptance contribution.
> 4. **Rounding too early** — keep at least 4 decimal places during intermediate steps to avoid 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not labeling OC curve axes** — always write "P_a" on Y-axis and "Fraction Defective p" on X-axis.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Acceptance Sampling** = Lot decision based on sample inspection (not 100% check)
- [x] **Single Plan parameters:** n (sample size), c (acceptance number)
- [x] **Double Plan parameters:** n₁, c₁, n₂, c₂ where c₂ > c₁
- [x] **Probability of acceptance** = Cumulative binomial sum from x = 0 to c
- [x] **OC Curve** = Graph of P_a vs p — the central tool of sampling plan analysis
- [x] **AQL** → High P_a (good protection for producer); **LTPD** → Low P_a (good protection for consumer)
- [x] **Producer's Risk α** = 1 - P_a(AQL); **Consumer's Risk β** = P_a(LTPD)
- [x] **AOQ Formula:** $\dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ (rectifying inspection)
- [x] **AOQL** = Maximum of AOQ over all p; provides outgoing quality guarantee
- [x] **ATI Formula:** $nP_a + N(1-P_a)$ = average inspection per lot
- [x] **ASN for double plan:** $n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_1$ — reflects inspection saving
- [x] **ANSI Z1.4 / MIL-STD-105E** — the most-used standard tables worldwide
- [x] **Double & Multiple plans save inspection** when p is small (good lots)
- [x] **Sequential plans** are the most efficient but operationally complex
- [x] **Hypergeometric → Binomial** when N ≥ 10n (always check this)
- [x] **Normal approximation** to binomial valid when np ≥ 5 and n(1-p) ≥ 5
- [x] **Rectifying inspection** = 100% inspection after rejection, replace defectives
- [x] **Inspection Levels** in standards: I, II, III (II = default), S-1 to S-4 for special cases
- [x] **Tightened inspection** when 2 of 5 consecutive lots rejected; **Reduced** when criteria met
- [x] **Variables sampling plans** use measured values (mean, standard deviation) instead of defect counts — more information per item but more complex
- [x] **Lot-by-lot acceptance** is the standard KTU expectation; ignore continuous lot chains in exam problems

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In ESE, sampling plan problems almost always come with given values of n, c, N, and p. You must (a) compute P_a via binomial sum, (b) draw/comment on OC curve, and (c) calculate AOQ/AOQL/ATI as required. Practice the binomial coefficient expansions — calculators are permitted but the formula must be on paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Charts and Their Applications - Life Testing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Charts and Their Applications - Life Testing

# Life Testing and Control Charts for Reliability

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Life Testing** is a systematic, time-based quality control procedure used to evaluate the **reliability**, **durability**, and **survival characteristics** of products, components, or systems by subjecting them to operating conditions (normal or accelerated) and recording the time-to-failure data. It forms the empirical backbone of **reliability engineering** and is statistically governed by life distributions such as the **Exponential**, **Weibull**, **Lognormal**, and **Gamma** distributions.

In KTU 2024 parlance, life testing is the **statistical methodology that bridges quality control (control charts) and reliability engineering**, where the variable of interest is the *time to failure* (TTF) rather than the conventional *measurable quality characteristic*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you manufacture **one lakh LED bulbs** and want to certify that *"95 out of every 100 bulbs will last at least 1000 hours."* You obviously cannot test all one lakh bulbs for 1000 hours — it is **too expensive and too slow**. So, you pick a small sample (say 50 bulbs), switch them on, and note the time at which each fails.

- If many fail early → your process is **out of control** → red flag.
- If they fail in a pattern consistent with a known statistical model (Exponential/Weibull) → your process is **in control** → green flag.

This entire exercise — drawing a sample, testing it till failure, plotting failures over time, and drawing **control limits** — is *Life Testing with Control Charts*. Think of it as the **"patience test"** of quality control: instead of asking *"is the dimension within tolerance?"*, you ask *"does the product survive long enough?"*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Callout

> [!NOTE]
> - **Reliability (R(t))**: Probability that a product performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time.
> - **Hazard Rate (λ(t))**: Instantaneous rate of failure at time `t`, given survival up to time `t`.
> - **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)**: Expected (mean) lifetime of a non-repairable product.
> - **Censoring**: Termination of a test before all specimens have failed.
> - **Accelerated Life Testing (ALT)**: Testing at elevated stress levels to induce early failure.

### 1.4 Why Life Testing is a Necessary Extension of Control Charts

Conventional Shewhart control charts (x̄, R, p, c, np) monitor **process variability** at a single point in time. They cannot answer: *"Will this product still be working next year?"* Life testing answers exactly that — by monitoring the **time dimension of quality**.

| Aspect | Conventional Control Charts | Life Testing Control Charts |
|---|---|---|
| Variable monitored | Dimension, weight, defect count | Time-to-failure |
| Time frame | Snapshot | Longitudinal (hours, cycles) |
| Sample | n units at random | n units run till failure |
| Output | Control limits on statistic | Control limits on failure time / count |
| Distribution | Normal / Poisson / Binomial | Exponential / Weibull / Lognormal |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Reliability Functions

Let **T** be a non-negative continuous random variable representing the *time to failure*. The life of a product is fully described by:

#### 2.1.1 Probability Density Function (PDF)

$$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$

This represents the *instantaneous likelihood of failure at time `t`*.

#### 2.1.2 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) — *Unreliability*

$$F(t) = P(T \le t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(u)\, du$$

#### 2.1.3 Reliability Function (Survival Function)

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(u)\, du$$

This is the **most important** metric in life testing — the probability that a unit **survives** beyond time `t`.

#### 2.1.4 Hazard Rate (Failure Rate Function)

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

This is the *conditional probability density of failure at time `t`*, given survival up to `t`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bathtub Curve** — A classic reliability concept. The hazard rate `h(t)` over the life of a product exhibits three regions:
> 1. **Infant Mortality** (decreasing h): Early failures due to manufacturing defects.
> 2. **Useful Life** (constant h): Random failures — described well by Exponential distribution.
> 3. **Wear-out** (increasing h): Aging-related failures — described well by Weibull distribution.

### 2.2 Major Life Distributions Used in KTU Life Testing

#### 2.2.1 Exponential Distribution

**When to use:** Constant hazard rate, memoryless property, useful life phase.

| Function | Formula |
|---|---|
| PDF | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0$ |
| Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| Hazard Rate | $h(t) = \lambda \quad (\text{constant})$ |
| MTTF | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ |
| Variance | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property**: $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$. A bulb that has already survived 500 hours has the *same* probability of lasting the next 100 hours as a brand new bulb. This is a **defining feature** of the Exponential distribution.

#### 2.2.2 Weibull Distribution

**When to use:** Wear-out failures, increasing/decreasing hazard rates, most general life model.

| Function | Formula |
|---|---|
| PDF | $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ |
| Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ |
| CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ |
| Hazard Rate | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ |
| MTTF | $\alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ |

Where:
- **α (alpha) > 0** is the **scale parameter** (characteristic life — 63.2% of units fail by this time).
- **β (beta) > 0** is the **shape parameter** (controls the shape of hazard rate).
  - β < 1 → decreasing hazard (infant mortality)
  - β = 1 → constant hazard (reduces to Exponential)
  - β > 1 → increasing hazard (wear-out)

#### 2.2.3 Lognormal Distribution

**When to use:** Failure times caused by multiplicative random effects (corrosion, crack growth).

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{t \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(\ln t - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

$$R(t) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$$

Where Φ(·) is the standard normal CDF.

### 2.3 Types of Life Testing Procedures

#### 2.3.1 Complete Life Test
All `n` units are run until every single unit fails. Provides complete information but is **expensive and time-consuming**.

#### 2.3.2 Censored Life Test
Test is terminated before all units fail — necessary in practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Principal Censoring Schemes (Highly KTU-Favoured):**
>
> **Type I Censoring (Time-Terminated):**
> - Test is run for a **pre-fixed time** `T₀`.
> - Number of failures `r` observed is **random**.
> - If total failures `r ≤ c` (acceptance number) → accept the lot.
> - If `r > c` → reject the lot.
> - **Use case:** When test time is constrained.
>
> **Type II Censoring (Failure-Terminated):**
> - Test is run until a **pre-fixed number of failures** `r` occur.
> - Time `T_r` at which the `r`th failure occurs is **random**.
> - If `T_r ≤ T₀` → reject; if `T_r > T₀` → accept.
> - **Use case:** When sample size is limited and you want fixed statistical power.

### 2.4 Control Charts for Life Testing

The fundamental philosophy of control charts — **plot a statistic, compare with 3σ limits, signal when out of control** — is extended to life data. Three principal approaches exist:

#### 2.4.1 Attribute Life Testing Control Chart (c-Chart for Failures)

When `n` identical units are put on test and the number of failures `c` in successive time intervals is counted.

| Parameter | Formula |
|---|---|
| Center Line (CL) | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{\text{Total failures}}{\text{Number of intervals}}$ |
| Upper Control Limit (UCL) | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |
| Lower Control Limit (LCL) | $\max\!\left(0, \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}\right)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> This is **mathematically identical** to the c-chart from Module 2, but is **re-interpreted** as a *life test chart* by plotting failures per unit time. The Poisson assumption (rare, independent events in a time interval) naturally fits failure processes.

#### 2.4.2 Fraction Defective Control Chart (p-Chart for Reliability)

Used when the test is *failure-terminated* and we record whether each unit survived beyond a specified mission time `t₀`.

| Parameter | Formula |
|---|---|
| CL | $\bar{p} = 1 - R(t_0)$ (estimated failure probability at mission time) |
| UCL | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ |
| LCL | $\max\!\left(0, \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}\right)$ |

#### 2.4.3 Time-Based Control Chart (X̄ Chart on Time-to-Failure)

Used when **time-to-failure** data is approximately Normally distributed (often the *logarithm* of TTF is normal — i.e., Lognormal life):

| Parameter | Formula |
|---|---|
| CL | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{\sum T_i}{r}$ (mean of failed items) |
| UCL | $\bar{T} + 3\,\dfrac{\sigma_T}{\sqrt{r}}$ |
| LCL | $\bar{T} - 3\,\dfrac{\sigma_T}{\sqrt{r}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> If raw TTF is skewed, use **ln(T)** transformation to Normalize before applying X̄ chart.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential |
| 2 | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ | Most general |
| 3 | MTTF (Exponential) | $1/\lambda$ | Easy to remember |
| 4 | MTTF (Weibull) | $\alpha\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | Gamma function |
| 5 | Hazard Rate | $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ | Instantaneous failure rate |
| 6 | c-Chart CL | $\bar{c}$ | Mean failures per interval |
| 7 | c-Chart UCL | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | 3-sigma limits |
| 8 | p-Chart CL | $1 - R(t_0)$ | Failure prob at mission time |
| 9 | p-Chart UCL | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | Sample size n |
| 10 | Time-Chart CL | $\bar{T}$ | Mean TTF |
| 11 | Time-Chart UCL | $\bar{T} + 3\sigma_T/\sqrt{r}$ | r failures observed |
| 12 | Type I: Prob. Accept | $\sum_{k=0}^{c} \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$ | Binomial |
| 13 | Type II: Prob. Accept | $\sum_{k=0}^{c} \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$ | Same formula, different test plan |
| 14 | OC Curve abscissa | $p_1/p_2$ | Producer's vs Consumer's risk |
| 15 | LTPD Ratio (c=0) | $1 - \text{LTPD}^{1/n}$ | Quick reliability test design |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Industry**: Life testing of capacitors, ICs, LEDs to predict field failure rates.
- **Automotive**: Brake pad, tyre, battery endurance life tests.
- **Aerospace**: Accelerated life testing of turbine blades, avionics.
- **Medical Devices**: Implant life testing (pacemakers, stents) — regulatory requirement.
- **Telecommunications**: MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) estimation for routers and servers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: MTTF of the Exponential Distribution

**Given:** PDF of exponential distribution: $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ for $t \ge 0$.

**Find:** MTTF = E[T].

**Step 1: Set up the expectation integral.**

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 2: Apply integration by parts.**

Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$. Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$E[T] = \left[-t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the boundary term.**

As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ faster than $t \to \infty$, so the boundary term is 0. At $t = 0$, the term is also 0. Hence the boundary contribution vanishes.

$$E[T] = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the remaining integral.**

$$E[T] = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \left(0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{\text{Exp}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

**Step 5: Variance derivation (quick).**

$$E[T^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^2 \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{2}{\lambda^2}$$

$$\text{Var}(T) = E[T^2] - (E[T])^2 = \frac{2}{\lambda^2} - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(T)_{\text{Exp}} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}}$$

This is a famous result: **standard deviation equals the mean** for the Exponential distribution.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Weibull Reliability from Hazard Rate Integration

**Given:** Hazard rate $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$

**Find:** Reliability function $R(t)$.

**Step 1: Use the fundamental relationship.**

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$$

**Step 2: Integrate both sides from 0 to t.**

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\, du = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

**Step 3: Apply initial condition R(0) = 1, so ln R(0) = 0.**

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\, du = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{u}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} du$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral using substitution** $z = (u/\alpha)^{\beta}$, $dz = (\beta/\alpha)(u/\alpha)^{\beta-1} du$.

$$\ln R(t) = -z \Big|_{0}^{(t/\alpha)^{\beta}} = -\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate.**

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}}$$

**Step 6: Obtain the PDF by differentiating.**

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Control Limits for the Time-Terminated Life Test Chart

**Given:** `n` units are tested for a fixed time `T₀`. Let `c` = number of failures observed.

**Step 1: Model each unit's outcome as Bernoulli.**

Each unit either fails (with probability $p = 1 - R(t_0)$) or survives (with probability $R(t_0)$) during time $t_0$.

**Step 2: Number of failures `c` follows Binomial distribution.**

$$P(C = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$$

**Step 3: For large n and small p, use Poisson approximation.**

$$C \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda = np)$$

**Step 4: For the control chart, the central line is the long-run average failure count.**

$$\bar{c} = np$$

**Step 5: Apply the 3-sigma rule. For Poisson, mean = variance = np.**

Standard deviation of `c` = $\sqrt{np}$.

$$\text{UCL} = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = np + 3\sqrt{np}$$

$$\text{LCL} = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = np - 3\sqrt{np}$$

**Step 6: LCL cannot be negative — apply truncation.**

$$\text{LCL} = \max\!\left(0,\ np - 3\sqrt{np}\right)$$

**Step 7: Worked Numerical Example.**

Suppose 100 LED bulbs are tested for 1000 hours. The historical failure rate is 0.02 (i.e., 2% fail by 1000 hours). Compute control limits.

$$\bar{c} = np = 100 \times 0.02 = 2$$

$$\text{UCL} = 2 + 3\sqrt{2} = 2 + 4.243 = 6.243 \approx 6$$

$$\text{LCL} = 2 - 3\sqrt{2} = 2 - 4.243 = -2.243 \to 0$$

If a new batch shows 8 failures in 1000 hours, it is **out of control** — the reliability has degraded.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: MTTF of Weibull Distribution (For β Integer or via Gamma Function)

**Given:** $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$

**Step 1: Set up the expectation.**

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}} dt$$

**Step 2: Substitute** $u = (t/\alpha)^{\beta}$, so $t = \alpha u^{1/\beta}$, and $dt = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta) - 1} du$.

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha u^{1/\beta} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \cdot u^{(\beta-1)/\beta} \cdot e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta) - 1} du$$

**Step 3: Simplify the exponents.**

$$u^{1/\beta} \cdot u^{(\beta-1)/\beta} \cdot u^{(1/\beta)-1} = u^{(1 + \beta - 1 + 1 - \beta)/\beta} = u^{1/\beta}$$

**Step 4: Combine constants.**

$$E[T] = \alpha \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/\beta} e^{-u} du = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{\text{Weibull}} = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$

**Step 5: Numerical examples for different β values.**

| β | Γ(1 + 1/β) | MTTF (in units of α) | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | Γ(3) = 2! = 2 | 2.000 α | Infant mortality dominated |
| 1.0 | Γ(2) = 1! = 1 | 1.000 α | Reduces to Exponential |
| 2.0 | Γ(1.5) ≈ 0.886 | 0.886 α | Moderate wear-out |
| 3.0 | Γ(4/3) ≈ 0.893 | 0.893 α | Strong wear-out |
| 5.0 | Γ(1.2) ≈ 0.918 | 0.918 α | Rapid wear-out |

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Life Test Chart

| Situation | Data Type | Sample Size | Recommended Chart | Distribution Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Counting failures in time intervals | Attribute (counts) | Large (n ≥ 50) | c-chart | Poisson |
| Recording pass/fail at mission time | Attribute (binary) | Any | p-chart | Binomial |
| Recording exact failure times | Variable (continuous) | Small to moderate | X̄ on T or ln(T) | Normal or Lognormal |
| Small sample, Weibull-shaped data | Variable | n < 30 | Weibull probability plot + control limits | Weibull |
| Highly reliable product, few failures expected | Attribute (rare events) | Large | c-chart with tight limits | Poisson |
| Field data with censoring | Censored variable | Any | Likelihood-based control limits | Weibull / Exponential |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** A manufacturer claims that bearings have a mean life of 5000 hours with a constant failure rate. A sample of 25 bearings is put on test. During a 1000-hour mission time, 4 bearings failed.

**(a)** Construct a 3σ control chart for this life test using the claimed reliability. Comment on the state of control.

**(b)** If the process shifts such that the new failure probability becomes 0.20, what is the probability of the chart *not* detecting this shift (i.e., the Type II error β) on the first sample?

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Compute claimed failure probability at 1000 hours.**

For exponential with MTTF = 5000 hours, $\lambda = 1/5000 = 0.0002$ per hour.

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

$$p = 1 - R(1000) = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813$$

**Step 2: Compute control limits for the c-chart.**

$$\bar{c} = np = 25 \times 0.1813 = 4.53$$

$$\text{UCL} = 4.53 + 3\sqrt{4.53} = 4.53 + 6.39 = 10.92$$

$$\text{LCL} = 4.53 - 3\sqrt{4.53} = 4.53 - 6.39 = -1.86 \to 0$$

**Step 3: Decision.**

Observed failures = 4. Since $0 \le 4 \le 10.92$, the process is **in statistical control**.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: New failure probability** $p_1 = 0.20$. Expected failures = $np_1 = 25 \times 0.20 = 5$.

**Step 2: Process is declared "out of control" if failures exceed UCL = 10.92, which rounds to 11.**

**Step 3: Compute probability of acceptance under shifted process.**

$$P(\text{accept} \mid p_1) = \sum_{k=0}^{10} \binom{25}{k} (0.20)^{k} (0.80)^{25-k}$$

**Step 4: Using binomial tables or calculation:**

$$P(C \le 10) = P(C \le 10 \mid n=25, p=0.20) \approx 0.9944$$

$$\beta \approx 0.9944$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

The probability of *not* detecting this quality shift is ~99.4% — indicating the chart is **very insensitive** to this level of shift. This demonstrates the well-known **insensitivity of attribute charts to small parameter shifts** — a key reason for the use of variable (X̄ on T) charts or CUSUM/EWMA charts in life testing.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Life Testing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need to assess product reliability] --> B{Type of product?}
    B -->|Non-repairable| C[Record Time-to-Failure T]
    B -->|Repairable| D[Record Time-Between-Failures]
    C --> E{Sample size and censoring?}
    E -->|Complete test| F[All n units fail]
    E -->|Time-terminated| G[Type I: Fixed T0, variable r]
    E -->|Failure-terminated| H[Type II: Fixed r, variable Tr]
    F --> I{Distribution fit?}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|Exponential| J[Use Exponential model: lambda constant]
    I -->|Weibull| K[Use Weibull model: alpha, beta]
    I -->|Lognormal| L[Use Lognormal model: mu, sigma]
    J --> M{Chart type?}
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M -->|Count failures| N[c-chart: CL=nc-bar, UCL+3-sigma]
    M -->|Pass/Fail at T0| O[p-chart: CL=p-bar, UCL+3-sigma]
    M -->|Record exact T| P[X-bar chart on T or lnT]
    N --> Q{Points within limits?}
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q -->|Yes| R[Process IN CONTROL: Accept lot]
    Q -->|No| S[Process OUT OF CONTROL: Investigate]
    S --> T[Root cause analysis and corrective action]
    T --> U[Re-test and verify]
    U --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Components of a Life Testing Control Chart System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Product Sample n units] --> A2[Define Mission Time t0]
        A2 --> A3[Select Stress Conditions]
    end
    subgraph TEST[Testing Stage]
        B1[Run Life Test] --> B2[Monitor Failures]
        B2 --> B3[Record Time-to-Failure Data]
        B3 --> B4{Censoring?}
        B4 -->|No| B5[Complete Data]
        B4 -->|Yes| B6[Censored Data]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS[Statistical Analysis Stage]
        C1[Choose Distribution] --> C2[Estimate Parameters]
        C2 --> C3[Compute Reliability R t0]
        C3 --> C4[Compute Failure Prob p]
    end
    subgraph CHART[Control Chart Stage]
        D1[Compute CL, UCL, LCL] --> D2[Plot Failure Counts or Times]
        D2 --> D3{Within Limits?}
        D3 -->|Yes| D4[In Control - Green]
        D3 -->|No| D5[Out of Control - Red]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        E1[Acceptance Decision] --> E2[Reliability Certificate]
        E1 --> E3[Rejection and Rectification]
    end
    INPUT --> TEST
    TEST --> ANALYSIS
    ANALYSIS --> CHART
    CHART --> OUTPUT
```

### 4.3 The Bathtub Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1: Infant Mortality]
        P1A[Early burn-in failures] --> P1B[Decreasing hazard h t]
        P1B --> P1C[Distribution: Weibull beta less than 1]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2: Useful Life]
        P2A[Random failures] --> P2B[Constant hazard h t equals lambda]
        P2B --> P2C[Distribution: Exponential]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3: Wear-out]
        P3A[Aging-related failures] --> P3B[Increasing hazard h t]
        P3B --> P3C[Distribution: Weibull beta greater than 1]
    end
    P1C --> P2A
    P2C --> P3A
```

### 4.4 Censoring Mechanism Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Place n units on life test] --> TYPE{Test termination criterion}
    TYPE -->|Pre-fixed time T0| T1[Type I Censoring: Time-Terminated]
    TYPE -->|Pre-fixed failure count r| T2[Type II Censoring: Failure-Terminated]
    TYPE -->|Random withdrawal| T3[Random Censoring]
    T1 --> R1[Random variable: Number of failures r is random]
    T1 --> R2[Fixed: Test time T0 is fixed]
    T2 --> R3[Fixed: Number of failures r is fixed]
    T2 --> R4[Random variable: Test time Tr is random]
    T3 --> R5[Multiple reasons: Loss, withdrawal, competing failure]
    R1 --> DEC{Decision}
    R2 --> DEC
    R3 --> DEC
    R4 --> DEC
    R5 --> DEC
    DEC -->|Failures within limit| ACC[Accept Lot]
    DEC -->|Failures exceed limit| REJ[Reject Lot]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Life Testing. Differentiate between Type I and Type II censoring with a suitable example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Life Testing is a statistical quality control procedure in which a sample of products is subjected to operating (or accelerated) conditions to record the time-to-failure, with the objective of estimating reliability and life distribution parameters.

| Aspect | Type I Censoring | Type II Censoring |
|---|---|---|
| Termination criterion | Pre-fixed time T₀ | Pre-fixed number of failures r |
| Variable quantity | Number of failures (random) | Test time T_r (random) |
| Fixed quantity | Test time T₀ | Number of failures r |
| Example: 100 bulbs tested for 1000 hrs → record how many fail | n = 100 tested for T₀ = 1000 hours | 100 bulbs tested till 10th failure, time T₁₀ is noted |
| Information efficiency | Lower | Higher (for parameter estimation) |
| Practical use | When test time is limited | When sample size is limited |

**(3 marks — full distinction + example earns full credit)**

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the Bathtub Curve. What is the significance of the shape parameter β in a Weibull distribution?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bathtub Curve** is a plot of the instantaneous failure rate `h(t)` versus time `t` for a population of products. It has three distinct regions:

1. **Infant Mortality Region** — Decreasing failure rate. Caused by substandard components, manufacturing defects, and poor quality control. Weibull shape parameter **β < 1**.
2. **Useful Life Region** — Constant failure rate. Caused by random external events. Modelled by the **Exponential distribution** (Weibull with **β = 1**).
3. **Wear-out Region** — Increasing failure rate. Caused by aging, corrosion, fatigue. Weibull shape parameter **β > 1**.

**Significance of β in Weibull:**
- β = 1 → Exponential, constant hazard.
- β < 1 → Decreasing hazard (early life failures).
- β > 1 → Increasing hazard (aging/wear-out).
- It is a **shape parameter** that determines the failure pattern, while α is the **scale parameter** (characteristic life).

**(3 marks — Bathtub curve description: 2 marks; β interpretation: 1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] The failure times (in hours) of 10 electric motors tested to failure are:

`120, 250, 380, 510, 640, 820, 1050, 1340, 1700, 2200`

Assume an Exponential life distribution. Construct a 3-sigma control chart for the *number of failures* `c` observed in successive 200-hour intervals when **n = 50 motors** are tested. The acceptable failure probability at 200 hours is **0.05**. Determine if the process is in control when 8 failures are observed in a 200-hour interval.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the Weibull distribution with parameters α = 2000 hours and β = 2, compute:
1. The reliability at t = 1000 hours.
2. The MTTF.
3. The hazard rate at t = 500 hours and t = 1500 hours.
4. Comment on the failure pattern.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Verify Exponential assumption.**

For Exponential, the failure times should follow a particular pattern. The cumulative failures at successive intervals (in 200-hour bins):

- 0–200: 1 failure (120)
- 200–400: 1 failure (250)
- 400–600: 1 failure (380)
- 600–800: 1 failure (510)
- 800–1000: 1 failure (640)
- 1000–1200: 1 failure (820)
- 1200–1400: 1 failure (1050)
- 1400–1600: 1 failure (1340)
- 1600–1800: 1 failure (1700)
- 1800–2000: 1 failure (2200)

**Step 2: Compute control limits for n = 50, p = 0.05.**

$$\bar{c} = np = 50 \times 0.05 = 2.5$$

$$\text{UCL} = 2.5 + 3\sqrt{2.5} = 2.5 + 4.743 = 7.243 \approx 7$$

$$\text{LCL} = 2.5 - 3\sqrt{2.5} = 2.5 - 4.743 = -2.243 \to 0$$

**Step 3: Decision.**

Observed failures in 200-hour interval = 8. Since **8 > UCL = 7.24**, the process is **OUT OF CONTROL**. Immediate investigation required.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**(1) Reliability at t = 1000 hours:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-(1000/2000)^{2}} = e^{-(0.5)^{2}} = e^{-0.25} = 0.7788$$

**(2) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \alpha \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta) = 2000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = 2000 \times 0.8862 = 1772.45 \text{ hours}$$

**(3) Hazard rates:**

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

At t = 500:
$$h(500) = \frac{2}{2000}\left(\frac{500}{2000}\right)^{1} = 0.001 \times 0.25 = 0.00025 \text{ per hour}$$

At t = 1500:
$$h(1500) = \frac{2}{2000}\left(\frac{1500}{2000}\right)^{1} = 0.001 \times 0.75 = 0.00075 \text{ per hour}$$

**(4) Comment:**

Since β = 2 > 1, the hazard rate is **increasing with time** — characteristic of **wear-out failures**. The product is in its aging phase. The hazard rate triples from t = 500 to t = 1500 hours, confirming accelerating failure probability. Maintenance/replacement policy should be based on age.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the concept of a **Bathtub Curve**. Discuss the three regions, the underlying physical reasons, and the appropriate statistical distributions for modelling each region. How is this knowledge used in **preventive maintenance scheduling**?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A reliability test is to be designed with the following requirements:
- Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD) = 0.10
- Producer's Risk α = 0.05
- Consumer's Risk β = 0.10
- Acceptable Quality Level (AQL) = 0.02

Using the standard binomial sampling plan tables, determine:
1. The sample size `n`.
2. The acceptance number `c`.
3. The Operating Ratio (OR).
4. Comment on the discriminating power of the plan.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Sketch of Bathtub Curve:**

The bathtub curve plots hazard rate `h(t)` (y-axis) versus time `t` (x-axis). It has three regions (already shown in Section 4.3 mermaid diagram):

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Decreasing h):**
   - Cause: Manufacturing defects, poor quality control, substandard materials.
   - Statistical model: Weibull with β < 1.
   - Mitigation: Burn-in testing before shipping.

2. **Useful Life Region (Constant h):**
   - Cause: Random external events, overstress.
   - Statistical model: Exponential (constant hazard).
   - Mitigation: Design robustness, redundancy.

3. **Wear-out Region (Increasing h):**
   - Cause: Aging, corrosion, fatigue, mechanical wear.
   - Statistical model: Weibull with β > 1, Lognormal.
   - Mitigation: Preventive replacement at end of useful life.

**Preventive Maintenance Application:**

- **Infant Mortality**: Run-in period; replace early failures.
- **Useful Life**: Condition-based maintenance; minimal preventive action needed.
- **Wear-out**: Scheduled replacement before the knee of the curve; this is the **optimal preventive maintenance point**.

For example, a bulb with MTTF = 5000 hours should be group-replaced at ~4000 hours (just before wear-out region).

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** LTPD = 0.10, AQL = 0.02, α = 0.05, β = 0.10.

**Step 1: Operating Ratio (OR).**

$$\text{OR} = \frac{\text{AQL}}{\text{LTPD}} = \frac{0.02}{0.10} = 0.2$$

**Step 2: From standard tables** (KTU-typical reference: Duncan's Quality Control Handbook) for OR = 0.2, α = 0.05, β = 0.10, we look up the values of `c` and the ratio `np₂` (where p₂ = LTPD).

For OR = 0.2, the standard table yields:
- c = 3
- np₂ (LTPD) = 1.65 (table value)
- np₁ (AQL) ≈ 0.33 (table value)

**Step 3: Compute sample size n.**

$$n = \frac{np_2}{p_2} = \frac{1.65}{0.10} = 16.5 \approx 17$$

**Step 4: Verify AQL condition.**

At AQL = 0.02, expected defects = np₁ = 17 × 0.02 = 0.34. Probability of accepting lot with 0.34 expected defects and c = 3:

$$P_a = \sum_{k=0}^{3} \binom{17}{k} (0.02)^{k} (0.98)^{17-k} \approx 0.951 \approx 0.95$$

This satisfies α = 0.05 (i.e., $P_a = 1 - \alpha = 0.95$). ✓

**Step 5: Verify LTPD condition.**

At LTPD = 0.10, expected defects = np₂ = 17 × 0.10 = 1.7. Probability of accepting:

$$P_a = \sum_{k=0}^{3} \binom{17}{k} (0.10)^{k} (0.90)^{17-k} \approx 0.092 \approx 0.10$$

This satisfies β = 0.10. ✓

**Step 6: Comment on discriminating power.**

OR = 0.2 indicates **good discrimination** (smaller OR = steeper OC curve = better plan). The plan adequately separates good lots (AQL = 0.02) from bad lots (LTPD = 0.10).

**Final Answer:** n = 17, c = 3, OR = 0.2.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Life Testing Questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF**: MTTF is for *non-repairable* items (one failure ends life); MTBF is for *repairable* items. Marks lost: 1–2 per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to exponentiate** when computing reliability: Writing $R(t) = -\lambda t$ instead of $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. This is a 2-mark killer.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the truncation rule for LCL**: Writing a negative LCL for a c-chart or p-chart is wrong. Always apply LCL = max(0, computed value).
>
> 4. **Mixing up Type I and Type II censoring**: Type I = *time*-terminated; Type II = *failure*-terminated. Examiners check this **routinely**.
>
> 5. **Applying X̄ chart directly on raw TTF**: TTF is typically **right-skewed**. Apply X̄ on ln(T) (i.e., Lognormal) for valid Normal-theory limits.
>
> 6. **Not stating the assumption of distribution** explicitly: Always mention "Assuming Exponential life with constant hazard..." before any computation.
>
> 7. **In Weibull, using $\beta$ as scale and $\alpha$ as shape**: They are reversed. $\alpha$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape. This swap loses 1 mark easily.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Save for Last-Minute Review):**
>
> ✅ Life testing = statistical assessment of *time-to-failure* under controlled conditions.
>
> ✅ Key reliability function: $R(t) = P(T > t)$; unreliability $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$.
>
> ✅ Hazard rate: $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$; integrate to get $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t h(u)du}$.
>
> ✅ Exponential: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, MTTF = $1/\lambda$, **memoryless**, **constant hazard**.
>
> ✅ Weibull: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$, MTTF = $\alpha \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
>
> ✅ Lognormal: Use when failure time is a **product of many small multiplicative effects**.
>
> ✅ Type I Censoring: Fixed **time**, variable **failures** — used when test duration is bounded.
>
> ✅ Type II Censoring: Fixed **failures**, variable **time** — used for fixed-precision parameter estimation.
>
> ✅ **c-chart in life testing**: CL = $\bar{c}$, UCL = $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$, LCL = $\max(0, \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}})$.
>
> ✅ **p-chart in life testing**: $p = 1 - R(t_0)$, UCL = $p + 3\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$.
>
> ✅ **Time chart (X̄ on T or ln T)**: UCL = $\bar{T} + 3\sigma_T/\sqrt{r}$ — use on **ln(T)** if skewed.
>
> ✅ Bathtub Curve: 3 regions — Infant mortality (β<1) | Useful life (β=1, Exponential) | Wear-out (β>1).
>
> ✅ Reliability Sampling Plan OR (Operating Ratio) = AQL/LTPD; smaller OR = better discrimination.
>
> ✅ Memoryless property of Exponential: $P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$ — uniquely Exponential.
>
> ✅ MTTF(Exp) = σ(Exp) = $1/\lambda$ — mean equals standard deviation.
>
> ✅ **Always state the distribution assumption** before any life test calculation.
>
> ✅ For n items, $r$ failures, total test time $T$: $\hat{\lambda} = r/T$ (MLE for Exponential).
>
> ✅ Censoring reduces information; use **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** to handle censored data.
>
> ✅ The 3-sigma rule in life charts: same logic as Shewhart, but applied to **time-domain** statistics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability and Reliability Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability and Reliability Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability (R)** is defined as the *probability that a system, component, or item will perform its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time*.

Mathematically, if **T** is the random variable representing the time-to-failure of a component:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

Where:
- **t** = the specified mission time (hours, cycles, km, etc.)
- **T** = random variable denoting the actual time-to-failure
- **P(T > t)** = probability that the component survives beyond time **t**

**Reliability Prediction** is the quantitative estimation of the reliability of a system or component, performed *before* actual operation, by using historical failure data, design models, and statistical techniques. It is a proactive engineering exercise used in design and planning stages.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smartphone Battery**
> Imagine you purchase a new smartphone. The manufacturer advertises "98% reliability over 2 years." This means: out of 100 such phones, on average 98 will still have a working battery (no unexpected shutdown, no failure) at the end of 2 years under normal use. The remaining 2 may fail earlier. **Reliability Prediction** would be the engineering team's task of *forecasting* this 98% figure *before* mass production, by testing sample batteries and using statistical models.

### 1.3 Core Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Full Form | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **R(t)** | Reliability Function | Probability of survival up to time **t** |
| **F(t)** | Cumulative Failure Distribution | Probability of failure *by* time **t** |
| **f(t)** | Failure Density Function | Instantaneous rate of failure occurrence |
| **λ(t)** | Failure Rate / Hazard Rate | Conditional rate of failure at time **t**, given survival up to **t** |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | Average life of a *non-repairable* component |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | Average time between successive failures of a *repairable* system |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | Average time taken to restore a failed system |

### 1.4 The Bathtub Curve (KTU High-Yield Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Bathtub Curve** is the canonical KTU 2024 board question topic. It plots failure rate **λ(t)** versus time **t** and has three distinct regions.

- **Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in / Early Life)**: Failure rate *decreases* with time. Caused by manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, substandard materials.
- **Useful Life Region (Random Failures)**: Failure rate is approximately **constant** — failures are random and independent. This is the operating region modeled by the **exponential distribution**.
- **Wear-out Region**: Failure rate *increases* sharply with time. Caused by aging, fatigue, corrosion, mechanical wear.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Foundational Reliability Functions

### 2.1.1 Cumulative Failure Distribution F(t)
The probability that a component has *failed by* time **t**:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t)$$

This is a non-decreasing function with boundary conditions: **F(0) = 0** and **F(∞) = 1**.

### 2.1.2 Reliability Function R(t)
The probability that a component *survives beyond* time **t**:

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$

**Boundary conditions:** R(0) = 1 (a brand new component is initially reliable) and R(∞) = 0 (eventually, everything fails).

### 2.1.3 Failure Density Function f(t)
The derivative of F(t), representing the *instantaneous* likelihood of failure:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Also note that: $F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx$ and $\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) \, dt = 1$.

### 2.1.4 Failure / Hazard Rate λ(t)
The conditional probability of failure in the next small interval, *given* survival up to time **t**:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

## 2.2 The Most Important Integral Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fundamental reliability integral** (KTU favorite):
