- [x] **Always** carry 4+ decimal places in financial calculations.
- [x] **Always** draw diagrams in Decision Tree, DMP, and Scientific Approach questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Under Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decision Under Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Under Risk** is a decision-making environment in which the decision-maker can identify all possible outcomes (states of nature) AND can assign a **quantifiable probability** (between 0 and 1) to each outcome. The probabilities may be derived from objective historical data, statistical inference, or validated subjective estimates.

This stands in stark contrast to:
- **Decision Under Certainty:** Only one outcome exists (probability = 1).
- **Decision Under Uncertainty:** Outcomes are known, but probabilities CANNOT be assigned.
- **Decision Under Risk:** Outcomes are known AND probabilities are assigned.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Industrial Engineering parlance, the **decision-maker is a rational economic agent** who selects among alternative courses of action (acts/courses) to maximize expected gain (or minimize expected loss) given the probabilistic structure of the world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Act / Alternative / Course of Action ($a_i$):** The available choices the decision-maker can select.
> - **State of Nature ($s_j$):** Future events outside the decision-maker's control (demand, weather, market response).
> - **Payoff ($P_{ij}$):** Outcome (profit, cost, or utility) if act $a_i$ is chosen and state $s_j$ occurs.
> - **Probability ($p_j$):** Likelihood of state $s_j$, where $\sum_j p_j = 1$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the owner of a **Kerala backwater tourism houseboat company** in Alappuzha. You must decide today how many houseboats to commission (build/lease) for the upcoming Onam season. The possible actions are:
- **Small fleet (50 boats)**
- **Medium fleet (100 boats)**
- **Large fleet (200 boats)**

The possible future states are:
- **Low tourist turnout (because of heavy monsoon)**
- **Moderate turnout**
- **Tourist boom (Onam vacation + international tourism surge)**

You cannot control the weather. **However**, based on 25 years of IMD rainfall data, Kerala tourism statistics, and advance booking trends, you CAN estimate:
- P(Low turnout) = **0.30**
- P(Moderate turnout) = **0.50**
- P(Tourist boom) = **0.20**

Because you can assign numbers to the odds, you are now in a **Decision Under Risk** environment. The "risk" is not the absence of knowledge — it is the **known uncertainty**, where the unknown is tamed by probability.

> [!TIP]
> **The word "Risk" ≠ "Hazard".** In KTU Operations Research, *risk* means *measured* uncertainty. A lottery where you know the exact odds is risky but not ambiguous. A startup IPO where no one can compute odds is ambiguous (decision under uncertainty).

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Industrial Engineering

Industrial Engineering is fundamentally about **optimizing scarce resources under variable conditions**. From inventory planning (newsvendor problem) to capacity expansion, from project crashing to reliability engineering, every real KTU-level Industrial Engineering problem involves decisions where outcomes fluctuate but probabilities can be quantified. Decision under risk provides the **mathematical engine** (Expected Monetary Value, Expected Opportunity Loss, EVPI) for these decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Decision-Making Pipeline Under Risk

The structured thought process a KTU student must follow:

1. **Identify the Decision Maker** — clearly state who is making the choice (firm, manager, investor).
2. **Enumerate Alternatives ($a_i$)** — list all feasible actions (e.g., Build, Buy, Lease, Wait).
3. **Identify States of Nature ($s_j$)** — list the mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive future events.
4. **Construct the Payoff Matrix** — tabulate $P_{ij}$ for every (act, state) combination.
5. **Assign Probabilities ($p_j$)** — using prior data, posterior Bayes updates, or frequency-based statistics.
6. **Apply a Decision Criterion** — typically Expected Monetary Value (EMV), Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL), or Utility.
7. **Compute the Value of Information (EVPI)** — quantify how much better you could do with perfect forecasts.
8. **Perform Sensitivity Analysis** — check robustness of the chosen act to probability perturbations.

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' of Each Criterion

- **EMV (Expected Monetary Value):** The 'Why' is the **Law of Large Numbers** — if the decision is repeated many times, the average outcome converges to EMV. The 'How' is multiplying each payoff by its probability and summing.
- **EOL (Expected Opportunity Loss / Regret):** The 'Why' reframes losses in terms of *missed opportunity*. The 'How' is to first convert the payoff matrix to a regret matrix, then compute its expected value.
- **EVPI (Expected Value of Perfect Information):** The 'Why' bounds the rational price a decision-maker should pay for an oracle. The 'How' is the difference between the EMV under perfect information and the EMV under current information.
- **Utility Theory:** The 'Why' accounts for **risk aversion** — a ₹1 crore gain is not equally attractive to a pauper and a billionaire. The 'How' is to replace payoffs with utility values via a concave utility function.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Interpretation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Monetary Value of act $a_i$ | $EMV(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{ij} \cdot p_j$ | Average payoff per trial if $a_i$ is chosen | ₹ / units |
| 2 | Optimal act via EMV | $a^* = \arg\max_i EMV(a_i)$ | Choose act with highest EMV | — |
| 3 | Opportunity Loss (Regret) of $a_i$ under $s_j$ | $OL_{ij} = \max_k P_{kj} - P_{ij}$ | Profit you missed by not picking the best act | ₹ / units |
| 4 | Expected Opportunity Loss of $a_i$ | $EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} OL_{ij} \cdot p_j$ | Average regret per trial | ₹ / units |
| 5 | Optimal act via EOL | $a^* = \arg\min_i EOL(a_i)$ | Minimize expected regret | — |
| 6 | EMV–EOL duality | $\max_i EMV(a_i) - \min_i EOL(a_i) = \sum_j p_j \cdot \max_k P_{kj}$ | Both criteria yield same optimal act | ₹ / units |
| 7 | Expected Value with Perfect Information | $EV_{wPI} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[ \max_i P_{ij} \right] \cdot p_j$ | EMV if state known in advance | ₹ / units |
| 8 | EVPI (Expected Value of Perfect Information) | $EVPI = EV_{wPI} - \max_i EMV(a_i)$ | Max price worth paying for perfect forecast | ₹ / units |
| 9 | Bayes' Theorem (posterior update) | $P(s_j \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid s_j) \cdot P(s_j)}{\sum_k P(E \mid s_k) \cdot P(s_k)}$ | Update prior with sample evidence | dimensionless |
| 10 | Expected Value of Sample Information | $EVSI = EV_{wPI_{posterior}} - \max_i EMV_{posterior}(a_i)$ | Worth of an imperfect sample | ₹ / units |
| 11 | Efficiency of Information | $\eta = \dfrac{EVSI}{EVPI} \times 100\%$ | Quality of the sample (0%–100%) | % |
| 12 | Risk-adjusted utility | $U(W) = a + b \cdot \ln(W)$ or $U(W) = -e^{-cW}$ | Risk-averse concave utility (Cobb-Douglas / exponential) | utils |
| 13 | Certainty Equivalent | $CE$ such that $U(CE) = EU(a^*)$ | Guaranteed amount equal to a risky act's expected utility | ₹ / units |
| 14 | Risk Premium | $RP = EU^{-1}(EU(a^*)) - EMV(a^*)$ | Amount decision-maker is willing to pay to avoid risk | ₹ / units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Alert:** In Question Paper solving, the most frequently tested formulas are #1, #3, #4, #5, #7, #8, and #9. Memorize these seven cold.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Engineering Field | Decision Under Risk Application |
|---|---|
| Manufacturing Capacity Planning | Decide plant size when demand is probabilistic |
| Inventory Control (Newsvendor) | Order quantity under stochastic demand |
| Project Management (PERT/CPM) | Crash cost vs. expected delay penalty |
| Reliability Engineering | Choose redundancy level given component failure probabilities |
| Quality Engineering | Accept/Reject lot based on sampling evidence (Bayesian QC) |
| Financial Engineering | Capital budgeting under stochastic NPV |
| Supply Chain | Supplier selection with disruption probabilities |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The EMV Criterion from First Principles (Law of Large Numbers)

**Starting Principle:** Suppose an act $a_i$ yields a random payoff $X$ whose realizations are $P_{i1}, P_{i2}, \dots, P_{in}$ occurring with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$ respectively. The *expected value* of a discrete random variable is the probability-weighted average.

$$
X = 
\begin{cases}
P_{i1} & \text{with probability } p_1 \\
P_{i2} & \text{with probability } p_2 \\
\vdots & \vdots \\
P_{in} & \text{with probability } p_n
\end{cases}
$$

By the axiomatic definition of expected value:

$$
E[X] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{ij} \cdot p_j
$$

By the **Weak Law of Large Numbers**, if the act is repeated $N$ times independently:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} P\!\left(\left| \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} X_t - E[X] \right| < \varepsilon \right) = 1
$$

That is, the long-run average payoff converges in probability to $E[X]$. This is the *justification* for using EMV as a decision criterion in a rational framework.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: EMV–EOL Duality (Why both criteria agree)

**Step 1:** Define the regret matrix element:

$$
OL_{ij} = \max_{k} P_{kj} - P_{ij}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the column maximum of the payoff matrix:

$$
M_j = \max_{k} P_{kj}
$$

**Step 3:** Expected Opportunity Loss for act $a_i$:

$$
EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} OL_{ij} \cdot p_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (M_j - P_{ij}) \cdot p_j
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the sum:

$$
EOL(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_j \cdot p_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{ij} \cdot p_j
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the two terms:

$$
EOL(a_i) = \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_j \cdot p_j}_{\text{Expected value under Perfect Information, } EV_{wPI}} - \underbrace{EMV(a_i)}_{\text{Expected value of act } a_i}
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange:

$$
EMV(a_i) = EV_{wPI} - EOL(a_i)
$$

**Step 7:** Since $EV_{wPI}$ is a **constant** independent of $a_i$:

$$
\arg\max_i EMV(a_i) \iff \arg\min_i EOL(a_i)
$$

Hence, the EMV and EOL criteria are mathematically equivalent and always identify the **same optimal act**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: EVPI Bound

Starting from the identity just derived:

$$
EV_{wPI} = \max_i EMV(a_i) + \min_i EOL(a_i)
$$

By definition:

$$
EVPI = EV_{wPI} - \max_i EMV(a_i)
$$

Substitute:

$$
EVPI = \left[ \max_i EMV(a_i) + \min_i EOL(a_i) \right] - \max_i EMV(a_i)
$$

$$
\boxed{EVPI = \min_i EOL(a_i)}
$$

**Important corollary:** EVPI equals the **minimum** EOL across all acts — this is a *very common KTU question type* and students often forget the equivalence.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Bayes' Theorem for Posterior Decision Updates

Let $\{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$ be the states of nature with prior probabilities $P(s_j)$. A sample/test/survey yields evidence $E$ with likelihoods $P(E \mid s_j)$.

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(s_j \mid E) = \frac{P(E \cap s_j)}{P(E)}
$$

By the multiplication rule:

$$
P(E \cap s_j) = P(E \mid s_j) \cdot P(s_j)
$$

By the law of total probability:

$$
P(E) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} P(E \mid s_k) \cdot P(s_k)
$$

Combining:

$$
P(s_j \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid s_j) \cdot P(s_j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} P(E \mid s_k) \cdot P(s_k)}
$$

The **posterior** $P(s_j \mid E)$ replaces the **prior** $P(s_j)$ when re-computing EMV, EOL, and EVPI in the light of new evidence.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Dec 2023 Model):** A KTU-affiliated engineering college is choosing between three new elective tracks ($a_1$: AI/ML, $a_2$: Robotics, $a_3$: Renewable Energy) for the next academic year. The state of the job market in 2027 can be Strong ($s_1$), Normal ($s_2$), or Weak ($s_3$) with prior probabilities $0.3$, $0.5$, and $0.2$ respectively. The expected 5-year placement revenue (in ₹ lakhs) is given in the payoff matrix below. A market research survey is offered at a cost of ₹ 1.5 lakhs; it predicts Strong demand with 80% accuracy when demand is Strong, and 70% accuracy otherwise.

**Payoff Matrix (₹ lakhs):**

| | $s_1$ (Strong, p=0.3) | $s_2$ (Normal, p=0.5) | $s_3$ (Weak, p=0.2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1$: AI/ML | 50 | 30 | 10 |
| $a_2$: Robotics | 40 | 35 | 20 |
| $a_3$: Renewable | 30 | 28 | 25 |

#### Step A: Compute EMV (Prior)

For $a_1$:
$$
EMV(a_1) = (50)(0.3) + (30)(0.5) + (10)(0.2) = 15 + 15 + 2 = 32
$$

For $a_2$:
$$
EMV(a_2) = (40)(0.3) + (35)(0.5) + (20)(0.2) = 12 + 17.5 + 4 = 33.5
$$

For $a_3$:
$$
EMV(a_3) = (30)(0.3) + (28)(0.5) + (25)(0.2) = 9 + 14 + 5 = 28
$$

**Best prior act: $a_2$ (Robotics) with EMV = ₹ 33.5 lakhs.**

#### Step B: Construct the Regret Matrix

For $s_1$: max = 50 → regrets are 0, 10, 20
For $s_2$: max = 35 → regrets are 5, 0, 7
For $s_3$: max = 25 → regrets are 15, 5, 0

| | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1$ | 0 | 5 | 15 |
| $a_2$ | 10 | 0 | 5 |
| $a_3$ | 20 | 7 | 0 |

EOL:
$$
EOL(a_1) = (0)(0.3) + (5)(0.5) + (15)(0.2) = 0 + 2.5 + 3 = 5.5
$$

$$
EOL(a_2) = (10)(0.3) + (0)(0.5) + (5)(0.2) = 3 + 0 + 1 = 4.0
$$

$$
EOL(a_3) = (20)(0.3) + (7)(0.5) + (0)(0.2) = 6 + 3.5 + 0 = 9.5
$$

**Best by EOL: $a_2$ with EOL = ₹ 4.0 lakhs** (same as EMV — duality verified).

#### Step C: Compute EVPI

$$
EV_{wPI} = (50)(0.3) + (35)(0.5) + (25)(0.2) = 15 + 17.5 + 5 = 37.5
$$

$$
EVPI = 37.5 - 33.5 = 4.0
$$

(Confirms: $EVPI = \min_i EOL(a_i) = 4.0$.)

#### Step D: Bayes' Update with Survey

Survey accuracy:
- P(Survey predicts Strong $\mid$ Strong) = 0.80
- P(Survey predicts Strong $\mid$ Normal) = 0.15  *(since 70% accurate for non-Strong, 30% wrong → 0.30 wrongly predicts Strong)*

Wait — re-read carefully: "predicts Strong demand with 80% accuracy when demand is Strong, and 70% accuracy otherwise." That means:
- P(Survey Strong $\mid$ Strong) = 0.80
- P(Survey Strong $\mid$ Normal) = 0.30 *(30% wrongly predicts Strong)*
- P(Survey Strong $\mid$ Weak) = 0.30

**Prior survey result** "Strong" probability:
$$
P(\text{Survey = Strong}) = (0.80)(0.3) + (0.30)(0.5) + (0.30)(0.2) = 0.24 + 0.15 + 0.06 = 0.45
$$

**Posterior (if Survey = Strong):**

$$
P(s_1 \mid \text{Strong}) = \frac{(0.80)(0.3)}{0.45} = \frac{0.24}{0.45} \approx 0.5333
$$

$$
P(s_2 \mid \text{Strong}) = \frac{(0.30)(0.5)}{0.45} = \frac{0.15}{0.45} \approx 0.3333
$$

$$
P(s_3 \mid \text{Strong}) = \frac{(0.30)(0.2)}{0.45} = \frac{0.06}{0.45} \approx 0.1333
$$

**Posterior EMV if Survey = Strong:**

$$
EMV(a_1) = (50)(0.5333) + (30)(0.3333) + (10)(0.1333) \approx 26.67 + 10.00 + 1.33 = 38.00
$$

$$
EMV(a_2) = (40)(0.5333) + (35)(0.3333) + (20)(0.1333) \approx 21.33 + 11.67 + 2.67 = 35.67
$$

$$
EMV(a_3) = (30)(0.5333) + (28)(0.3333) + (25)(0.1333) \approx 16.00 + 9.33 + 3.33 = 28.66
$$

**Best act given Survey = Strong → $a_1$ (AI/ML), EMV ≈ 38.00**

**Posterior survey result** "Not Strong" probability:
$$
P(\text{Survey ≠ Strong}) = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55
$$

**Posterior (if Survey ≠ Strong):**

$$
P(s_1 \mid \text{Not Strong}) = \frac{(0.20)(0.3)}{0.55} = \frac{0.06}{0.55} \approx 0.1091
$$

$$
P(s_2 \mid \text{Not Strong}) = \frac{(0.70)(0.5)}{0.55} = \frac{0.35}{0.55} \approx 0.6364
$$

$$
P(s_3 \mid \text{Not Strong}) = \frac{(0.70)(0.2)}{0.55} = \frac{0.14}{0.55} \approx 0.2545
$$

**Posterior EMV if Survey ≠ Strong:**

$$
EMV(a_1) = (50)(0.1091) + (30)(0.6364) + (10)(0.2545) \approx 5.45 + 19.09 + 2.55 = 27.09
$$

$$
EMV(a_2) = (40)(0.1091) + (35)(0.6364) + (20)(0.2545) \approx 4.36 + 22.27 + 5.09 = 31.72
$$

$$
EMV(a_3) = (30)(0.1091) + (28)(0.6364) + (25)(0.2545) \approx 3.27 + 17.82 + 6.36 = 27.45
$$

**Best act given Survey ≠ Strong → $a_2$ (Robotics), EMV ≈ 31.72**

**Step E: Expected Value with Sample Information (EVwSI):**

$$
EV_{wSI} = (38.00)(0.45) + (31.72)(0.55) = 17.10 + 17.45 = 34.55
$$

**Step F: Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI):**

$$
EVSI = EV_{wSI} - \max(EMV_{prior}) = 34.55 - 33.5 = 1.05
$$

**Step G: Decision on Survey Purchase:**

Survey cost = ₹ 1.5 lakhs; EVSI = ₹ 1.05 lakhs.

$$
\text{Net gain from survey} = EVSI - \text{Cost} = 1.05 - 1.5 = -0.45 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Conclusion:** Do NOT buy the survey. Stick with the prior best act $a_2$ (Robotics).

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision-Making Workflow Under Risk

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Industrial Decision Problem] --> B[Enumerate Alternatives a1, a2, ..., am]
    B --> C[Identify States of Nature s1, s2, ..., sn]
    C --> D[Construct Payoff Matrix Pij]
    D --> E{Is Information Available?}
    E -- "No sample data" --> F[Assign Prior Probabilities pj]
    E -- "Yes, sample E" --> G[Compute Likelihoods P_E_given_sj]
    G --> H[Apply Bayes Theorem]
    H --> I[Compute Posterior P_sj_given_E]
    I --> J[Compute Posterior EMV per act]
    F --> K[Compute EMV per act]
    J --> L[Select act with max EMV]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Build Regret Matrix OLij]
    M --> N[Compute EOL per act]
    N --> O[Select act with min EOL]
    O --> P[Compute EVwPI and EVPI]
    P --> Q[Is EVPI less than Survey Cost?]
    Q -- "Yes" --> R[Do not buy survey]
    Q -- "No" --> S[Buy survey if EVSI greater than cost]
    S --> T[Final Decision Tree Strategy]
    R --> T
    L --> T
    O --> T
    T --> U[Implement Decision]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Survey-Based Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Decision Node: Choose Action] --> D1{Branch by Prior Information}
    D1 -- "Buy Survey first" --> S0[Survey Node]
    S0 -- "Strong" --> S1{Choose Act given Strong}
    S0 -- "Not Strong" --> S2{Choose Act given Not Strong}
    S1 -- "a1 AI/ML" --> R1[EMV approx 38.00 lakhs]
    S1 -- "a2 Robotics" --> R2[EMV approx 35.67 lakhs]
    S1 -- "a3 Renewable" --> R3[EMV approx 28.66 lakhs]
    S2 -- "a1 AI/ML" --> R4[EMV approx 27.09 lakhs]
    S2 -- "a2 Robotics" --> R5[EMV approx 31.72 lakhs]
    S2 -- "a3 Renewable" --> R6[EMV approx 27.45 lakhs]
    D1 -- "No Survey" --> N1{Choose Act with Prior Only}
    N1 -- "a1 AI/ML" --> R7[EMV equals 32 lakhs]
    N1 -- "a2 Robotics" --> R8[EMV equals 33.5 lakhs]
    N1 -- "a3 Renewable" --> R9[EMV equals 28 lakhs]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bayesian Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Prior Probabilities]
        I2[Likelihoods from Survey]
        I3[Payoff Matrix]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Layer]
        C1[Bayesian Update Module]
        C2[EMV Calculator]
        C3[Regret Matrix Builder]
        C4[EOL Calculator]
        C5[EVwPI and EVPI Module]
        C6[EVSI and Efficiency Module]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision Output]
        O1[Optimal Prior Act]
        O2[Optimal Posterior Acts]
        O3[Survey Buy or Not Decision]
        O4[Sensitivity Report]
    end
    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I1 --> C2
    I3 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    I3 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C1 --> C4
    I1 --> C5
    C2 --> C5
    I2 --> C5
    C5 --> C6
    C2 --> O1
    C2 --> O2
    C6 --> O3
    C2 --> O4
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
Differentiate between **decision making under risk** and **decision making under uncertainty**. Give one real-world example of each from an engineering context.

**Model Answer (Concise):**  
In decision under **risk**, the decision-maker can assign a numerical probability to each possible state of nature, typically based on historical data or statistical analysis. Example: deciding the lot size for an inventory item when past demand data is available.  
In decision under **uncertainty**, the states of nature are known, but **no probabilities** can be assigned. Example: introducing a brand-new disruptive technology in a market with no historical analogue. (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)**. State its formula in terms of Expected Monetary Value and Expected Opportunity Loss.

**Model Answer:**  
EVPI is the maximum amount a rational decision-maker should be willing to pay for a perfectly accurate forecast of the future state.  
$$EVPI = EV_{wPI} - \max_i EMV(a_i) = \min_i EOL(a_i)$$  
(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently **lose 2 to 3 marks** on Part B questions by:
> (a) Forgetting to **subtract the survey cost** when evaluating EVSI — the survey's *net* value is EVSI − Cost, not EVSI alone.
> (b) Mixing up the formula for $EV_{wPI}$ — it is $\sum_j [\max_i P_{ij}] \cdot p_j$, NOT $\max_i [\sum_j P_{ij} \cdot p_j]$.
> (c) Not verifying the **EMV–EOL duality** when both criteria are required — failing to mention $\min_i EOL = EVPI$ costs full marks on the verification sub-part.
> (d) Using rounded intermediate values in Bayes' updates — *always retain 4 decimal places* in posterior probabilities.

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**  
A startup in Kochi is planning to launch a new electric two-wheeler. The management must choose between three plant capacities: $a_1$ (Small: 5,000 units/year), $a_2$ (Medium: 15,000 units/year), and $a_3$ (Large: 30,000 units/year). The future demand can be Low ($s_1$), Moderate ($s_2$), or High ($s_3$) with prior probabilities 0.4, 0.4, and 0.2 respectively. The profit (in ₹ lakhs) under each combination is:

| | $s_1$ (Low) | $s_2$ (Moderate) | $s_3$ (High) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1$ | 80 | 60 | 40 |
| $a_2$ | 40 | 90 | 80 |
| $a_3$ | -20 | 50 | 120 |

**(a) [7 marks]** Construct the payoff matrix and determine the optimal decision using:
1. The Expected Monetary Value (EMV) criterion
2. The Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) criterion
3. Verify that both criteria yield the same optimal act.

**(b) [7 marks]** A market research firm offers a sample survey for ₹ 5 lakhs. The survey's accuracy is: P(Survey = High $\mid$ High) = 0.85, P(Survey = High $\mid$ Moderate) = 0.25, P(Survey = High $\mid$ Low) = 0.10. Determine:
1. The posterior probabilities using Bayes' theorem
2. The optimal decision after the survey
3. The Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI) and recommend whether to buy the survey.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — EMV calculation:**

$$
EMV(a_1) = (80)(0.4) + (60)(0.4) + (40)(0.2) = 32 + 24 + 8 = 64
$$

$$
EMV(a_2) = (40)(0.4) + (90)(0.4) + (80)(0.2) = 16 + 36 + 16 = 68
$$

$$
EMV(a_3) = (-20)(0.4) + (50)(0.4) + (120)(0.2) = -8 + 20 + 24 = 36
$$

**Optimal act by EMV: $a_2$ (Medium plant), EMV = ₹ 68 lakhs.** (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Regret matrix:**

For $s_1$: max = 80 → regrets: 0, 40, 100
For $s_2$: max = 90 → regrets: 30, 0, 40
For $s_3$: max = 120 → regrets: 80, 40, 0

| | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1$ | 0 | 30 | 80 |
| $a_2$ | 40 | 0 | 40 |
| $a_3$ | 100 | 40 | 0 |

**Step 3 — EOL calculation:**

$$
EOL(a_1) = (0)(0.4) + (30)(0.4) + (80)(0.2) = 0 + 12 + 16 = 28
$$

$$
EOL(a_2) = (40)(0.4) + (0)(0.4) + (40)(0.2) = 16 + 0 + 8 = 24
$$

$$
EOL(a_3) = (100)(0.4) + (40)(0.4) + (0)(0.2) = 40 + 16 + 0 = 56
$$

**Optimal act by EOL: $a_2$ (Medium plant), EOL = ₹ 24 lakhs.** (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Duality verification:**

$EV_{wPI} = (80)(0.4) + (90)(0.4) + (120)(0.2) = 32 + 36 + 24 = 92$

$EVPI = EV_{wPI} - \max EMV = 92 - 68 = 24$

$\min_i EOL = 24$

$\therefore EVPI = \min_i EOL$, confirming duality. Both criteria select $a_2$. (3 marks)

**Part (b):**

**Step 5 — Survey prior probability:**

$$
P(\text{Survey} = \text{High}) = (0.85)(0.2) + (0.25)(0.4) + (0.10)(0.4) = 0.17 + 0.10 + 0.04 = 0.31
$$

$$
P(\text{Survey} \neq \text{High}) = 1 - 0.31 = 0.69
$$

**Step 6 — Posterior probabilities (if Survey = High):**

$$
P(s_1 \mid H) = \frac{(0.10)(0.4)}{0.31} = \frac{0.04}{0.31} \approx 0.1290
$$

$$
P(s_2 \mid H) = \frac{(0.25)(0.4)}{0.31} = \frac{0.10}{0.31} \approx 0.3226
$$

$$
P(s_3 \mid H) = \frac{(0.85)(0.2)}{0.31} = \frac{0.17}{0.31} \approx 0.5484
$$

**Posterior EMV if Survey = High:**

$$
EMV(a_1) = (80)(0.1290) + (60)(0.3226) + (40)(0.5484) \approx 10.32 + 19.36 + 21.94 = 51.62
$$

$$
EMV(a_2) = (40)(0.1290) + (90)(0.3226) + (80)(0.5484) \approx 5.16 + 29.03 + 43.87 = 78.06
$$

$$
EMV(a_3) = (-20)(0.1290) + (50)(0.3226) + (120)(0.5484) \approx -2.58 + 16.13 + 65.81 = 79.36
$$

**If Survey = High → choose $a_3$ (Large plant), EMV ≈ ₹ 79.36 lakhs.** (1 mark)

**Step 7 — Posterior probabilities (if Survey ≠ High):**

$$
P(s_1 \mid \overline{H}) = \frac{(0.90)(0.4)}{0.69} = \frac{0.36}{0.69} \approx 0.5217
$$

$$
P(s_2 \mid \overline{H}) = \frac{(0.75)(0.4)}{0.69} = \frac{0.30}{0.69} \approx 0.4348
$$

$$
P(s_3 \mid \overline{H}) = \frac{(0.15)(0.2)}{0.69} = \frac{0.03}{0.69} \approx 0.0435
$$

**Posterior EMV if Survey ≠ High:**

$$
EMV(a_1) = (80)(0.5217) + (60)(0.4348) + (40)(0.0435) \approx 41.74 + 26.09 + 1.74 = 69.57
$$

$$
EMV(a_2) = (40)(0.5217) + (90)(0.4348) + (80)(0.0435) \approx 20.87 + 39.13 + 3.48 = 63.48
$$

$$
EMV(a_3) = (-20)(0.5217) + (50)(0.4348) + (120)(0.0435) \approx -10.43 + 21.74 + 5.22 = 16.53
$$

**If Survey ≠ High → choose $a_1$ (Small plant), EMV ≈ ₹ 69.57 lakhs.** (1 mark)

**Step 8 — EVwSI and EVSI:**

$$
EV_{wSI} = (79.36)(0.31) + (69.57)(0.69) \approx 24.60 + 48.00 = 72.60
$$

$$
EVSI = EV_{wSI} - \max EMV_{prior} = 72.60 - 68.00 = 4.60
$$

**Step 9 — Survey decision:**

Net value of survey = $EVSI - \text{Cost} = 4.60 - 5.00 = -0.40$ lakhs.

**Conclusion: Do NOT purchase the survey. The optimal prior decision $a_2$ (Medium plant) is recommended.** (1 mark)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative)]**  
A construction company must choose between bidding on a Large bridge project ($a_1$), a Medium commercial complex ($a_2$), or a Small residential building ($a_3$). Profit (₹ crores) under various economic states is:

| | $s_1$ Recession (p=0.25) | $s_2$ Stable (p=0.50) | $s_3$ Boom (p=0.25) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1$ (Large) | -5 | 8 | 20 |
| $a_2$ (Medium) | 3 | 6 | 9 |
| $a_3$ (Small) | 2 | 4 | 5 |

**(a) [7 marks]** Compute the optimal decision using EMV, EOL, and EVPI.

**(b) [7 marks]** A consulting firm offers an economic survey for ₹ 1.5 crores. The survey predicts Boom with probabilities: P(Boom $\mid$ Boom) = 0.90, P(Boom $\mid$ Stable) = 0.20, P(Boom $\mid$ Recession) = 0.05. Decide using EVSI whether to buy the survey.

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a):**

EMV values:
$$
EMV(a_1) = (-5)(0.25) + (8)(0.50) + (20)(0.25) = -1.25 + 4.00 + 5.00 = 7.75
$$

$$
EMV(a_2) = (3)(0.25) + (6)(0.50) + (9)(0.25) = 0.75 + 3.00 + 2.25 = 6.00
$$

$$
EMV(a_3) = (2)(0.25) + (4)(0.50) + (5)(0.25) = 0.50 + 2.00 + 1.25 = 3.75
$$

**Best by EMV: $a_1$ (Large bridge), EMV = ₹ 7.75 crores.** (2 marks)

Regret matrix:
- $s_1$: max = 3 → 8, 0, 1
- $s_2$: max = 8 → 0, 2, 4
- $s_3$: max = 20 → 0, 11, 15

EOL:
$$
EOL(a_1) = (8)(0.25) + (0)(0.50) + (0)(0.25) = 2.00
$$

$$
EOL(a_2) = (0)(0.25) + (2)(0.50) + (11)(0.25) = 0 + 1.00 + 2.75 = 3.75
$$

$$
EOL(a_3) = (1)(0.25) + (4)(0.50) + (15)(0.25) = 0.25 + 2.00 + 3.75 = 6.00
$$

**Best by EOL: $a_1$, EOL = ₹ 2.00 crores.** (2 marks)

EVPI:
$$
EV_{wPI} = (3)(0.25) + (8)(0.50) + (20)(0.25) = 0.75 + 4.00 + 5.00 = 9.75
$$

$$
EVPI = 9.75 - 7.75 = 2.00 = \min EOL \text{ (verified)} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

**Part (b):**

Survey prior:
$$
P(\text{Boom}) = (0.90)(0.25) + (0.20)(0.50) + (0.05)(0.25) = 0.225 + 0.10 + 0.0125 = 0.3375
$$

$$
P(\text{Not Boom}) = 0.6625
$$

Posterior (if Boom predicted):
$$
P(s_1 \mid B) = \frac{(0.05)(0.25)}{0.3375} = \frac{0.0125}{0.3375} \approx 0.0370
$$

$$
P(s_2 \mid B) = \frac{(0.20)(0.50)}{0.3375} = \frac{0.10}{0.3375} \approx 0.2963
$$

$$
P(s_3 \mid B) = \frac{(0.90)(0.25)}{0.3375} = \frac{0.225}{0.3375} \approx 0.6667
$$

Posterior EMV if Boom:
$$
EMV(a_1) \approx (-5)(0.0370) + (8)(0.2963) + (20)(0.6667) \approx -0.185 + 2.370 + 13.333 = 15.518
$$

$$
EMV(a_2) \approx (3)(0.0370) + (6)(0.2963) + (9)(0.6667) \approx 0.111 + 1.778 + 6.000 = 7.889
$$

$$
EMV(a_3) \approx (2)(0.0370) + (4)(0.2963) + (5)(0.6667) \approx 0.074 + 1.185 + 3.333 = 4.593
$$

**If Boom predicted → $a_1$, EMV ≈ 15.52 crores.** (1 mark)

Posterior (if Not Boom):
$$
P(s_1 \mid \overline{B}) = \frac{(0.95)(0.25)}{0.6625} = \frac{0.2375}{0.6625} \approx 0.3585
$$

$$
P(s_2 \mid \overline{B}) = \frac{(0.80)(0.50)}{0.6625} = \frac{0.40}{0.6625} \approx 0.6038
$$

$$
P(s_3 \mid \overline{B}) = \frac{(0.10)(0.25)}{0.6625} = \frac{0.025}{0.6625} \approx 0.0377
$$

Posterior EMV if Not Boom:
$$
EMV(a_1) \approx (-5)(0.3585) + (8)(0.6038) + (20)(0.0377) \approx -1.793 + 4.830 + 0.755 = 3.792
$$

$$
EMV(a_2) \approx (3)(0.3585) + (6)(0.6038) + (9)(0.0377) \approx 1.075 + 3.623 + 0.340 = 5.038
$$

$$
EMV(a_3) \approx (2)(0.3585) + (4)(0.6038) + (5)(0.0377) \approx 0.717 + 2.415 + 0.189 = 3.321
$$

**If Not Boom → $a_2$, EMV ≈ 5.04 crores.** (1 mark)

EVwSI:
$$
EV_{wSI} = (15.52)(0.3375) + (5.04)(0.6625) \approx 5.238 + 3.339 = 8.577
$$

$$
EVSI = 8.577 - 7.75 = 0.827
$$

Net value of survey = $0.827 - 1.5 = -0.673$ crores.

**Conclusion: Do NOT buy the survey. Optimal prior decision: $a_1$ (Large bridge).** (2 marks)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Decision Under Risk (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- ✅ **Risk vs. Uncertainty:** Risk = outcomes AND probabilities known. Uncertainty = outcomes known, probabilities NOT known.
- ✅ **EMV formula:** $EMV(a_i) = \sum_j P_{ij} \cdot p_j$. Choose the **maximum** EMV.
- ✅ **Regret formula:** $OL_{ij} = \max_k P_{kj} - P_{ij}$ (column-wise max minus the cell value).
- ✅ **EOL formula:** $EOL(a_i) = \sum_j OL_{ij} \cdot p_j$. Choose the **minimum** EOL.
- ✅ **EMV–EOL Duality:** $EMV(a_i) + EOL(a_i) = EV_{wPI}$ (a constant). So both criteria give the **same optimal act**.
- ✅ **EVPI:** $EVPI = EV_{wPI} - \max_i EMV(a_i) = \min_i EOL(a_i)$.
- ✅ **EVSI = EVwSI − best prior EMV.** Always subtract the survey **cost** before recommending.
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(s_j \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid s_j) \cdot P(s_j)}{\sum_k P(E \mid s_k) \cdot P(s_k)}$.
- ✅ **Posterior must sum to 1.** Always check $\sum_j P(s_j \mid E) = 1$ to catch arithmetic errors.
- ✅ **Decision tree convention:** Square node = decision; Circle node = chance; Triangle = outcome/EMV.
- ✅ **Law of Large Numbers** is the philosophical foundation of EMV — without repetition, EMV is just a weighted sum, not a "long-run average."
- ✅ **Utility function** (e.g., $U(W) = \sqrt{W}$) replaces money when the decision-maker is **risk-averse** — the curvature captures diminishing marginal value of wealth.
- ✅ **Certainty Equivalent (CE)** < **EMV** for a risk-averse person. The difference is the **Risk Premium**.
- ✅ **KTU examiner traps:** forgetting probability sum = 1, mixing $EV_{wPI}$ formula direction, omitting survey cost, using integers in the regret matrix.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** "**E**MV → max**E**; **E**OL → min**E**L" (look for the bolded letter pattern in the word itself).

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Engineering and Operations Research**, a **Decision Problem** is a situation in which a decision-maker (DM) must select an optimal course of action (called an **Alternative** or **Strategy**) from a finite set of available options, given a set of possible future conditions (called **States of Nature** or **Events**).

A decision is said to be taken **Under Uncertainty** when the decision-maker **cannot assign objective probabilities** (or even reliable subjective probabilities) to the various states of nature. The DM knows the possible outcomes, but the odds, frequencies, or likelihoods of those outcomes are either **unknown, missing, or unreliable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Decision Under Uncertainty** → Probabilities of states of nature are **NOT known**.
> - **Decision Under Risk** → Probabilities of states of nature **ARE known** (solved using Expected Monetary Value / EMV).
> - The current module focuses strictly on the **Uncertainty** case.

### 1.2 Components of a Decision-Making Model

A standard decision problem under uncertainty is represented by a **Payoff Matrix** (or Payoff Table):

$$
\text{Payoff Matrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a_{ij} \end{bmatrix}_{m \times n}
$$

Where:
- $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ indexes the **Alternatives** (rows — actions the DM controls).
- $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ indexes the **States of Nature** (columns — events the DM does not control).
- $a_{ij}$ is the **payoff** (profit, cost, utility, yield, time) associated with alternative $i$ under state $j$.

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix is called a **Payoff Matrix** when entries are profits/revenues/gains, and a **Regret Matrix** (or Opportunity Loss Table) when entries represent losses for not having chosen the best alternative for a given state.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mona Lisa Shopkeeper"

Imagine you are a **Kerala-based handicraft shopkeeper** deciding how many raincoats to stock for the upcoming monsoon (June–August) without access to a reliable weather forecast:

- **Alternatives:** Order 100, 200, 500, or 1000 raincoats.
- **States of Nature:** Monsoon will be *Light*, *Normal*, or *Severe*.
- **Payoff:** Profit earned (or loss incurred).

You have **no IMD data, no prior year's exact rainfall record, and no climate model** to assign a probability to each weather outcome. You are squarely operating **under uncertainty**. You must still choose an order quantity. The **Decision Criteria** covered in this module are the systematic, mathematical rules that guide such choices in the absence of probability data.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In real KTU-style problems, "payoffs" are commonly given in **lakhs of ₹**, **thousand units**, **percentage yield**, or **hours of downtime**. Always read the units before computing.

### 1.4 Classification of Decision Environments

| Environment | Outcome Known? | Probabilities Known? | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Certainty | Yes | Trivial (100%) | Linear Programming, Break-Even |
| Risk | Yes | **Yes** | Expected Value (EMV), EVPI, Decision Trees |
| **Uncertainty** | Yes | **No** | Maximin, Maximax, Hurwicz, Laplace, Savage |
| Conflict | Yes (opponent) | Assumed rational | Game Theory (Minimax) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Classical Decision Criteria

The KTU 2024 Industrial Engineering syllabus explicitly tests the following five criteria. Each represents a different **psychological / strategic posture** of the decision-maker.

#### (a) Maximin Criterion (Pessimistic / Wald Criterion)
- **Philosophy:** Assume the worst possible state of nature will occur. Choose the alternative whose *worst-case* payoff is the highest.
- **Application:** Risk-averse managers (e.g., public sector, safety-critical industries).
- **Logic:**
  1. For each alternative (row), find the minimum payoff: $\min_j \{a_{ij}\}$.
  2. Among these row-minima, choose the alternative with the **maximum** value.

$$
i^* = \arg\max_{i} \left[ \min_{j} (a_{ij}) \right]
$$

#### (b) Maximax Criterion (Optimistic / Best-Case Criterion)
- **Philosophy:** Hope for the best possible state. Choose the alternative whose *best-case* payoff is the highest.
- **Application:** Aggressive entrepreneurs, R&D investments.
- **Logic:**
  1. For each alternative, find the maximum payoff: $\max_j \{a_{ij}\}$.
  2. Among these row-maxima, choose the alternative with the **maximum** value.

$$
i^* = \arg\max_{i} \left[ \max_{j} (a_{ij}) \right]
$$

#### (c) Laplace Criterion (Principle of Insufficient Reason)
- **Philosophy:** Since the probabilities are unknown, treat **all states of nature as equally likely**. The DM then optimizes the expected payoff.
- **Application:** When there is no empirical reason to favor one state over another.
- **Logic:**
  1. Compute the **average payoff** of each alternative across all states.
  2. Choose the alternative with the highest average.

$$
i^* = \arg\max_{i} \left[ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} \right]
$$

#### (d) Hurwicz Criterion (Weighted Average / Coefficient of Optimism)
- **Philosophy:** A compromise between Maxmin and Maximax. The DM specifies a **Coefficient of Optimism** $\alpha \in [0, 1]$, which measures their degree of optimism.
- **Application:** Most realistic, allows the manager's attitude to be quantified.
- **Logic:**
  - $\alpha = 0$ → pure pessimist (reduces to Maximin).
  - $\alpha = 1$ → pure optimist (reduces to Maximax).
  - $0 < \alpha < 1$ → real-world manager.

$$
H(i) = \alpha \cdot \left[ \max_{j} a_{ij} \right] + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \left[ \min_{j} a_{ij} \right]
$$

$$
i^* = \arg\max_{i} \, H(i)
$$

#### (e) Minimax Regret Criterion (Savage Criterion)
- **Philosophy:** Minimize the *opportunity loss* (regret) incurred by not having chosen the *best* alternative for the state that actually occurred.
- **Application:** When the cost of a wrong decision is what matters most (avoids "kicking yourself" decisions).
- **Logic (Two-Stage Construction):**
  1. For each state $j$, find the best payoff: $b_j = \max_i \{a_{ij}\}$.
  2. Construct the **Regret Matrix** $r_{ij} = b_j - a_{ij}$.
  3. For each alternative, find its **maximum regret**: $\max_j \{r_{ij}\}$.
  4. Choose the alternative with the **minimum** maximum regret.

$$
i^* = \arg\min_{i} \left[ \max_{j} (r_{ij}) \right]
$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Criterion | Per-Alternative Score Function | Selection Rule | Attitude of DM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximin (Wald) | $W(i) = \min_{j} a_{ij}$ | $\max_i W(i)$ | Pessimist |
| Maximax | $O(i) = \max_{j} a_{ij}$ | $\max_i O(i)$ | Optimist |
| Laplace | $L(i) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j} a_{ij}$ | $\max_i L(i)$ | Neutral / Equal belief |
| Hurwicz | $H(i) = \alpha \max_{j} a_{ij} + (1-\alpha)\min_{j} a_{ij}$ | $\max_i H(i)$ | Tunable ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$) |
| Minimax Regret (Savage) | $R(i) = \max_{j}(b_j - a_{ij})$ | $\min_i R(i)$ | Loss-averse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions:**
> - $\alpha$ is a **dimensionless coefficient** in the closed interval $[0, 1]$.
> - All payoffs $a_{ij}$ **must be in identical units** (do not mix ₹ with hours).
> - The matrix can be **m × n** in size, with $m$ alternatives and $n$ states. KTU 2024 problems typically use $m \in \{3, 4\}$ and $n \in \{2, 3, 4\}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How Decision Under Uncertainty is Used |
|---|---|
| Capital Investment | Choosing plant capacity when demand is unpredictable |
| R&D Project Selection | Picking technology platform when market response is unknown |
| Inventory / Sourcing | Single-period new-product stocking decisions |
| Energy Sector | Capacity expansion under uncertain regulatory futures |
| Construction | Bidding strategy for tenders with unknown competitor behavior |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Example)

### 3.1 The Master Payoff Matrix (Worked Throughout)

A KTU-style problem gives the following payoff matrix (profits in **₹ lakhs**) for a firm choosing among three factory sizes with two possible demand states:

| Alternative \ State | $S_1$ : Low Demand | $S_2$ : High Demand |
|---|---:|---:|
| $A_1$ : Small Factory | **50** | **70** |
| $A_2$ : Medium Factory | **30** | **100** |
| $A_3$ : Large Factory | **−20** | **150** |

We will apply all five criteria step-by-step. No probability information is provided → **Decision Under Uncertainty**.

---

### 3.2 Applying Maximin (Wald Criterion)

**Step 1:** Compute the row-wise minimum of each alternative.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_j a_{1j} &= \min(50,\ 70) = 50 \\
\min_j a_{2j} &= \min(30,\ 100) = 30 \\
\min_j a_{3j} &= \min(-20,\ 150) = -20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Select the alternative with the highest of these row-minima.

$$
\max(50,\ 30,\ -20) = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i^* = A_1
$$

> **Decision: Build the Small Factory (₹ 50 lakhs guaranteed minimum).**

---

### 3.3 Applying Maximax Criterion

**Step 1:** Compute the row-wise maximum of each alternative.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_j a_{1j} &= \max(50,\ 70) = 70 \\
\max_j a_{2j} &= \max(30,\ 100) = 100 \\
\max_j a_{3j} &= \max(-20,\ 150) = 150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Select the alternative with the highest of these row-maxima.

$$
\max(70,\ 100,\ 150) = 150 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i^* = A_3
$$

> **Decision: Build the Large Factory (₹ 150 lakhs potential upside).**

---

### 3.4 Applying Laplace Criterion

**Step 1:** Compute the unweighted average of payoffs across the 2 states for each alternative.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(A_1) &= \frac{50 + 70}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \\
L(A_2) &= \frac{30 + 100}{2} = \frac{130}{2} = 65 \\
L(A_3) &= \frac{-20 + 150}{2} = \frac{130}{2} = 65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Select the alternative with the highest average.

$$
\max(60,\ 65,\ 65) = 65
$$

> **Decision: Tie between $A_2$ (Medium) and $A_3$ (Large).** In case of a tie, KTU examiners typically accept either with a note; the tie is broken only if an additional criterion is applied (e.g., variance, risk).

---

### 3.5 Applying Hurwicz Criterion (with $\alpha = 0.6$)

**Given:** Coefficient of Optimism $\alpha = 0.6$ (moderately optimistic DM).

**Step 1:** Recall row-wise maximum and minimum from previous steps.

| Alternative | Max | Min |
|---|---:|---:|
| $A_1$ | 70 | 50 |
| $A_2$ | 100 | 30 |
| $A_3$ | 150 | −20 |

**Step 2:** Apply the Hurwicz composite score $H(i) = \alpha(\text{max}) + (1-\alpha)(\text{min})$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(A_1) &= (0.6)(70) + (0.4)(50) = 42 + 20 = 62 \\
H(A_2) &= (0.6)(100) + (0.4)(30) = 60 + 12 = 72 \\
H(A_3) &= (0.6)(150) + (0.4)(-20) = 90 - 8 = 82
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Select the alternative with the highest Hurwicz score.

$$
\max(62,\ 72,\ 82) = 82 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i^* = A_3
$$

> **Decision: Build the Large Factory.**
>
> **Verification of boundary behavior:**
> - If $\alpha = 0$: $H(A_1) = 50,\ H(A_2) = 30,\ H(A_3) = -20$ → Maximin picks $A_1$ ✓
> - If $\alpha = 1$: $H(A_1) = 70,\ H(A_2) = 100,\ H(A_3) = 150$ → Maximax picks $A_3$ ✓

---

### 3.6 Applying Minimax Regret (Savage Criterion)

**Step 1:** For each state of nature, find the **best** payoff achievable in that column.

$$
b_1 = \max_i a_{i1} = \max(50,\ 30,\ -20) = 50
$$

$$
b_2 = \max_i a_{i2} = \max(70,\ 100,\ 150) = 150
$$

**Step 2:** Construct the **Regret Matrix** $r_{ij} = b_j - a_{ij}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{11} &= 50 - 50 = 0, \quad r_{12} = 150 - 70 = 80 \\
r_{21} &= 50 - 30 = 20, \quad r_{22} = 150 - 100 = 50 \\
r_{31} &= 50 - (-20) = 70, \quad r_{32} = 150 - 150 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The full Regret Matrix is:

| Alternative \ State | $S_1$ | $S_2$ |
|---|---:|---:|
| $A_1$ | 0 | 80 |
| $A_2$ | 20 | 50 |
| $A_3$ | 70 | 0 |

**Step 3:** Find the **maximum regret** in each row.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(A_1) &= \max(0,\ 80) = 80 \\
R(A_2) &= \max(20,\ 50) = 50 \\
R(A_3) &= \max(70,\ 0) = 70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Select the alternative with the **minimum** maximum regret.

$$
\min(80,\ 50,\ 70) = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i^* = A_2
$$

> **Decision: Build the Medium Factory.** This minimizes the worst-case "kickself" of having made the wrong choice.

---

### 3.7 Consolidated Decision Table (Final Answer Summary)

| Criterion | Selected Alternative | Payoff / Score | DM's Posture |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Maximin | $A_1$ (Small) | 50 | Pessimist |
| Maximax | $A_3$ (Large) | 150 | Optimist |
| Laplace | $A_2$ or $A_3$ (tie) | 65 | Neutral |
| Hurwicz ($\alpha=0.6$) | $A_3$ (Large) | 82 | Moderately optimistic |
| Minimax Regret | $A_2$ (Medium) | Max regret = 50 | Loss-averse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A complete answer must list **all five criteria**, compute each in a clearly labeled section, and conclude with a one-sentence **managerial interpretation** of the result.

---

### 3.8 Edge Case — Cost Payoff Matrix

If the original matrix is given as **costs** (e.g., operating expenses, downtime hours) instead of profits, **invert the optimization direction**:

- Maximin becomes **Minimax** (minimize the worst cost).
- Maximax becomes **Minimin** (minimize the best cost).
- Laplace becomes **Minimize** the average cost.
- Hurwicz score becomes $H(i) = \alpha(\min_j a_{ij}) + (1-\alpha)(\max_j a_{ij})$ → **minimize** $H(i)$.
- For Regret: change is automatic — the regret $r_{ij} = a_{ij} - \min_i a_{ij}$ (worst minus chosen).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Decision Under Uncertainty Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Input: Payoff Matrix a_ij] --> B{Define Dimensions}
    B --> C1[Row: Alternatives A_i, i=1..m]
    B --> C2[Column: States S_j, j=1..n]
    C1 --> D[Criterion Selector]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E1[Maximin: row-min then global-max]
    D --> E2[Maximax: row-max then global-max]
    D --> E3[Laplace: row-mean then global-max]
    D --> E4[Hurwicz: alpha*row-max + 1-alpha*row-min]
    D --> E5[Savage: Build Regret, then row-max-min]
    E1 --> F[Selected Alternative i*]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Decision Report with DM Posture]
```

### 4.2 Decision-Tree Schematic (Stages of Computation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Read Payoff Matrix]) --> alt[Decision Node: Choose Alternative A_i]
    alt --> s1[State S1: Low Demand]
    alt --> s2[State S2: High Demand]
    s1 --> p1[Payoff a_i1]
    s2 --> p2[Payoff a_i2]
    p1 --> eval[Apply Decision Criterion]
    p2 --> eval
    eval --> opt[Optimal Alternative Selected]
    opt --> stop([End])
```

### 4.3 Concept-Map of the Five Criteria

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Decision Under Uncertainty] --> B[Pessimistic Strategy]
    A --> C[Optimistic Strategy]
    A --> D[Neutral Strategy]
    A --> E[Compromise Strategy]
    A --> F[Loss-Averse Strategy]
    B --> B1[Maximin / Wald]
    C --> C1[Maximax]
    D --> D1[Laplace / Equal Likelihood]
    E --> E1[Hurwicz / Alpha Index]
    F --> F1[Minimax Regret / Savage]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Regret Matrix Construction (Savage)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Original Payoff Matrix a_ij] --> R1[For each state S_j: find best payoff b_j = max a_ij]
    R1 --> R2[Compute opportunity loss r_ij = b_j - a_ij]
    R2 --> R3[Regret Matrix r_ij]
    R3 --> R4[Find row-wise max regret R_i = max r_ij]
    R4 --> R5[Choose alternative with min R_i]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid blocks are **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representations. They describe the **computational flow** and decision logic without attempting to draw geometric payoff surfaces (which are out of scope for static diagrams).
