## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between decision-making under uncertainty and decision-making under risk. State one real-world industrial engineering example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Uncertainty | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge of probabilities | **Not known** | **Known** |
| Decision tool | Maximin, Maximax, Laplace, Hurwicz, Savage | EMV, Decision Trees, EVPI |
| Example | Choosing plant capacity without market data | Buying a machine whose failure rate is known to be 5% |

> **Industrial Engineering Example (Uncertainty):** Deciding the size of a new warehouse without demand forecast data. **(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for risk distinction + 1 mark for examples)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the Hurwicz criterion of decision-making. What does the coefficient of optimism $\alpha$ represent? What happens when $\alpha = 0$ and $\alpha = 1$?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The Hurwicz criterion is a **compromise** between the pessimistic Maxmin and the optimistic Maximax criteria. **(1 mark)**
- $\alpha$ is the **coefficient of optimism**; a dimensionless number in $[0, 1]$ quantifying the decision-maker's belief that the most favorable state will occur. **(1 mark)**
- $\alpha = 0$ → pure pessimist → reduces to **Maximin**; $\alpha = 1$ → pure optimist → reduces to **Maximax**. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU ESE – July 2024 Model]

A KTU engineering firm must choose between three manufacturing process options ($P_1$, $P_2$, $P_3$). Demand for the product can be **Low ($S_1$)**, **Medium ($S_2$)**, or **High ($S_3$)**. The estimated profit (in ₹ lakhs) is:

| Process | $S_1$ : Low | $S_2$ : Medium | $S_3$ : High |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| $P_1$ | 40 | 60 | 75 |
| $P_2$ | 20 | 70 | 110 |
| $P_3$ | −30 | 50 | 160 |

**(a)** Determine the optimal process using **(i) Maxmin**, **(ii) Maxmax**, and **(iii) Laplace** criteria. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using a coefficient of optimism $\alpha = 0.7$, apply the **Hurwicz criterion**, and then construct the **Regret Matrix** to apply the **Minimax Regret (Savage) criterion**. State the final recommendation. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Maxmin (Pessimistic) — 2 Marks**

Row-wise minima:
- $\min(P_1) = \min(40, 60, 75) = 40$
- $\min(P_2) = \min(20, 70, 110) = 20$
- $\min(P_3) = \min(-30, 50, 160) = -30$

Selecting the maximum: $\max(40, 20, -30) = 40$ → **Choose $P_1$**. (1 mark)

**(ii) Maxmax (Optimistic) — 2 Marks**

Row-wise maxima:
- $\max(P_1) = \max(40, 60, 75) = 75$
- $\max(P_2) = \max(20, 70, 110) = 110$
- $\max(P_3) = \max(-30, 50, 160) = 160$

Selecting the maximum: $\max(75, 110, 160) = 160$ → **Choose $P_3$**. (1 mark)

**(iii) Laplace (Equal Probability) — 3 Marks**

Row-wise averages (since $n = 3$, divide by 3):

$$
L(P_1) = \frac{40 + 60 + 75}{3} = \frac{175}{3} \approx 58.33
$$

$$
L(P_2) = \frac{20 + 70 + 110}{3} = \frac{200}{3} \approx 66.67
$$

$$
L(P_3) = \frac{-30 + 50 + 160}{3} = \frac{180}{3} = 60.00
$$

Selecting the maximum: $\max(58.33, 66.67, 60.00) = 66.67$ → **Choose $P_2$**. (1 mark)

---

##### **Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Hurwicz Criterion with $\alpha = 0.7$ — 3 Marks**

Recall maxima and minima: $P_1$: (75, 40); $P_2$: (110, 20); $P_3$: (160, −30).

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(P_1) &= 0.7(75) + 0.3(40) = 52.5 + 12 = 64.5 \\
H(P_2) &= 0.7(110) + 0.3(20) = 77 + 6 = 83 \\
H(P_3) &= 0.7(160) + 0.3(-30) = 112 - 9 = 103
\end{aligned}
$$

$\max(64.5, 83, 103) = 103$ → **Hurwicz recommends $P_3$**. (1 mark)

**Minimax Regret (Savage) Criterion — 4 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Column maxima $b_j$:

$$
b_1 = \max(40, 20, -30) = 40, \quad b_2 = \max(60, 70, 50) = 70, \quad b_3 = \max(75, 110, 160) = 160
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Regret matrix $r_{ij} = b_j - a_{ij}$:

$$
r_{11}=0,\ r_{12}=10,\ r_{13}=85
$$
$$
r_{21}=20,\ r_{22}=0,\ r_{23}=50
$$
$$
r_{31}=70,\ r_{32}=20,\ r_{33}=0
$$

| Process | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | Max Regret |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $P_1$ | 0 | 10 | 85 | **85** |
| $P_2$ | 20 | 0 | 50 | **50** |
| $P_3$ | 70 | 20 | 0 | **70** |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Minimize the max regret: $\min(85, 50, 70) = 50$ → **Savage recommends $P_2$**.

**Final Recommendation (1 mark):**
- $P_1$: chosen by Maxmin.
- $P_3$: chosen by Maxmax and Hurwicz.
- $P_2$: chosen by Laplace and Minimax Regret.

A **conservative, loss-averse** industrial manager would adopt **$P_2$** as it is supported by 2 of 5 criteria and minimizes opportunity loss. An **aggressive** manager with $\alpha = 0.7$ would adopt **$P_3$**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice] [KTU ESE – Dec 2023 Model]

A startup must choose between three product launch strategies: **Conservative ($C$)**, **Moderate ($M$)**, and **Aggressive ($A$)**. Market response can be **Weak ($W$)**, **Average ($R$)**, or **Strong ($S$)**. The NPV (Net Present Value, in ₹ crores) is given below:

| Strategy | Weak $W$ | Average $R$ | Strong $S$ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Conservative $C$ | 5 | 8 | 10 |
| Moderate $M$ | 2 | 9 | 14 |
| Aggressive $A$ | −4 | 7 | 20 |

**(a)** Apply the **Maxmin, Maxmax, and Laplace** criteria. Identify the optimal alternative in each case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The founder is moderately optimistic and uses $\alpha = 0.6$. Apply the **Hurwicz criterion**. Also, apply the **Minimax Regret** criterion using the full regret-matrix procedure. State which strategy is best supported overall. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Maxmin — 2 Marks**

$$
\min(C) = 5,\quad \min(M) = 2,\quad \min(A) = -4
$$

$\max(5, 2, -4) = 5$ → **Choose $C$ (Conservative).** (1 mark)

**(ii) Maxmax — 2 Marks**

$$
\max(C) = 10,\quad \max(M) = 14,\quad \max(A) = 20
$$

$\max(10, 14, 20) = 20$ → **Choose $A$ (Aggressive).** (1 mark)

**(iii) Laplace — 3 Marks**

$$
L(C) = \frac{5 + 8 + 10}{3} = \frac{23}{3} \approx 7.67
$$

$$
L(M) = \frac{2 + 9 + 14}{3} = \frac{25}{3} \approx 8.33
$$

$$
L(A) = \frac{-4 + 7 + 20}{3} = \frac{23}{3} \approx 7.67
$$

$\max(7.67, 8.33, 7.67) = 8.33$ → **Choose $M$ (Moderate).** (1 mark)

---

##### **Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Hurwicz with $\alpha = 0.6$ — 3 Marks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(C) &= 0.6(10) + 0.4(5) = 6 + 2 = 8 \\
H(M) &= 0.6(14) + 0.4(2) = 8.4 + 0.8 = 9.2 \\
H(A) &= 0.6(20) + 0.4(-4) = 12 - 1.6 = 10.4
\end{aligned}
$$

$\max(8, 9.2, 10.4) = 10.4$ → **Hurwicz recommends $A$.** (1 mark)

**Minimax Regret — 4 Marks**

Column maxima: $b_W = 5$, $b_R = 9$, $b_S = 20$.

Regret matrix:

$$
r_{ij} = b_j - a_{ij}
$$

| Strategy | $W$ | $R$ | $S$ | Max Regret |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $C$ | 0 | 1 | 10 | **10** |
| $M$ | 3 | 0 | 6 | **6** |
| $A$ | 9 | 2 | 0 | **9** |

$\min(10, 6, 9) = 6$ → **Savage recommends $M$.** (1 mark)

**Final Recommendation (1 mark):**

- $C$ → Maxmin.
- $A$ → Maxmax, Hurwicz.
- $M$ → Laplace, Minimax Regret.

**Overall best-supported strategy: $M$ (Moderate).** It is optimal in 2 of 5 criteria and balances risk with upside.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Inverted direction for costs:** Students often apply Maxmin to a *cost* matrix, which is mathematically wrong. For costs, the criterion is **Minimax**.
> 2. **Forgetting to use the same $\alpha$ formula:** Hurwicz is $\alpha(\text{max}) + (1-\alpha)(\text{min})$. Many students invert it.
> 3. **Savage regret sign error:** Regret = (column best) − (chosen payoff). For a cost matrix, swap the sign: regret = (chosen) − (column minimum).
> 4. **Skipping the Laplace n:** Always divide by the **number of states**, not the number of alternatives.
> 5. **Tie-breaking:** In Laplace ties, mention both alternatives explicitly; do not silently pick one.
> 6. **Units mismatch:** Mixing lakhs and crores in the same matrix leads to silent valuation errors. KTU examiners specifically check this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A decision is "under uncertainty" when states of nature exist but their probabilities are **unknown**.
- **Five Criteria** (all must be memorized for KTU 2024):
  1. **Maxmin (Wald)** → Pessimist → max of row-min.
  2. **Maxmax** → Optimist → max of row-max.
  3. **Laplace** → Equal belief → max of row-average.
  4. **Hurwicz** → Tunable → max of $\alpha(\text{max}) + (1-\alpha)(\text{min})$.
  5. **Minimax Regret (Savage)** → Loss-averse → min of max-regret after building regret matrix.
- **Decision rule formula at a glance:**
  $$
  i^* = \begin{cases}
  \arg\max_i \min_j a_{ij} & \text{(Maxmin)} \\
  \arg\max_i \max_j a_{ij} & \text{(Maxmax)} \\
  \arg\max_i \frac{1}{n}\sum_j a_{ij} & \text{(Laplace)} \\
  \arg\max_i \left[ \alpha \max_j a_{ij} + (1-\alpha)\min_j a_{ij} \right] & \text{(Hurwicz)} \\
  \arg\min_i \max_j (b_j - a_{ij}) & \text{(Savage)}
  \end{cases}
  $$
- **Coefficient of Optimism $\alpha$** lies in $[0, 1]$. Boundary: $\alpha = 0$ → Maxmin, $\alpha = 1$ → Maxmax.
- **Payoff Matrix must be uniform units** before any calculation.
- **For cost matrices, the direction of optimization is reversed** for all five criteria.
- **Always conclude with a one-line managerial interpretation** of the recommended alternative.
- **KTU 2024 weightage** (typical): 14-mark questions = full 5-criterion problem; 3-mark questions = definition/distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Tree
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT526 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decision Tree

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Decision Tree** is a quantitative, graphical decision-making tool used in Industrial Engineering to represent a sequence of interrelated decisions, chance events, and their associated payoffs in a tree-like branching structure. It employs the principle of **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** to identify the optimal course of action under conditions of **risk**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Industrial Engineering syllabus (Module 4: Quantitative Methods for Decision Making)**, the decision tree is classified as a *Probabilistic Decision-Making Model*, used when the decision-maker has historical data or subjective estimates of probabilities for different states of nature.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crossroads and the Weather Forecast"

Imagine you are planning a major outdoor industrial expo. You stand at a **crossroads** (the decision point). You have two routes:
- **Route A:** Book an open-air venue (cheaper, but a rainstorm could ruin it).
- **Route B:** Book a covered stadium (expensive, but weatherproof).

Before choosing, you check a **weather forecast** (a chance event with probabilities). You draw lines from your current position to each option, then from each option to the possible weather outcomes, writing the cost/revenue (payoff) at the end of each path.

> **At the tip of every branch, you calculate the average profit (Expected Value) and pick the path with the highest expected return.** This visual "branching from present to future outcomes" is precisely what a Decision Tree is — a map of *if-this-then-that* outcomes weighted by their likelihood.

### 1.2 Core Components of a Decision Tree

A decision tree is built using four standard symbols:

1. **Decision Node** (drawn as a **square** □): A point where the decision-maker exercises control and chooses among alternatives.
2. **Chance Node** (drawn as a **circle** ○): A point where the outcome is determined by probability (state of nature), not by the decision-maker.
3. **Branch Lines**: Solid lines indicating either a decision alternative (from a square) or a probabilistic outcome (from a circle).
4. **End/Terminal Node** (drawn as a **triangle** △): The final leaf showing the **payoff** (profit, cost, or utility) for that complete path.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental metric required for evaluating decision tree branches is the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)**, measured in monetary units (₹, $, or any standard currency).

### 1.3 Classification of Decision Environments

| Environment Type | Description | Tool Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Certainty** | Outcomes of every alternative are **known** | Simple comparison / Linear Programming |
| **Risk** | Probabilities of outcomes are **known/estimable** | **Decision Tree (EMV)** |
| **Uncertainty** | Probabilities are **completely unknown** | Maximin, Maximax, Hurwicz, Laplace, Minimax Regret |

> The **Decision Tree is exclusively used under the RISK environment**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind Decision Tree Analysis

The decision tree operationalizes a **rational, sequential, probabilistic thought process**. The logic proceeds in distinct phases:

- **Step 1: Problem Definition.** The industrial engineering problem (e.g., "Make or Buy?", "Expand or Wait?", "Launch Product A or B?") is clearly articulated.
- **Step 2: Identification of Decision Points.** A chronological sequence of decisions is established. For example, a firm may first decide *whether to test-market*, and then subsequently *whether to launch* — giving a **multi-stage decision tree**.
- **Step 3: Identification of Alternatives & States of Nature.** At each decision node, all feasible alternatives are listed. At each chance node, all possible states of nature are enumerated (e.g., High Demand, Medium Demand, Low Demand).
- **Step 4: Probability Assignment.** Subjective or historical probabilities $P_i$ are assigned such that $\sum P_i = 1$ at every chance node.
- **Step 5: Payoff Computation.** The terminal payoffs are calculated for every complete path. These may be *profits* (revenue − cost) or *costs*.
- **Step 6: Roll-Back (Folding-Back) Analysis.** The tree is evaluated from **right (terminal) to left (root)**. At each chance node, the EMV is calculated. At each decision node, the alternative with the **best EMV** (max for profit, min for cost) is selected, and the others are "pruned" (shown with double tick marks ‖).
- **Step 7: Sensitivity Analysis.** The robustness of the decision is tested by varying probabilities and payoffs to find the breakeven thresholds.

> **The "Why":** The roll-back technique is powerful because it respects the *chronological nature of decisions* — later decisions can be contingent on earlier outcomes (e.g., "If the test-market succeeds, THEN launch").

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Condition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot X_i$ | $\sum P_i = 1$; $X_i$ = payoff of outcome $i$ | ₹ / $ |
| **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)** | $EOL = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot L_i$ | $L_i$ = regret of outcome $i$ | ₹ / $ |
| **Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)** | $EVwPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot (\text{Best payoff in state } i)$ | Always $\geq$ EMV of best act | ₹ / $ |
| **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)** | $EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*}$ | $EMV^{*}$ = best EMV from acts | ₹ / $ |
| **Expected Profit (Multi-stage)** | $EMV_{node} = P_{high} \cdot X_{high} + P_{low} \cdot X_{low}$ | Applies to chance nodes only | ₹ / $ |
| **Roll-Back Selection Rule (Profit)** | Choose $\max(EMV_1, EMV_2, \ldots, EMV_n)$ | At decision node (square) | — |
| **Roll-Back Selection Rule (Cost)** | Choose $\min(EMV_1, EMV_2, \ldots, EMV_n)$ | At decision node (square) | — |
| **Probability Constraint** | $0 \leq P_i \leq 1$ | For all chance events | Dimensionless |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Decision trees are used in *capital investment*, *R&D portfolio selection*, *reliability engineering*, *maintenance strategy selection (PM vs. CM)*, *inventory policy*, *product launch strategy*, and *facility location under demand uncertainty*. They form the conceptual backbone of modern **machine learning classification algorithms** (e.g., ID3, CART, Random Forest) used in Industry 4.0 smart manufacturing.

### 2.3 Advantages and Limitations

> [!TIP]
> **Advantages:** (i) Visual clarity, (ii) Handles multi-stage sequential decisions, (iii) Incorporates both quantitative (EMV) and qualitative (subjective probability) inputs, (iv) Allows for risk quantification via EVPI.
>
> **Limitations:** (i) Outcomes and states must be **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive**, (ii) Payoffs are typically expressed in a **single common unit** (money), (iii) Exponential growth of branches with more stages (curse of dimensionality).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Expected Monetary Value (EMV) Framework

Let us derive EMV from first principles using the linearity of expectation.

Let a chance node have $n$ mutually exclusive outcomes $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$ with corresponding probabilities $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ and monetary payoffs $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$.

By the **Kolmogorov Axioms of Probability**, the outcomes form a partition of the sample space:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = 1$$

The expected value of a discrete random variable $X$ is defined as the probability-weighted average of all possible values:

$$E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \cdot P(X = X_i)$$

Substituting our notation:

$$EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \cdot P_i$$

This is the foundation. Now let us extend it to a **multi-stage decision tree** to demonstrate the **roll-back** (folding-back) procedure.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Derivation: Multi-Stage Decision Tree

**Problem Setup:** A pharmaceutical company must decide whether to:
- **A1:** Conduct a clinical trial first (cost ₹10,00,000), and based on results either *Launch* or *Abandon*.
- **A2:** Launch directly without a trial.
- **A3:** Do nothing.

**Outcomes & Data:**

The trial has two possible results — *Success* (probability 0.7) or *Failure* (probability 0.3).

If trial succeeds, the company may *Launch* (yielding ₹80,00,000 profit) or *Abandon* (₹0 profit). If trial fails, *Launch* causes a loss of ₹40,00,000, while *Abandon* yields ₹0.

If launching directly (A2), the probability of market success is 0.5 (yielding ₹80,00,000), and failure 0.5 (loss of ₹40,00,000).

**Step 1: Construct the tree conceptually with decision and chance nodes.**

**Step 2: Compute terminal payoffs (Net Payoff = Final outcome − cost of trial if applicable).**

For A1 (Trial then Launch, Success path):
$$\text{Net Payoff} = 80,00,000 - 10,00,000 = 70,00,000$$

For A1 (Trial then Abandon, Success path):
$$\text{Net Payoff} = 0 - 10,00,000 = -10,00,000$$

For A1 (Trial then Launch, Failure path):
$$\text{Net Payoff} = -40,00,000 - 10,00,000 = -50,00,000$$

For A1 (Trial then Abandon, Failure path):
$$\text{Net Payoff} = 0 - 10,00,000 = -10,00,000$$

For A2 (Direct Launch, Success):
$$\text{Net Payoff} = 80,00,000$$

For A2 (Direct Launch, Failure):
$$\text{Net Payoff} = -40,00,000$$

**Step 3: Roll-back at the second-stage decision node (after trial).**

**Sub-step 3a: At the chance node for "Success of Trial"** — but first we evaluate the decision node *after* observing the trial result.

If trial is **Successful** (Probability 0.7):
- Launch yields ₹70,00,000
- Abandon yields −₹10,00,000
- Decision: Choose **Launch** (max payoff).

If trial is **Failure** (Probability 0.3):
- Launch yields −₹50,00,000
- Abandon yields −₹10,00,000
- Decision: Choose **Abandon** (max payoff, since −10,00,000 > −50,00,000).

**Step 4: Compute EMV at the chance node for the trial outcome.**

$$EMV_{A1} = (0.7 \times 70,00,000) + (0.3 \times -10,00,000)$$

$$EMV_{A1} = 49,00,000 + (-3,00,000)$$

$$EMV_{A1} = 46,00,000$$

**Step 5: Compute EMV for A2 (Direct Launch).**

$$EMV_{A2} = (0.5 \times 80,00,000) + (0.5 \times -40,00,000)$$

$$EMV_{A2} = 40,00,000 + (-20,00,000)$$

$$EMV_{A2} = 20,00,000$$

**Step 6: EMV for A3 (Do Nothing) = ₹0.**

**Step 7: Final decision at the root decision node.**

Comparing $EMV_{A1} = 46,00,000$, $EMV_{A2} = 20,00,000$, and $EMV_{A3} = 0$:

$$\max(46,00,000, \ 20,00,000, \ 0) = 46,00,000$$

**Decision: Choose A1 (Conduct the Clinical Trial first).**

### 3.3 Derivative Metric: Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

The EVPI quantifies the maximum amount a rational decision-maker should pay to obtain *perfect* information about an uncertain state.

**Step 1: Calculate Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI).**

With perfect information, we would know the state in advance and pick the best payoff for that state.

For the A1 branch (under perfect info, we would *know* the trial outcome and act accordingly):
- If Success: Best is Launch → ₹70,00,000
- If Failure: Best is Abandon → −₹10,00,000

$$EVwPI_{A1} = (0.7 \times 70,00,000) + (0.3 \times -10,00,000) = 46,00,000$$

For A2 (direct launch, perfect info):
$$EVwPI_{A2} = (0.5 \times 80,00,000) + (0.5 \times 0) = 40,00,000$$

(Note: Under perfect info, we would not launch if failure is certain — we abandon and get ₹0.)

**Step 2: Overall EVwPI = max of all EVwPI values.**

$$EVwPI = \max(46,00,000, \ 40,00,000) = 46,00,000$$

**Step 3: Compute EVPI.**

$$EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*} = 46,00,000 - 46,00,000 = 0$$

> **Interpretation:** In this particular problem, since the trial provides nearly perfect predictive information (failure reliably leads to abandonment), additional perfect information adds **zero marginal value**. The decision is already optimal under existing information.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Decision Tree Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following block-level schematic illustrates the **logical architecture** of a typical two-stage decision tree under risk, mapping the flow from the initial decision point through chance events to terminal payoffs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["D1: ROOT DECISION NODE"]) -->|"ACT A1: TRIAL"| B(["C1: TRIAL RESULT"])
    A -->|"ACT A2: DIRECT LAUNCH"| C(["C2: MARKET RESPONSE"])
    A -->|"ACT A3: DO NOTHING"| X["PAYOFF = 0"]

    B -->|"P = 0.7 SUCCESS"| D(["D2: POST TRIAL DECISION"])
    B -->|"P = 0.3 FAILURE"| E(["D3: POST TRIAL DECISION"])

    D -->|"CHOOSE LAUNCH"| F["PAYOFF = 70 LAKHS"]
    D -->|"CHOOSE ABANDON"| G["PAYOFF = -10 LAKHS"]

    E -->|"CHOOSE LAUNCH"| H["PAYOFF = -50 LAKHS"]
    E -->|"CHOOSE ABANDON"| I["PAYOFF = -10 LAKHS"]

    C -->|"P = 0.5 SUCCESS"| J["PAYOFF = 80 LAKHS"]
    C -->|"P = 0.5 FAILURE"| K["PAYOFF = -40 LAKHS"]

    classDef decision fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef chance fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef payoff fill:#D4FFB4,stroke:#339933,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class A,D,E decision
    class B,C chance
    class F,G,H,I,J,K,X payoff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend:**
> - **Orange/Decision nodes** (rounded squares): Where the decision-maker selects an alternative.
> - **Blue/Chance nodes** (circles): Where probabilistic outcomes occur.
> - **Green/Payoff nodes** (rectangles): Terminal monetary outcomes.
> - **Pruning:** In actual valuation, branches with suboptimal EMV at decision nodes are marked with double tick marks (‖) to indicate they are rejected.

### 4.2 Roll-Back Sequence Diagram

The following diagram depicts the **chronological evaluation flow** (right-to-left roll-back) used to solve a decision tree, which is the standard procedure taught in KTU 2024 Scheme modules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1 IDENTIFY PAYOFFS"] --> S2["STEP 2 COMPUTE EMV AT CHANCE NODES"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 PRUNE SUBOPTIMAL ALTERNATIVES AT DECISION NODES"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 PROPAGATE BEST EMV UPWARD"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 FINAL DECISION AT ROOT"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 OPTIONAL SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS"]

    classDef stepBox fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 stepBox
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows indicate the *logical dependency* — Step 1 must complete before Step 2, and so on. In an exam, missing even one step leads to partial marking deductions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Decision Tree. List its main components and state the key metric used for evaluation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Decision Tree** is a graphical analytical tool that represents decisions (squares), chance events (circles), and their payoffs in a tree-like structure, used for decision-making under conditions of **risk**.

**Main Components (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Decision Nodes** (squares □) — points of choice.
2. **Chance Nodes** (circles ○) — points of probabilistic outcomes.
3. **Branches** — alternatives or outcomes.
4. **Terminal Nodes / Payoffs** (triangles △) — final monetary values.

**Key Metric (1.5 Marks):** The primary evaluation metric is the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)**, computed as $EMV = \sum P_i X_i$, where the alternative with the maximum EMV (for profit problems) is selected via **roll-back analysis**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between decision-making under certainty, risk, and uncertainty. Which environment specifically uses the decision tree technique?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Environment | Characteristics (1 Mark) | Probabilities (1 Mark) | Tool (1 Mark) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Certainty** | Outcomes of every alternative are **known and deterministic** | Not required (P = 1 for the only outcome) | Linear Programming, Break-Even Analysis |
| **Risk** | Multiple possible outcomes with **known/estimated probabilities** | Known, $\sum P_i = 1$ | **Decision Tree (EMV Analysis)** |
| **Uncertainty** | Outcomes exist but **probabilities are unknown** | Cannot be assigned | Maximin, Maximax, Hurwicz, Laplace, Minimax Regret |

The **Decision Tree technique is specifically applied under the RISK environment** (1 Mark) because it requires probability estimates to compute the EMV of each alternative.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE full question from a choice of TWO. Each question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.*

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set B]*

**A company is considering three options for a new product line:**
- **A1:** Build a large plant (cost ₹50 lakhs)
- **A2:** Build a small plant (cost ₹25 lakhs)
- **A3:** Do nothing

The demand could be **High (probability 0.6)** or **Low (probability 0.4)**. The annual profits are given below:

| Decision | High Demand | Low Demand |
|---|---|---|
| Large Plant | ₹150 lakhs | −₹30 lakhs |
| Small Plant | ₹70 lakhs | ₹40 lakhs |
| Do Nothing | ₹0 | ₹0 |

**(a)** Draw the decision tree and calculate the EMV for each alternative. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Identify the optimal decision and compute the **EVPI**. Explain its managerial significance. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Identify the decision and chance nodes.**
- Decision Node: D1 (Choice of plant size)
- Chance Node: C1 (Demand state: High or Low)

**Step 2: Compute the EMV for each alternative.**

EMV for **A1 (Large Plant)**:
$$EMV_{A1} = (0.6 \times 150) + (0.4 \times -30)$$
$$EMV_{A1} = 90 + (-12) = 78 \text{ lakhs}$$

EMV for **A2 (Small Plant)**:
$$EMV_{A2} = (0.6 \times 70) + (0.4 \times 40)$$
$$EMV_{A2} = 42 + 16 = 58 \text{ lakhs}$$

EMV for **A3 (Do Nothing)**:
$$EMV_{A3} = 0$$

**Step 3: Decision at the root node.**

$$\max(78, \ 58, \ 0) = 78 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Optimal Decision: Build the Large Plant (A1).**

> Note: The capital cost of ₹50 lakhs for the large plant is already absorbed in the profit values (or alternatively subtracted; clarify whether payoffs are *gross* or *net* — the convention should be stated by the examiner).

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Compute Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI).**

With perfect information, the decision-maker would know the demand state in advance and pick the best payoff:

- If **High Demand (P = 0.6)**: Best option is Large Plant → ₹150 lakhs
- If **Low Demand (P = 0.4)**: Best option is Small Plant → ₹40 lakhs

$$EVwPI = (0.6 \times 150) + (0.4 \times 40) = 90 + 16 = 106 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 2: Compute EVPI.**

$$EVPI = EVwPI - EMV^{*} = 106 - 78 = 28 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3: Managerial Significance.**

The **EVPI of ₹28 lakhs** represents the **maximum amount the company should be willing to pay for perfect market research/forecasting information** before committing to the plant decision. If a market research firm offers a study for less than ₹28 lakhs, it is economically rational to purchase it, since the value of the information exceeds its cost. If the cost exceeds ₹28 lakhs, the company should rely on the existing EMV-based decision (Large Plant).

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set A — Alternative Choice]*

A manufacturing firm must decide whether to:
- **B1:** Manufacture a component in-house.
- **B2:** Outsource the component from a vendor.

If manufactured in-house, the cost of setting up the line is ₹20 lakhs. Production costs yield:
- Profit of ₹60 lakhs if quality is Good (P = 0.7)
- Loss of ₹10 lakhs if quality is Poor (P = 0.3)

If outsourced, no setup cost is incurred. Outcomes are:
- Profit of ₹40 lakhs if vendor delivers Good quality (P = 0.8)
- Loss of ₹5 lakhs if vendor delivers Poor quality (P = 0.2)

**(a)** Construct the decision tree and recommend the optimal alternative using EMV analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the firm can conduct a **quality pre-test** at a cost of ₹3 lakhs, which predicts vendor quality with 90% accuracy. Should the firm invest in the pre-test? Justify using EMV comparison. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step:**

**EMV for B1 (In-house Manufacturing):**
$$EMV_{B1} = (0.7 \times 60) + (0.3 \times -10) = 42 + (-3) = 39 \text{ lakhs}$$

**EMV for B2 (Outsource):**
$$EMV_{B2} = (0.8 \times 40) + (0.2 \times -5) = 32 + (-1) = 31 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Decision at root node:**
$$\max(39, \ 31) = 39 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Optimal Decision: Manufacture in-house (B1).**

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Compute EMV of outsourcing WITH the pre-test.**

The pre-test gives a "Good" or "Poor" forecast:
- If forecast is **Good** (joint probability = 0.8 × 0.9 + 0.2 × 0.1 = 0.74): Outsource → ₹40 lakhs profit
- If forecast is **Poor** (joint probability = 0.8 × 0.1 + 0.2 × 0.9 = 0.26): Manufacture in-house → ₹39 lakhs profit

Net payoffs after deducting pre-test cost of ₹3 lakhs:
- Good forecast path: $40 - 3 = 37$ lakhs
- Poor forecast path: $39 - 3 = 36$ lakhs

$$EMV_{\text{with test}} = (0.74 \times 37) + (0.26 \times 36) = 27.38 + 9.36 = 36.74 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 2: Compare with the best EMV WITHOUT the test (B1 = 39 lakhs).**

$$EMV_{\text{no test}} = 39 \text{ lakhs} > EMV_{\text{with test}} = 36.74 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Decision: Do NOT invest in the pre-test.**

**Justification:** The pre-test costs ₹3 lakhs and provides only marginal improvement in decision accuracy. The net expected gain from testing (36.74 lakhs) is **less than** the EMV of the best no-test alternative (39 lakhs). Therefore, the firm should retain the in-house manufacturing decision without the pre-test.

> The **Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI)** can also be computed as:
> $$EVSI = 36.74 - 39 = -2.26 \text{ lakhs}$$
> The **negative EVSI** confirms the pre-test destroys value in this scenario.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion (Most Common Mistake):** Students frequently confuse *profit* with *cost* in the EMV formula. For **profit problems** → choose **max**; for **cost problems** → choose **min**. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Roll-Back:** In multi-stage trees, students often compute EMVs at *all* chance nodes but forget to apply the decision rule at intermediate decision nodes, leading to incorrect propagation.
> 3. **Probability Constraint Violation:** Always verify $\sum P_i = 1$ at every chance node. Examiners deduct 1 mark for un-normalized probabilities.
> 4. **Confusing EVPI with EVwPI:** EVPI is a *difference* (in ₹/$ units), not a probability. Students sometimes express it as a percentage, which is incorrect.
> 5. **Mermaid Diagram Mistakes in Theory Questions:** When asked to "draw the tree," always label all branches with **probabilities AND payoffs**. Unlabelled branches invite 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Ignoring Capital/Setup Costs:** If setup cost is mentioned separately, ensure it is **subtracted from terminal payoffs** before computing EMV.

---

### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Definition:** A Decision Tree is a **graphical probabilistic decision model** for problems under **risk**, using **EMV** as the evaluation metric.
- ✅ **Symbols:** Square = Decision; Circle = Chance; Triangle = Payoff.
- ✅ **EMV Formula:** $EMV = \sum P_i X_i$ where $\sum P_i = 1$.
- ✅ **Selection Rule:** **Maximize** EMV for profit; **Minimize** EMV for cost.
- ✅ **Roll-Back Direction:** Always **right-to-left** (from leaves to root).
- ✅ **EVPI** = $EVwPI - EMV^{*}$; represents the **upper bound on the price of perfect information**.
- ✅ **EVSI** = $EMV_{with\_sample} - EMV_{without\_sample}$; negative EVSI means don't buy the sample information.
- ✅ **Pruning:** Suboptimal branches are marked with ‖ (double tick) to indicate rejection.
- ✅ **Sensitivity Analysis:** Always perform to check robustness of the optimal decision against probability/payoff variations.
- ✅ **Multi-Stage Trees:** Later decisions are *contingent* on earlier chance outcomes — preserve this chronological logic.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Outcomes must be **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE)**.
- ✅ **Unit of EMV:** Monetary (₹ / $) — never expressed as a ratio or percentage.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Focus:** Expect at least one 14-mark question combining decision tree with EVPI/EVSI in every ESE paper.
